Jam 2:14 (M38-A-VCG-04) applies moral evaluation to faith itself: "can that faith save him?" — assessing whether faith without works meets the standard of genuine saving faith. 2 Th 2:10 (M38-A-VCG-08) evaluates those who "refused the love of truth" as morally culpable for forfeiting salvation. Heb 2:3 (M38-A-VCG-05) evaluates neglect of salvation as morally indefensible. Jude 23 (M38-A-VCG-10) calls for moral evaluation of sin-contamination in the act of snatching others from destruction. Moral evaluation is engaged in assessing the conditions, authenticity, and culpability around salvation.
  - _cites:_ Act 15:1,Jam 2:14,2Th 2:10,Heb 2:3
- **[21403 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Conscience is engaged as an activating and diagnostic faculty. Act 16:30 (M38-A-VCG-02) presents the paradigmatic instance: the jailer's "what must I do to be saved?" emerges from a conscience awakened to existential danger — acute inner witness driving the will toward rescue. 2 Cor 7:10 (M38-A-VCG-09, ANCHOR) specifies that "godly grief" (conscience pierced by recognition of sin) "produces a repentance that leads to salvation" — the conscience is the causal pathway into the salvific turning. 1 Ti 1:15 (M38-A-VCG-01) shows the apostle identifying himself as "chief of sinners," a conscience fully aware of its own guilt as the backdrop against which grace reaches. Tit 3:5 (M38-A-VCG-03) implicitly cleanses conscience through divine mercy. Heb 2:3 (M38-A-VCG-05) warns the conscience against the passive guilt of neglect. Conscience functions as both catalyst (driving toward salvation) and diagnostic (remaining as unclean background against which grace is received).
  - _cites:_ Act 16:30,2Co 7:10,1Ti 1:15,Tit 3:5,Heb 2:3
- **[21404 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged in the perseverance and working-out dimensions of the characteristic. Phili 2:12 (M38-A-VCG-05) is the clearest instance: "work out your own salvation with fear and trembling" — this is the integrated response of moral seriousness (fear and trembling), volition (working out), and sustained action (ongoing process). 1 Ti 4:16 (M38-A-VCG-05) shows the teacher who "watches his life and doctrine carefully" — conscientiousness as sustained moral-volitional watchfulness that mediates salvation for self and others. Jam 1:21 (M38-A-VCG-02) calls for "receiving with meekness the implanted word" — an integrated humble-receptive stance of moral awareness and active reception. The endurance cluster (Mat 10:22; Mar 13:13; M38-A-VCG-05) represents conscientiousness under sustained external pressure.
  - _cites:_ 1Ti 4:16,Jam 1:21,Mat 10:22,Mar 13:13
- **[21405 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Relational capacity is multiply engaged. (1) Vertically toward God: Joh 5:34 (M38-A-VCG-02) frames salvation as the goal of Jesus's testimony — a relational response to personal witness. Act 5:31 (M38-A-VCG-01) frames salvation as bestowed through the relational posture of repentance and forgiveness received from Christ as leader and savior. Rom 5:10 (M38-A-VCG-03, ANCHOR) defines the salvific movement as reconciliation — the restoration of broken vertical relation — through which rescue flows. (2) Horizontally: 1 Cor 7:16 (M38-A-VCG-10) names the relational hope of a believing spouse saving an unbelieving partner. Jam 5:20 (M38-A-VCG-10) frames the turning of a wandering sinner as person-to-person rescue mediated through relational persistence. Rom 11:14 (M38-A-VCG-08) names Paul's relational witness strategy toward his kinspeople as person-saving. Salvation is fundamentally relational in structure — both in its vertical reception from God and its horizontal mediation between persons.
  - _cites:_ Joh 5:34,Act 5:31,Rom 5:10,1Co 7:16,Jam 5:20,Rom 11:14
- **[21406 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic enables and deepens perceptive capacity. Luk 2:30 (M38-A-VCG-04) shows Simeon's eyes perceiving what was spiritually invisible to others — salvation enables spiritual sight. Heb 6:4 (M38-A-VCG-03) describes those who have "tasted the heavenly gift" as having a deepened inner sensory experience unavailable to the unsaved. Act 13:26 (M38-A-VCG-02) shows the receptive hearing that receives salvation as a qualitatively distinct perceptive openness. Negatively, Act 7:25 (M38-A-VCG-07) shows that closed perception blocks reception of salvation, implying that opened perception enables it. Where salvation is refused or abandoned (2 Pe 2:20, M38-A-VCG-01), perceptive capacity is impaired relative to initial reception — those who knew the Savior and fell back are worse off than those who never perceived. The direction of effect is: salvation received opens and deepens perception; salvation rejected or abandoned impairs it.
  - _cites:_ Luk 2:30,Heb 6:4,Act 13:26,Act 7:25,2Pe 2:20
- **[21407 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic enables and deepens cognition. Luk 1:77 (M38-A-VCG-04) names "knowledge of salvation" as part of salvation's content — the saved receive knowledge they previously lacked. 2 Ti 3:15 (M38-A-VCG-10) shows salvation deepening cognitive formation through Scripture's wisdom-shaping. 1 Ti 2:4 (M38-A-VCG-08) joins salvation with "knowledge of the truth," suggesting that genuine cognitive reception of truth is inseparable from rescue. The characteristic does not bypass cognition: 1 Cor 1:21 (M38-A-VCG-02) is explicit that human wisdom failed to reach God by its own path, but God's proclamation creates a new cognitive pathway through faith. Cognition is redirected and deepened rather than bypassed. Negatively, the gospel appears as "folly" to those perishing (1 Cor 1:18, M38-A-VCG-04), suggesting that unsaved cognition is impaired in its capacity to evaluate the salvific reality correctly.
  - _cites:_ Luk 1:77,2Ti 3:15,1Ti 2:4,1Co 1:21,1Co 1:18
- **[21408 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic engages memory in an enabling and stabilizing direction, though evidence is partial. Jude 5 (M38-A-VCG-10) uses recalled historical salvation to stabilize present inner faithfulness — the memory of past rescue functions as a warning resource. Rom 13:11 (M38-A-VCG-05) assumes the believer holds memory of "when we first believed" as a temporal anchor for present wakefulness. 1 Cor 15:2 (M38-A-VCG-04) warns against "vain" belief, implying the need to genuinely hold what was received — memory as a retention faculty that must remain active. The warning function is most prominent: where memory of salvation is not actively maintained, drift and neglect result (Heb 2:3, M38-A-VCG-05). No evidence suggests this characteristic impairs memory. The evidence is indirect and partial; the corpus does not explicitly theorize the characteristic's effect on memory.
  - _cites:_ Rom 13:11,1Co 15:2,Heb 2:3
- **[21409 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic consistently enables and deepens affect. Peace is conferred (Luk 7:50; Luk 2:30, M38-A-VCG-02 and -04). Joy is produced (Rev 7:10; 19:1, M38-A-VCG-10; the jailer's response in Act 16:30–31). Gratitude and praise overflow in 2 Cor 9:15 (M38-A-VCG-03) and Rev 7:10. Godly grief that leads to salvation (2 Cor 7:10, M38-A-VCG-09) is itself a deepened affective capacity — the capacity to feel appropriate sorrow over sin as a salvific movement. Astonishment at grace received by the unexpected (Act 10:45, M38-A-VCG-03) represents a widening of affective range. The characteristic does not impair affect; it transforms it from fear and guilt toward peace, gratitude, and joy. The "fear and trembling" of Phili 2:12 (M38-A-VCG-05) represents a refined and serious affective engagement with salvation rather than impairment.
  - _cites:_ Luk 7:50,Luk 2:30,Rev 7:10,Rev 7:19,Act 16:30,Act 16:31,2Co 9:15,2Co 7:10,Act 10:45
- **[21410 · silent]** **[CHAR-1]** S — The corpus is silent on creativity. No verse in M38-A evidences this characteristic enabling, deepening, bypassing, or impairing the creative or imaginative faculty. This silence is consistent with the characteristic's entirely receptive structure: salvation is received, not created. The absence of creativity-engagement is itself a datum about the characteristic's nature.
- **[21411 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic both presupposes and transforms volition. (1) Capacity: salvation reception requires a functioning will — the will must call (Act 2:21; Rom 10:13), turn (2 Cor 7:10), confess (Rom 10:9), surrender (Mat 16:25), and endure (Mat 10:22). The characteristic does not bypass the will's capacity but requires its engagement. (2) Interaction: the will interacts with conscience (which awakens it — Act 16:30), grace (which enables it — Eph 2:8; 2 Ti 1:9), and the word (which activates it — Jam 1:21). The characteristic enables the will by providing the divine power within which it operates: "it is God who works in you to will and to do" (Phili 2:13, implied by Phili 2:12 context in M38-A-VCG-05). (3) Constraints: the will operates under the constraint of grace — it cannot originate salvation but must receive it. Eph 2:8 (ANCHOR) explicitly excludes self-origination while requiring faith-reception. The will is constrained from self-sufficiency and enabled for receptive action.
  - _cites:_ Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,2Co 7:10,Rom 10:9,Mat 16:25,Mat 10:22,Act 16:30,Eph 2:2,Eph 2:8,Jam 1:21
- **[21412 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic enables and reshapes human agency without bypassing it. The primary salvific agency belongs to God throughout the corpus (M38-A-VCG-01: God sends, saves, exalts; M38-A-VCG-03: gift given). Human agency is enabled as a derivative, responsive capacity: calling (Act 2:21; Rom 10:13), confessing (Rom 10:9), working out (Phili 2:12), striving (Luk 13:23), adapting for others' salvation (1 Cor 9:22), turning sinners back (Jam 5:20). Salvation does not produce passivity — it redirects and empowers agency toward God and others. Where salvation is refused (2 Th 2:10; Luk 8:12), agency is impaired in its direction: the will actively refuses truth, and that refusal is judged as agentic failure. The characteristic does not bypass agency; it structures it as response to divine initiative.
  - _cites:_ Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Rom 10:9,Luk 13:23,1Co 9:22,Jam 5:20,2Th 2:10,Luk 8:12
- **[21413 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic deepens and clarifies moral evaluation. The gospel of salvation provides a new moral framework: 1 Cor 1:18 (M38-A-VCG-04) reveals that those being saved correctly evaluate the cross as God's power while those perishing evaluate it as folly — salvation sharpens the inner being's capacity to discern the true from the apparent. Jam 2:14 (M38-A-VCG-04) shows salvation enabling moral evaluation of faith's authenticity: "can that faith save him?" is a rigorous self-evaluative question. Heb 2:3 (M38-A-VCG-05) calls the inner being to evaluate the seriousness of neglecting rescue. Jude 23 (M38-A-VCG-10) shows moral evaluation operating in "holy revulsion toward sin-contamination" as part of rescuing others. The characteristic does not impair moral evaluation; it elevates its standard and sharpens its application.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 1:18,Jam 2:14,Heb 2:3
- **[21414 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic both activates and cleanses conscience. Activating: 2 Cor 7:10 (M38-A-VCG-09) shows conscience activated through godly grief as the precondition for repentance-leading-to-salvation. Act 16:30 (M38-A-VCG-02) shows conscience already activated, urgently driving the jailer to seek rescue. Cleansing: Tit 3:5 (M38-A-VCG-03) and 1 Ti 1:15 (M38-A-VCG-01) show salvation received by the "chief of sinners" — conscience that was weighted with guilt is addressed by the very salvation it sought. The helmet of salvation (Eph 6:17, M38-A-VCG-10) protects the mind/conscience from renewed accusation. Rev 12:10 (M38-A-VCG-10) explicitly shows the conscience vindicated as the accuser is cast down: "salvation has come" as the declaration that ends condemnation. The characteristic does not impair conscience; it first activates it (bringing to the surface what guilt is present) and then cleanses and vindicates it.
  - _cites:_ 2Co 7:10,Act 16:30,Tit 3:5,1Ti 1:15,Eph 6:17,Rev 12:10
- **[21415 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic deepens conscientiousness as an integrated moral-volitional-action response. Phili 2:12 (M38-A-VCG-05) is the fullest instance: "work out your own salvation with fear and trembling" requires the integration of moral seriousness (fear and trembling), deliberate volition (working out), and sustained action — which is precisely the definition of conscientiousness. 1 Ti 4:16 (M38-A-VCG-05) deepens this: the faithful teacher "watches his life and doctrine carefully" in an integrated sustained moral watchfulness motivated by the salvific goal. Jam 1:21 (M38-A-VCG-02) calls for the "meekness" of receptive conscientiousness. The endurance cluster (Mat 10:22; Mat 24:13; Mar 13:13) represents conscientiousness sustained under external assault. The characteristic does not bypass or impair conscientiousness; it provides the motivational ground (the weight of salvation received and to be secured) that drives its deepening.
  - _cites:_ 1Ti 4:16,Jam 1:21,Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Mar 13:13
- **[21416 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — This characteristic enables and deepens relational capacity in both vertical and horizontal directions. Vertically: Rom 5:10 (M38-A-VCG-03, ANCHOR) shows reconciliation as the constitutive relational act — former enmity transformed into ongoing relational security in God. Act 5:31 (M38-A-VCG-01) shows repentance and forgiveness as the relational restoration that salvation effects. Horizontally: the community of the saved (Act 2:47, M38-A-VCG-04) constitutes a relational body incorporated together. Jam 5:20 and 1 Cor 7:16 (M38-A-VCG-10) show salvation activating relational concern for others' rescue. Rom 11:14 (M38-A-VCG-08) shows Paul's relational investment in his kinspeople's salvation. The characteristic does not impair relational capacity; the one notable exception — 2 Pe 2:20 (M38-A-VCG-01) — shows that abandoned salvation correlates with deteriorated relational and moral capacity, confirming that received salvation is what sustains it.
  - _cites:_ Rom 5:10,Act 5:31,Act 2:47,Jam 5:20,1Co 7:16,Rom 11:14,2Pe 2:20
- **[21417 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that eschatological salvation necessarily enters through the perceptive faculty but is not reducible to it. The dominant perceptive mode is hearing (word received, message heard, gospel proclaimed), followed by sight (Simeon's eyes, the witnessed Savior), and taste (heavenly gift tasted in Heb 6:4). The consistent pattern is: an externally given salvific reality must be perceptively received before it is cognitively processed and volitionally accepted. Perception is the entry point, not the seat, of the characteristic. The negative evidence — closed understanding (Act 7:25), word stolen from the heart (Luk 8:12) — confirms that blocked perception blocks salvation-reception. This reveals that the characteristic is not self-generated by the inner being; it requires an external input that the inner perceptive faculty must be open to receive. The characteristic is constitutionally dependent on receptive perception.
  - _cites:_ Heb 6:4,Act 7:25,Luk 8:12
- **[21418 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that this characteristic requires cognition without being generated by it. Cognition is consistently engaged as a receptive and discerning faculty: grasping the word, understanding salvation offered, being formed by Scripture toward wisdom. But 1 Cor 1:21 (M38-A-VCG-02) is the critical statement: human wisdom could not reach God through its own cognitive effort — "the world did not know God through wisdom." God bypassed autonomous cognitive route and provided the foolishness of proclamation, through which faith (not cognition alone) receives rescue. The pattern shows that cognition is necessary but insufficient: it must be redirected from self-originating reasoning toward receptive comprehension of revealed truth. The characteristic reveals that human cognition in its unsaved state is constitutionally incapable of generating its own rescue — a finding about cognitive limitation in the unredeemed condition.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 1:21
- **[21419 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The limited but consistent pattern of memory-engagement reveals that eschatological salvation is a temporally extended rather than merely instantaneous characteristic. The initial reception (when one "first believed," Rom 13:11) must be held across time; the historical precedents of rescue (Jude 5) must be retained as warning resources; the received salvation must be maintained against drift (Heb 2:3; 1 Cor 15:2). This reveals that salvation is not only a point-event but an ongoing state that requires the memory faculty to anchor it against time-pressure. The modest engagement of memory — it appears in warning and stability contexts, not in the primary reception mechanism — suggests that memory is a supporting rather than constitutive faculty for this characteristic: it preserves and stabilizes received salvation but is not the means by which it is received.
  - _cites:_ Rom 13:11,Heb 2:1,Heb 2:3
- **[21420 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The rich and consistent affective engagement reveals that eschatological salvation is constitutionally transformative of the affective life — it cannot be received without producing inner experiential change. The pattern shows three affective phases: (1) pre-salvific affect — godly grief (2 Cor 7:10), awakened urgency (Act 16:30), astonishment at the barrier to salvation (Mat 19:25); (2) reception affect — peace conferred (Luk 7:50; Luk 2:30), awe at unexpected grace (Act 10:45); (3) ongoing and eschatological affect — gratitude (2 Cor 9:15), joy in community (Act 2:47 context), and the full corporate doxological eruption of the redeemed (Rev 7:10; 19:1). The pattern reveals that the characteristic is affectively whole: it engages and transforms the feeling-life from guilt and urgency through peace to joy and praise. A salvation without affective engagement would be, in this evidence-base, anomalous.
  - _cites:_ 2Co 7:10,Act 16:30,Mat 19:25,Luk 7:50,Luk 2:30,Act 10:45,2Co 9:15,Act 2:47,Rev 7:10,Rev 7:19
- **[21421 · silent]** **[CHAR-1]** S — The complete silence of the corpus on creativity reveals that eschatological salvation as evidenced in M38-A is entirely a received and responsive characteristic — nothing in its reception, sustaining, or completion requires or engages originative or imaginative capacity. The inner being's role is to receive, hold, confess, endure, and praise — all responsive modes. This consistent non-engagement of creativity is itself a structural finding: the characteristic is defined by its receptive rather than generative inner-being posture.
- **[21422 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The pervasive and multidimensional engagement of volition reveals that eschatological salvation is not a purely passive inner-being state but a characteristic that structures, redirects, and requires the will throughout its reception and maintenance. Three volitional patterns emerge: (1) Initial turning — the will turns toward Christ in calling, confession, faith-reception, and surrender of self-preservation (M38-A-VCG-02, -06, -09); (2) Sustained perseverance — the will must hold under trial and not defect (M38-A-VCG-05); (3) Missionary direction — the will is reshaped toward others' salvation through self-adaptation, relational persistence, and turning wanderers back (M38-A-VCG-08, T3.11). Critically, volitional engagement does not imply volitional self-sufficiency: Eph 2:8 (ANCHOR) and 2 Ti 1:9 exclude works and self-origination. The pattern reveals a characteristic that fully engages the will as receptive and persevering instrument while leaving it constitutionally dependent on grace.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:8,2Ti 1:9
- **[21423 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals a constitutive asymmetry in agency: God's agency is primary, unlimited, and originating; human agency is derivative, responsive, and enabled by divine initiative. The recurring structure is: God saves (active divine agency) → person calls, believes, confesses, endures (responsive human agency). The characteristic does not impair human agency but re-structures it: human agency is re-oriented away from self-generated rescue toward responsive participation in divinely initiated rescue. The most revealing instances are the missionary agency verses (1 Cor 9:22; Rom 11:14; Jam 5:20) — persons acting as agents of others' salvation, but always in the mode of servants of God's saving purpose (Act 13:47), not independent rescuers. The pattern discloses a characteristic that is simultaneously the most exclusively divine act (God alone saves) and the one that most fully mobilizes human agency in its service.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 9:22,Rom 11:14,Jam 5:20,Act 13:47
- **[21424 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The consistent engagement of moral evaluation reveals that eschatological salvation is not morally neutral — it creates a new moral framework within which the inner being evaluates truth, faith, and action. The pattern shows moral evaluation operating in three modes: (1) Evaluating conditions — assessing what salvation requires (Act 15:1; Jam 2:14); (2) Evaluating culpability — judging those who refuse or neglect salvation as morally accountable (Heb 2:3; 2 Th 2:10); (3) Evaluating authenticity — testing whether faith is genuine saving faith (Jam 2:14). The cross creates a new moral evaluation standard: 1 Cor 1:18 (M38-A-VCG-04) shows that what those being saved evaluate as "the power of God" is evaluated by those perishing as "folly." Received salvation calibrates moral evaluation; its absence or rejection distorts it. The characteristic is both the object of moral evaluation (is this genuine rescue?) and its recalibrator (it reshapes what the inner being evaluates as true, good, and powerful).
  - _cites:_ Act 15:1,Jam 2:14,Heb 2:2,Heb 2:3,1Co 1:18
- **[21425 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that conscience serves as the threshold faculty for salvation-reception: it must be activated before the will turns, and it must be cleansed after the will receives. This two-phase pattern — conscience as catalyst and conscience as object of cleansing — reveals that the characteristic addresses the inner being at its most morally sensitive point. The awakening of conscience (Act 16:30; 2 Cor 7:10) precedes the turn to salvation; the cleansing of conscience (Tit 3:5; Rev 12:10) is the mark of salvation received. The warning pattern (Heb 2:3; Jude 5) shows that an inactive conscience that fails to register the weight of salvation at risk is itself a sign of spiritual danger. The pattern reveals that eschatological salvation is the one inner-being reality that both requires a functioning conscience (to see the need for rescue) and constitutively addresses it (removing guilt and silencing accusation).
  - _cites:_ Act 16:2,Act 16:30,Tit 3:5,Rev 12:10,Heb 2:3
- **[21426 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that eschatological salvation, once received, generates conscientiousness as its ongoing inner-being expression. The evidence concentrates in M38-A-VCG-05 and the perseverance cluster: Phili 2:12, 1 Ti 4:16, Mat 10:22, Mat 24:13, and Mar 13:13 all show salvation sustained through integrated moral-volitional-action response. This reveals that the characteristic is not static or punctiliar in its inner-being effect — received salvation transforms the inner being into an agent of sustained conscientious response. The "working out" of salvation (Phili 2:12) is not an add-on to salvation but its present inner expression. The pattern suggests a constitutive connection: genuine eschatological salvation received generates conscientiousness; the absence of conscientiousness (endurance collapsing, life and doctrine unwatched) creates risk for the salvific state itself (1 Ti 4:16). Conscientiousness is both fruit and condition of maintained salvation.
  - _cites:_ Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Mar 13:13,1Ti 4:16
- **[21427 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The consistent and bilateral engagement of relational capacity reveals that eschatological salvation is constitutively relational in structure at every level. The pattern shows: (1) Salvation is received through a relational encounter (the Samaritans' direct encounter with Jesus, Joh 4:42; Simeon's personal witness, Luk 2:30; the jailer's direct appeal, Act 16:30–31); (2) Its content is relational restoration — reconciliation from enmity to access (Rom 5:10, ANCHOR); (3) Its communal expression is a body of persons incorporated together (Act 2:47); (4) Its ongoing motivation is relational concern for others' rescue (Rom 11:14; Jam 5:20; 1 Cor 7:16). The complete pattern reveals that this characteristic cannot be adequately described in purely transactional or forensic terms alone — it is simultaneously forensic (justification, condemnation reversed) and constitutively relational (reconciliation, community, person-to-person witness and mediation). Relational capacity is not incidental to the characteristic but belongs to its definition.
  - _cites:_ Joh 4:42,Luk 2:30,Act 16:30,Act 16:31,Rom 5:10,Act 2:47,Rom 11:14,Jam 5:1,Jam 5:20
- **[21428 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence overwhelmingly shows eschatological salvation operating primarily as a divine initiative directed toward the human person. God is the consistent grammatical and theological agent: in Joh 3:17 "God sent his Son not to condemn but to save the world"; in Eph 2:8 salvation by grace through faith is "God's gift"; in Tit 3:5 "God saved us not by our righteous deeds but by his mercy"; in 2Ti 1:9 "God saved believers and called them to holiness by grace purposed before time." The Spirit is given as dōrea (Act 2:38, Act 10:45, Act 11:17). Righteousness is bestowed as gift (Rom 5:15-17, M38-A-VCG-03). Christ is the Savior sent by divine appointment (Act 13:23, 1Jo 4:14, Act 5:31). The divine-to-human vector is structurally foundational — salvation does not originate in the human person and moves toward them from outside, grounded in divine purpose established before time.
  - _cites:_ Joh 3:17,Eph 2:8,Tit 3:5,2Ti 1:9,Act 2:38,Act 10:45,Act 11:17,Rom 5:15,Rom 5:16,Rom 5:17,Act 13:23,1Jn 4:14,Act 5:31
- **[21429 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows multiple forms of human movement toward God in connection with eschatological salvation. Calling on the name of the Lord (Act 2:21, Rom 10:13) is an act of invocation directed toward God. Heart-belief and mouth-confession (Rom 10:9-10) are inner and outer acts of orientation toward God and Christ. The jailer's urgent question "what must I do to be saved?" (Act 16:30) represents conscience-awakened seeking directed toward the divine. Simeon's reception of salvation (Luk 2:30) and his readiness to depart in peace represent fulfilled hope resting in God. Doxological response — the heavenly multitude ascribing salvation to God and the Lamb (Rev 7:10, Rev 19:1) — constitutes worship as the human-to-God culmination of salvation. The apostle's intercessory prayer for others' salvation (Rom 10:1) represents the saved person's movement toward God on behalf of others.
  - _cites:_ Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Rom 10:9,Rom 10:10,Act 16:30,Luk 2:30,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,Rom 10:1
- **[21430 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows eschatological salvation being extended person-to-person in several modes. Peter exhorts the crowd to be saved from a corrupt generation (Act 2:40), functioning as a herald extending the opportunity of rescue. Paul adapts himself to all people in order to save some (1Cor 9:22) and denies himself to seek others' rescue (1Cor 10:33). Paul endures suffering so the elect may obtain salvation (2Ti 2:10). The apostle aims to provoke jealousy in his kinspeople to save some (Rom 11:14). A teacher who watches his doctrine and life will save his hearers (1Ti 4:16). Jam 5:20 names rescuing a wandering sinner as saving a soul from death. Jude 23 urges snatching others from fire. The human giving of this characteristic is consistently mediated through proclamation, witness, relational persistence, and costly self-sacrifice — the person does not originate salvation but acts as its conduit.
  - _cites:_ Act 2:40,1Co 9:22,1Co 10:33,2Ti 2:10,Rom 11:14,1Ti 4:16,Jam 5:20
- **[21431 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence frequently presents salvation as received through a human intermediary bearing a proclaimed message. Act 11:14 links household salvation to a message proclaimed by Cornelius's messenger. Act 13:26 presents the word of salvation as dispatched to receptive hearers. Eph 1:13 structures reception as: "hearing the gospel of salvation → believing → being sealed with the Spirit." Rom 1:16 identifies the gospel as God's power unto salvation received through faith in the message. 1Cor 1:21 states God chose the "foolishness of preaching to save those who believe," making the proclaimed word the human channel. Jam 1:21 presents the "implanted word" as the medium through which salvation is received. The inner act required for reception is faith — hearing, trusting, and accepting the message rather than generating rescue from within.
  - _cites:_ Act 11:14,Act 13:26,Eph 1:13,Rom 1:16,1Co 1:21,Jam 1:21
- **[21432 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows salvation operating across relational distance as a characteristic feature. Joh 4:42 identifies Jesus as "Savior of the world," confirmed by Samaritans — crossing ethnic and religious boundaries. Act 13:47 mandates salvation to the ends of the earth. Rom 11:11 and 11:26 show salvation moving from Israel's stumbling to Gentile inclusion and back. Act 10:45 and Act 11:17 present the Spirit-gift given equally to Gentiles as astonishing boundary-crossing. 1Ti 2:4 states God desires "all people" to be saved. Within existing bonds, 1Cor 7:16 shows a believing spouse holding uncertain hope for an unbelieving partner, and 1Ti 2:15 links salvation to faithful domestic conduct. The evidence suggests no operation restricted to pre-existing relational bonds — salvation characteristically crosses ethnic, social, and religious distance — but relational proximity also functions as a channel (household salvation, Act 11:14, Act 16:31).
  - _cites:_ Joh 4:42,Act 13:47,Rom 11:11,Act 10:45,Act 11:17,1Ti 2:4,1Co 7:16,1Ti 2:15,Act 11:14,Act 16:31
- **[21433 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows both angelic and adversarial engagement with eschatological salvation. On the angelic side: Luk 2:11 presents an angel announcing the Savior's birth; Rev 7:10 and Rev 12:10 depict heavenly beings witnessing and proclaiming salvation's completion. Rev 12:10 specifically names the accuser's defeat as the moment salvation is declared — the heavenly accuser is cast down, conscience of the accused is vindicated. On the adversarial side: Luk 8:12 shows the devil actively removing the word from hearts to prevent belief and thereby block salvation — faith is the battleground. Rev 12:10 identifies Satan as the one who "accuses them day and night," framing eschatological salvation as the resolution of adversarial accusation. The adversary works against the reception of salvation; angelic beings witness and declare it.
  - _cites:_ Luk 2:11,Rev 7:10,Rev 12:10,Luk 8:12
- **[21434 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence reveals a complex basis combining unconditional divine initiative with covenantal grounding and responsive appropriation. The initiative is unconditional in origin: Eph 2:8 states salvation is "God's gift, not of works"; 2Ti 1:9 places it in grace "purposed before time"; Tit 3:5 grounds it in "mercy" not human righteousness. The covenantal dimension is explicit: Act 13:23 roots the Savior in David's covenant line; Rom 11:26-27 grounds all Israel's future rescue in prophetic covenant promise. The responsive dimension is present but secondary: salvation is appropriated by those who call (Act 2:21, Rom 10:13), believe (Eph 2:8, Act 16:31), confess (Rom 10:9), and hear (Eph 1:13). The faith-response is itself not an originating condition but a reception point — Eph 2:8 explicitly denies that even faith is self-generated. The overall structure is: unconditional divine initiative, covenantally grounded, received through responsive faith.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:8,2Ti 1:9,Tit 3:5,Act 13:23,Rom 11:26,Rom 11:27,Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Act 16:31,Rom 10:9,Eph 1:13
- **[21435 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence identifies multiple inner postures required for the human-toward-God movement in receiving salvation. Heart-belief (Rom 10:9-10) names an inner faculty of trust directed toward the risen Christ. Calling on the Lord's name (Act 2:21, Rom 10:13) requires the will to invoke, an active turning of the person toward God. Repentance (Act 2:38, Act 2:40) involves a decisive inner turning away from sin and moral separation. Godly sorrow (2Cor 7:10) identifies a specific grief-quality that produces genuine repentance rather than worldly despair. Meekness/humility of will (Jam 1:21) enables the soul to receive the implanted word. The self-renunciation texts (Mat 16:25, Mar 8:35, Luk 9:24) reveal that surrender of self-protective instincts is the posture through which the true self is eschatologically saved. An awakened conscience seeking rescue (Act 16:30) and a will striving to enter the narrow gate (Luk 13:23) also appear. The common inner posture is receptive, non-self-sufficient, will-turning orientation.
  - _cites:_ Rom 10:9,Rom 10:10,Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Act 2:38,Act 2:40,2Co 7:10,Jam 1:21,Mat 16:25,Mar 8:35,Luk 9:24,Act 16:30,Luk 13:23
- **[21436 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence reveals specific inner conditions required in those who mediate salvation to others. Missional love is foundational: Paul's self-adaptation (1Cor 9:22) and self-denial (1Cor 10:33) are driven by love for others' rescue, with the will wholly oriented toward the eschatological good of others rather than personal advantage. Intercessory desire and prayer (Rom 10:1) name the heart's sustained longing as an enabling condition. Willingness to suffer for others' rescue (2Ti 2:10) identifies self-sacrifice as an enabling posture. Faithful teaching with a watchful conscience (1Ti 4:16) names doctrinal and moral integrity as enabling conditions for saving one's hearers. The rescue of wandering sinners (Jam 5:20) and snatching from fire (Jude 23) require courageous, fear-engaged moral seriousness. The common thread is: the giver must themselves be rooted in received grace (not self-originating rescue) and must be moved by other-directed will rather than self-interest.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 9:22,1Co 10:33,Rom 10:1,2Ti 2:10,1Ti 4:16,Jam 5:20
- **[21437 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence identifies both enabling and blocking conditions for reception from a human intermediary. Enabling conditions include: a receptive, fear-reverent hearing stance (Act 13:26); an awakened conscience seeking rescue (Act 16:30); meekness of will before the implanted word (Jam 1:21); and belief in the message received (Eph 1:13, 1Cor 1:21). The will's openness to hearing is essential — Act 28:28 contrasts Gentiles "who will listen" with those who rejected the message. Blocking conditions include: love of truth refused (2Th 2:10, "perishing refused love of truth"), understanding closed to the deliverance offered (Act 7:25), and the devil removing the word before it can take root (Luk 8:12). Act 8:20 reveals a heart oriented toward purchasing grace as a commodity — a mercenary inner orientation that fundamentally misunderstands what is being offered. The primary blocker is the will's closing against truth; the primary enabler is the will's openness and receptive faith.
  - _cites:_ Act 13:26,Act 16:30,Jam 1:21,Eph 1:13,1Co 1:21,Act 28:28,2Th 2:10,Act 7:25,Luk 8:12,Act 8:20
- **[21438 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence consistently shows eschatological salvation crossing covenantal boundaries rather than remaining confined to them. The Samaritans confess Jesus as "Savior of the world" (Joh 4:42); salvation is declared available to "all who call" without ethnic restriction (Act 2:21, Rom 10:13); Act 13:47 mandates carrying salvation to "the ends of the earth"; Act 10:45 and 11:17 record the Spirit-gift crossing to Gentiles; Rom 11:11-14 describes salvation reaching Gentiles through Israel's stumbling; 1Ti 2:4 states God desires "all people" to be saved. Joh 4:22 preserves the covenantal origin ("salvation is from the Jews") while Act 13:23 roots Jesus in Davidic covenant. The pattern is: covenantal origin that generates universal scope — salvation originates within covenant history but is not bounded by covenantal membership. The ethnic and merit-based restrictions are explicitly refused (Act 15:11, Act 15:1 contrast).
  - _cites:_ Joh 4:42,Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Act 13:47,Act 10:45,Rom 11:11,Rom 11:12,Rom 11:13,Rom 11:14,1Ti 2:4,Joh 4:22,Act 13:23,Act 15:11,Act 15:1
- **[21439 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence explicitly identifies eschatological salvation as a site of adversarial attack and distortion. Luk 8:12 names the devil as the agent who "removes the word from hearts to prevent belief and salvation" — the inner act of faith is the battleground and the devil targets it directly. Rev 12:10 presents Satan as the perpetual accuser whose activity works against the inner-being assurance that salvation secures — his defeat is the occasion for salvation's declaration. Act 8:20 shows the gift of God being targeted for commodification — a distortion of salvation's grace-character into a purchasable commodity, which Peter identifies as a spiritually dangerous inner orientation. False teaching that adds conditions to salvation (Act 15:1, Act 15:11) also functions as a distorting force — placing works-requirements where grace-reception belongs. The adversary's primary strategy appears to be: remove or corrupt the word before faith can be exercised, or distort salvation's character from gift to transaction.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:12,Rev 12:10,Act 8:20,Act 15:1,Act 15:11
- **[21440 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence reveals a divine disposition of unreserved, initiative-taking saving intent toward the human person. Joh 3:17 states the entire purpose of the Son's sending is to save, not condemn, revealing saving as God's primary disposition rather than a reluctant concession. 1Ti 2:4 shows God desiring "all people" to be saved — the disposition is universal in scope. Tit 2:11 presents grace "appearing" to bring salvation to all, revealing a disposition of generous self-disclosure. Eph 2:5 shows God acting on an inner state of spiritual death — the person contributes nothing, God acts wholly from his own merciful disposition. Rom 5:15-17 (M38-A-VCG-03) shows the gift abounding "far beyond" what the trespass produced, revealing an overflowing rather than minimal generosity. Tit 3:5 grounds salvation in "mercy," not human worth. The aggregate picture is a God whose disposition toward the human person is actively, extravagantly, and unconditionally saving — prior to and without regard to human merit.
  - _cites:_ Joh 3:17,1Ti 2:4,Tit 2:11,Eph 2:5,Rom 5:15,Rom 5:16,Rom 5:17,Tit 3:5
- **[21441 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The human-toward-God movement in eschatological salvation reveals a relationship of complete dependence, recognized need, and responsive trust. The disciples' astonished question "who then can be saved?" (Mat 19:25, Mar 10:26, Luk 18:26) reveals a relationship in which the human person recognizes their own complete inability — no self-sufficiency before God is possible. Calling on the name (Act 2:21, Rom 10:13) reveals a relationship of invocation — the person as one who calls, God as the one who saves. Confession of Christ as Lord (Rom 10:9) reveals the person's relational alignment under divine authority. The doxological response (Rev 7:10, Rev 19:1) reveals that at completion the relationship is one of joyful ascription — the person giving all credit for rescue to God and the Lamb, holding nothing for themselves. Self-renunciation texts (Mat 16:25 etc.) reveal that the relationship to God requires release of autonomous self-possession. The relationship is one of acknowledged need, receptive dependence, and grateful worship.
  - _cites:_ Mat 19:25,Mar 10:26,Luk 18:26,Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Rom 10:9,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,Mat 16:25
- **[21442 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence implies that genuine mediation of salvation to others requires prior reception of salvation oneself, though this is stated indirectly. Paul's self-adaptation (1Cor 9:22) and self-denial (1Cor 10:33) are intelligible only as responses to grace already received — the motivation is "that many may be saved" from a position of already being saved. 2Ti 2:10 shows Paul enduring suffering for the elect's salvation as one who has himself received it. The teacher who saves his hearers (1Ti 4:16) must watch his own life and doctrine — implying a prior integrity of received truth. Jude 23 urges rescue of others "with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh" — requiring a conscience already engaged by what sin means. Rom 10:1 reveals intercessory desire as a movement of one who has already received what they desire for others. The rescue of wandering sinners (Jam 5:20) presupposes the rescuer is not themselves wandering. The evidence consistently implies: to mediate salvation one must first have received the gift rather than originated it.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 9:22,1Co 10:33,2Ti 2:10,1Ti 4:16,Rom 10:1,Jam 5:20
- **[21443 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence describes the non-receiving person in terms of several inner states. Refusal of truth's love (2Th 2:10) characterizes the perishing — the will is closed against the very content that would bring rescue. Act 7:25 describes understanding that was closed to the deliverance offered — the people "did not understand" that God was working salvation, a failure of perception combined with unreceptive inner state. Luk 8:12 shows the word removed before faith can form — the inner state remains unchanged, without the transformation faith would bring. Those who reject the message in Act 28:28 are contrasted with Gentiles who will listen. The state of the non-receiver in Rev 12:10 is one of perpetual accusation without vindication. 2Pe 2:20 describes those who once knew the Savior but turned back — characterized as worse off than their original ignorance, with defilement returning. The non-receiver exists in a state of perishing (1Cor 1:18), wrath-facing (1Th 5:9), condemnation (Mar 16:16), and ongoing spiritual danger — the structural opposite of the rescued state.
  - _cites:_ 2Th 2:10,Act 7:25,Luk 8:12,Act 28:28,Rev 12:10,2Pe 2:20,1Co 1:18,1Th 5:9,Mar 16:16
- **[21444 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence presents eschatological salvation with an explicitly universal intended scope bounded only by receptive response. Included: all who call (Rom 10:13, "everyone who calls"), all who believe (Act 16:31, "you and your household"), Samaritans (Joh 4:42), Gentiles to the ends of the earth (Act 13:47), all Israel eschatologically (Rom 11:26), all people as God's desire (1Ti 2:4), a multitude from every nation (Rev 7:10). The gift is characterized as given equally — not differentiated by ethnicity, gender, or social standing. Excluded by their own response: those who refuse truth (2Th 2:10), those who do not call or believe (Mar 16:16), those who neglect salvation (Heb 2:3), those who do not hold fast (1Cor 15:2). The remnant-election texts (Rom 9:27) preserve divine sovereign selectivity within this universal scope. The structure is: universal divine intent meeting particular response, with no human category intrinsically excluded from the offer.
  - _cites:_ Rom 10:13,Act 16:31,Joh 4:42,Act 13:47,Rom 11:26,1Ti 2:4,Rev 7:10,2Th 2:10,Mar 16:16,Heb 2:3,1Co 15:2,Rom 9:27
- **[21445 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows angelic involvement at two points, though not as primary mediators of salvation itself. Luk 2:11 records an angel announcing the Savior's birth — angelic ministry at the point of disclosure. Rev 7:10 and Rev 19:1 depict heavenly beings (angelic and redeemed multitudes) participating in the doxological proclamation of salvation's completion — their role is witness and worship rather than mediation. Rev 12:10 presents the heavenly voice declaring salvation at the moment the accuser is cast down — an angelic or heavenly figure announcing salvation's vindication. The birth announcement (Luk 2:11) is the clearest case of angelic communication of salvation-news. However, the primary mediatorial role is assigned to Christ (Heb 7:25, his ongoing intercession), to the proclaimed word (1Cor 1:21), and to the Spirit (Eph 1:13, Tit 3:5). Angelic ministry in the evidence is more declaratory and doxological than structurally constitutive of how salvation reaches the human person.
  - _cites:_ Luk 2:11,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,Rev 12:10,Heb 7:25,1Co 1:21,Eph 1:13,Tit 3:5
- **[21446 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on God-to-human operation; it is the dominant vector of this entire characteristic. See T4.1.1 and T4.1.2 for detailed findings. God's initiative in sending the Son (Joh 3:17), giving grace as gift (Eph 2:8), giving the Spirit (Act 10:45, Act 11:17), and working salvation through mercy (Tit 3:5) constitutes the structural foundation of M38-A across all ten VCGs.
  - _cites:_ Joh 3:17,Eph 2:8,Act 10:45,Act 11:17,Tit 3:5
- **[21447 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on human-to-God operation. See T4.2.1 and T4.2.2 for detailed findings. Calling, belief, confession, repentance, intercessory prayer, and doxological worship all appear as human-toward-God movements in the evidence across M38-A-VCG-02, VCG-09, and VCG-10.
- **[21448 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on the giving direction. See T4.3.1 through T4.3.3. Human-to-human mediation of salvation appears in M38-A-VCG-05 (Paul's missionary self-sacrifice), VCG-08 (apostolic witness and intercession), VCG-10 (Jam 5:20, Jude 23), and VCG-04 (Act 2:40). The giving direction is a sustained pattern in the evidence.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:20,Act 2:40
- **[21449 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on the receiving direction. See T4.4.1 through T4.4.3. Reception of salvation through a human-borne proclaimed word is a core mechanism in M38-A-VCG-02 (Eph 1:13, 1Cor 1:21, Rom 1:16) and M38-A-VCG-04 (Act 2:47, Jam 1:21). The receiving direction is extensively documented.
  - _cites:_ Eph 1:13,1Co 1:21,Rom 1:16,Act 2:47,Jam 1:21
- **[21450 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on relational boundaries. See T4.5.1 through T4.5.3. The evidence is notably explicit about boundary-crossing (Gentile inclusion, universal scope) and boundary-origin (covenantal rootedness in Jewish-Davidic history), making relational scope one of the most extensively discussed dimensions of this characteristic.
- **[21451 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on the spiritual beings interface. See T4.6.1 through T4.6.3. Adversarial activity against salvation-reception (Luk 8:12, Rev 12:10), angelic declaration of salvation (Luk 2:11, Rev 7:10), and the accuser's defeat as the occasion of salvation's announcement (Rev 12:10) all appear in the evidence.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:12,Rev 12:10,Luk 2:11,Rev 7:10
- **[21452 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows transformation operating at the level of condition, orientation, and both simultaneously. Condition is changed: in Eph 2:5 the person moves from spiritual death to being "made alive with Christ"; in Rom 5:15-17 the inner state moves from condemnation to justification; in Tit 3:5 the Spirit's regenerating work changes the person's inner constitution; in Act 2:47 persons are added to the community of the saved from a prior state of being outside it. Orientation is also changed: Rom 5:9-10 shows the person's orientation shifting from enemy-of-God to reconciled-through-Christ; the self-renunciation texts (Mat 16:25, Mar 8:35) show the will reoriented from self-preservation to Christ-following; 2Cor 7:10 shows godly grief reorienting the person away from sin toward life. The doxological completion texts (Rev 7:10, Rev 19:1) show both a condition of being fully delivered and an orientation of praise. Both condition and orientation are transformed, with condition-change typically preceding and enabling orientation-change in the evidence's sequence.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:5,Rom 5:15,Rom 5:16,Rom 5:17,Tit 3:5,Act 2:47,Rom 5:9,Rom 5:10,Mat 16:25,Mar 8:35,2Co 7:10,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1
- **[21453 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence describes sequences explicitly in several texts. Eph 1:13 gives a clear sequence: hearing the gospel → faith exercised → sealed with the Spirit — three distinguishable inner states. 2Cor 7:10 sequences: encounter with godly grief → repentance → salvation without regret, contrasting with worldly grief → death. Rom 5:10 moves: enemy status → reconciliation through Christ's death → present and future salvation through risen life — three stages. The jailer sequence (Act 16:30-31): conscience-awakened danger → urgent seeking → faith directed at Christ → promised rescue. Eph 2:5 implies: spiritual death → divine action on that dead state → made alive with Christ. The self-renunciation texts (Mat 16:25) structure: self-preservation will → surrender → eschatological finding/keeping. The before-state is consistently: sin-laden, condemned, dead, lost, or enemy; the during-state involves the will turning, faith exercised, or word received; the after-state is saved, justified, reconciled, sealed, and secured.
  - _cites:_ Eph 1:13,2Co 7:10,Rom 5:10,Act 16:30,Act 16:31,Eph 2:5,Mat 16:25
- **[21454 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence reveals multiple mechanisms. The primary mechanism is encounter with the proclaimed word meeting the act of faith: 1Cor 1:21 (God's "foolishness of preaching" saves those who believe), Eph 1:13 (gospel heard and believed), Jam 1:21 (implanted word saves the soul). This encounter-mechanism can be sudden (Act 16:30-31, the jailer's immediate crisis and response) or presupposed as a completed event from which ongoing transformation flows. A second mechanism is divine regenerative action: Tit 3:5 names the Spirit's "regenerating and renewing work" as the mechanism, acting on the inner person directly rather than through cognitive encounter alone. A third mechanism is the sustained will under pressure: M38-A-VCG-05 presents endurance as the mechanism through which salvation is reached (Mat 10:22, Mat 24:13, Heb 5:9). A fourth is self-renunciation: the will releasing self-interest (Mat 16:25 register) as a mechanism of eschatological finding. The mechanisms differ by context — crisis produces sudden encounter, formation produces gradual endurance, Spirit-action produces regeneration — but all converge on the same transformation.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 1:21,Eph 1:13,Jam 1:21,Act 16:30,Act 16:31,Tit 3:5,Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Heb 5:9,Mat 16:25
- **[21455 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows suffering functioning in multiple relations to eschatological salvation. Suffering as a context requiring endurance: Mat 10:22, Mat 24:13, and Mar 13:13 present persecution and hatred as the context within which the persevering will holds until salvation is reached. Suffering as the path of the mediator: 2Ti 2:10 shows Paul accepting suffering as the context in which the elect obtain salvation; Heb 2:10 describes Christ as "made perfect through suffering" as the founder of salvation — suffering is structurally part of the path to eschatological completion. Self-renunciation as voluntary suffering: Mat 16:25 register frames willingness to lose life as the condition of eschatological finding — suffering-of-self-surrender as the mechanism. Salvation as deliverance from affliction: Heb 2:3, Heb 5:9, and Rev 12:10 position salvation as the resolution of danger, affliction, and accusation. The evidence treats suffering not as incidental but as structurally present — both in the mediator's path (Christ and apostles) and in the believer's perseverance context.
  - _cites:_ Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Mar 13:13,2Ti 2:10,Heb 2:10,Mat 16:25,Heb 2:3,Heb 5:9,Rev 12:10
- **[21456 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows partial but genuine engagement with ongoing formation. 1Cor 1:18 and 1Cor 15:2 use present-tense sōzō ("being saved"), indicating salvation as an ongoing state rather than solely a past event. Phili 2:12 urges "working out your own salvation with reverent seriousness," indicating that salvation engages the will, conscience, and sustained moral effort over time. 1Ti 4:16 links watching one's life and doctrine to saving both self and hearers — an ongoing formational discipline. 1Ti 2:15 conditions ongoing salvation on persisting in faith, love, holiness, and self-control — naming virtues that must be sustained over time. 2Ti 1:9 grounds the calling in a "holy calling" — linking salvation's reception to ongoing sanctified identity. 2Pe 3:18 calls those who know the Savior to grow in grace and knowledge — salvation-identity as a starting point for ongoing formation. The evidence does not present sanctification as the mechanism of salvation but does show salvation generating an ongoing formational trajectory in the person.
  - _cites:_ 1Co 1:18,1Co 15:2,1Ti 4:16,1Ti 2:15,2Ti 1:9,2Pe 3:18
- **[21457 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence extensively points present salvation toward eschatological fullness. Rom 5:9 grounds present justification in future assurance of being "saved from wrath." Rom 13:11 names salvation as "nearer than when we first believed," framing it as an approaching horizon. Mat 10:22 and Mat 24:13 tie final salvation explicitly to "the end" — a future telos reached through present endurance. Heb 7:25 describes Christ saving "completely" those who come to him through unbroken intercession — a salvation that extends into the future without limit. Rev 7:10 and Rev 19:1 present the doxological endpoint of the eschatological arrival. Eph 6:17 describes the "helmet of salvation" worn in present spiritual warfare oriented toward final protection. 2Ti 4:18 anticipates rescue into "the heavenly kingdom." The present experience of salvation is consistently oriented toward a future fullness: complete rescue from wrath, entry into the kingdom, and the reigning in life (Rom 5:17) — with present salvation as the real but partial first installment.
  - _cites:_ Rom 5:9,Rom 13:11,Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Heb 7:25,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,Eph 6:17,2Ti 4:18,Rom 5:17
- **[21458 · gap]** **[CHAR-1]** G — T2.8 findings are not available within this tier-pair's evidence block. The question requires data from T2.8 (constitutional deposit analysis) which has not been presented in the current segment. A gap is recorded: the developmental consequence of any constitutional deposit cannot be determined from the evidence provided here without the T2.8 finding to anchor it.
- **[21459 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence presents a genuinely mixed picture, with both irreversibility and reversibility indicated in different texts, creating partial disjointedness. Irreversibility is implied by: Heb 7:25 ("saves completely," with unbroken intercession), Eph 2:5 (made alive, a past completed state), 2Ti 1:9 (grace "purposed before time"), 1Th 5:9 (appointed for salvation, not wrath), and the assurance language of Rom 5:9-10. Reversibility or serious risk is indicated by: 2Pe 2:20 (those who escaped defilement through knowing the Savior but fall back are "worse off"), 1Cor 15:2 (believing "in vain" is a genuine risk if the gospel is not held fast), Heb 2:3 (neglecting salvation risks inescapable judgment), Heb 5:9 (salvation conditioned on obedience), and Mat 10:22 (salvation tied to enduring to the end). The evidence does not resolve this tension — it holds both irreversible-divine-purpose and genuine-human-risk as live data points. The transformation's permanence appears to depend on which axis of the evidence is foregrounded: the divine-gift axis (irreversible) or the human-receptivity axis (conditionally maintained).
  - _cites:_ Heb 7:25,Eph 2:5,2Ti 1:9,1Th 5:9,Rom 5:9,Rom 5:10,2Pe 2:20,1Co 15:2,Heb 2:3,Heb 5:9,Mat 10:22
- **[21460 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Drawing on the sequences identified in T5.2.1, the recurring inner states across the evidence are: (1) **Before**: spiritual death (Eph 2:5), enemy-status (Rom 5:10), lost/perishing (Luk 19:10, 1Cor 1:18), burdened by sin (Luk 7:50, 1Ti 1:15), conscience awakened to danger (Act 16:30), or the will closed against truth (2Th 2:10). (2) **At reception**: faith exercised (Eph 2:8), will turning (Act 2:38, Rom 10:9), word received (Eph 1:13), calling invoked (Act 2:21, Rom 10:13), self surrendered (Mat 16:25). (3) **After reception**: justified (Rom 5:16), reconciled (Rom 5:10), sealed with Spirit (Eph 1:13), peace received (Luk 7:50), made alive (Eph 2:5), incorporated into community (Act 2:47). (4) **Ongoing**: being saved (1Cor 1:18), working out salvation (Phili 2:12), enduring to the end (Mat 10:22), growing in grace (2Pe 3:18). (5) **Completion**: rescued fully into heavenly kingdom (2Ti 4:18), reigning in life (Rom 5:17), doxological worship (Rev 7:10, Rev 19:1). The sequence reveals that salvation is structured as movement from death/lostness through a pivot of receptive faith into present-and-progressing rescue with eschatological completion.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:5,Rom 5:10,Luk 19:10,1Co 1:18,Luk 7:50,1Ti 1:15,Act 16:30,2Th 2:10,Eph 2:8,Act 2:38,Rom 10:9,Eph 1:13,Act 2:21,Rom 10:13,Mat 16:25,Rom 5:16,Act 2:47,Mat 10:22,2Pe 3:18,2Ti 4:18,Rom 5:17,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1
- **[21461 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence does distinguish mechanisms by context, though not always explicitly. In crisis contexts: Act 16:30-31 shows sudden encounter, awakened conscience, immediate faith-response, and rapid rescue — a crisis-encounter mechanism. In preaching contexts: 1Cor 1:21 and Eph 1:13 show the proclaimed word as the mechanism, requiring hearing, then believing, then sealing — a sequential word-faith-Spirit mechanism. In perseverance contexts: Mat 10:22, Mat 24:13, and Heb 5:9 show sustained endurance of the will as the mechanism — a gradual, sustained-will mechanism operating over extended time under pressure. In inner-moral contexts: 2Cor 7:10 shows godly sorrow producing repentance as the mechanism — an affective-moral movement. In divine-regenerative contexts: Tit 3:5 shows the Spirit acting directly on the person through regeneration and renewal — a divine-transformation mechanism not mediated through human crisis or preaching. In self-renunciation contexts: Mat 16:25 register shows the will's release of self-interest as the mechanism. The mechanisms are context-differentiated, not uniform, suggesting that salvation's transformation operates through varied divine and human channels suited to the person's specific condition.
  - _cites:_ Act 16:30,Act 16:31,1Co 1:21,Eph 1:13,Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Heb 5:9,2Co 7:10,Tit 3:5,Mat 16:25
- **[21462 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows suffering functioning primarily as a testing and deepening context rather than as the generative cause of salvation. Suffering tests perseverance: Mat 10:22, Mat 24:13, and Mar 13:13 present external hatred and persecution as the pressure under which the will's genuine commitment to salvation is tested — those who endure show their salvation is real; those who defect show it was not. Suffering reveals salvific priority: 1Cor 5:5 (delivering the man to Satan for fleshly destruction so his spirit may be saved) reveals that suffering of the body can be the occasion through which eschatological rescue of the spirit is distinguished from temporal comfort. Paul's suffering in 2Ti 2:10 deepens his identification with others' salvation — the apostle's affliction becomes the medium through which others receive the eschatological gift. Heb 2:10 shows Christ's suffering as the constitutive path through which he became the "founder of salvation" — suffering not incidental but perfecting. The evidence is partially disjointed here: suffering is more consistently a testing/deepening context than a producing cause, with the exception of Christ's suffering which is structurally generative.
  - _cites:_ Mat 10:22,Mat 24:13,Mar 13:13,1Co 5:5,2Ti 2:10,Heb 2:10
- **[21463 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence reveals that eschatological salvation functions as both the origin-event and the ongoing context of sanctification, though the relationship is more implicit than systematically stated. Salvation received generates a calling to holiness: 2Ti 1:9 states God "saved us and called us to a holy calling" — salvation and sanctification are joined in the same divine act, with sanctification as the trajectory opened by rescue. 2Th 2:13 describes salvation received through "the Spirit's sanctifying work and personal belief in truth" — the Spirit's sanctifying activity is the inner medium through which salvation is appropriated, making sanctification constitutive of the reception. 1Ti 2:15 conditions ongoing salvation on persisting in holiness as a virtue-state. 2Pe 3:18 shows the saved person called to grow in grace and knowledge. The evidence positions salvation not as sanctification's endpoint but as its precondition and initiating frame — rescue from condemnation opens the space within which ongoing formation proceeds. The characteristic does not replace sanctification but generates the saved-and-calling context within which character formation becomes possible and mandatory.
  - _cites:_ 2Ti 1:9,2Th 2:13,1Ti 2:15,2Pe 3:18
- **[21464 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence specifies what the present experience of salvation anticipates. Present justification anticipates future deliverance from wrath: Rom 5:9 explicitly draws this inference — "having been justified, we shall be saved from wrath." Present sealing with the Spirit (Eph 1:13) anticipates the fullness of Spirit-possession as eschatological guarantee. Present reigning through received grace and righteousness (Rom 5:17) anticipates the full exercise of that reign in eschatological life. The "helmet of salvation" (Eph 6:17) as present armor anticipates final protection. Present being-saved (1Cor 1:18) anticipates full deliverance. The approaching character of salvation (Rom 13:11) anticipates a state where temporal proximity becomes arrival. Rev 7:10 and Rev 19:1 show the completion state as corporate, joyful, doxological, and unbounded — what present salvation's individual, partial assurance anticipates. 2Ti 4:18 names specific content: "the heavenly kingdom." The present experience is thus a real but incomplete anticipation — it possesses the thing (justification, reconciliation, sealing) while awaiting its fullness (complete rescue from wrath, entry into glory, and eternal reigning).
  - _cites:_ Rom 5:9,Eph 1:13,Rom 5:17,Eph 6:17,1Co 1:18,Rom 13:11,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,2Ti 4:18
- **[21465 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows limited but present generational extension. Household salvation (Act 11:14, Act 16:31) indicates that an individual's reception of salvation creates conditions for others in the same relational unit to receive rescue — the salvific reality reaches beyond the individual to those connected by household bond. Paul's endurance in suffering produces salvation for the elect (2Ti 2:10) — one person's sustained inner posture creates eschatological consequence for others not yet saved. The apostle's missionary self-adaptation (1Cor 9:22) and self-denial (1Cor 10:33) generate salvation in others across time as a consequence of one person's inner orientation. However, the evidence does not describe a clearly biological or generational deposit — the extension is relational and missional rather than genealogical. The evidence for strictly generational consequence (parent to child, covenant line to descendant) is limited to Act 2:39 ("the promise is to you and your children") which is not in the evidence block, making this finding partial and noting its boundaries honestly.
  - _cites:_ Act 11:14,Act 16:31,2Ti 2:10,1Co 9:22,1Co 10:33,Act 2:39
- **[21466 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on transformation. T5.1.1 and T5.1.2 document extensive evidence of both condition-change and orientation-change across M38-A-VCG-01 through VCG-10. This prompt is answered by the presence of transformation evidence; no silence to record.
- **[21467 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on sequence. T5.2.1 and T5.2.2 identify five distinguishable stages (before, reception, after-reception, ongoing, completion) drawn from multiple VCGs. No silence to record.
- **[21468 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on mechanism. T5.3.1 and T5.3.2 identify at least five distinct mechanisms (crisis-encounter, word-faith-Spirit sequence, sustained endurance, affective-moral repentance, and divine regeneration) differentiated by context. No silence to record.
- **[21469 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on the relationship to suffering. T5.4.1 and T5.4.2 document suffering as context for endurance (M38-A-VCG-05), as path of mediators (2Ti 2:10, Heb 2:10), as constitutive of Christ's founding of salvation, and as the occasion for testing whether perseverance is genuine. No silence to record.
  - _cites:_ 2Ti 2:10,Heb 2:10
- **[21470 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on formation and sanctification, though the engagement is partial rather than systematic. T5.5.1 and T5.5.2 document relevant evidence in Phili 2:12, 1Ti 4:16, 2Ti 1:9, 2Th 2:13, 2Pe 3:18, and 1Ti 2:15. The evidence shows salvation as the precondition and initiating frame of ongoing formation, though sanctification is not the primary focus of this characteristic's vocabulary.
  - _cites:_ 1Ti 4:16,2Ti 1:9,2Th 2:13,2Pe 3:18,1Ti 2:15
- **[21471 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent on eschatological trajectory; it is among the most extensively documented dimensions of this characteristic. T5.6.1 and T5.6.2 draw on texts across M38-A-VCG-05, VCG-08, VCG-10, and VCG-04. No silence to record.
- **[21472 · gap]** **[CHAR-1]** G — T2.8 findings are not present in this segment's evidence block. The deposit-consequence and generational-extension questions (T5.7.1, T5.7.2) have been answered as far as the current evidence allows: T5.7.1 recorded a gap regarding the T2.8 constitutional deposit specifically; T5.7.2 found partial evidence of relational-missional extension beyond the individual. T5.7 is hereby closed pending T2.8 findings from the appropriate prior segment. No finding can be fabricated from absent data.
- **[21473 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Across the 120-verse evidence base, three adjacent characteristics recur with notable frequency. First, *forgiveness/conscience-cleansing* appears in VCG-02 (Luk 7:50 "faith has saved her… peace"), VCG-03 (Act 2:38 "repentance… forgiveness"), VCG-04 (Luk 1:77 "salvation-knowledge through forgiveness"), and VCG-10 (Jude 23; Rev 12:10 "accuser defeated… conscience cleansed"). Second, *justification/righteousness-received* co-occurs in VCG-03 (Rom 5:16 dōrēma → justification; Rom 5:17 "gift of righteousness"), VCG-02 (Rom 10:10 "heart-belief → justification"), and VCG-10 (Rom 5:9 "justified by his blood"). Third, *repentance/will-turning* appears in VCG-09 (2Cor 7:10; Act 2:38; Act 2:40) and threads through VCG-02 (Act 5:31 "repentance given"), VCG-05, and VCG-06. Peace as an inner-being outcome is a fourth co-occurring element (Luk 7:50; Luk 2:30; Rev 7:10).
  - _cites:_ Luk 7:50,Act 2:38,Luk 1:77,Rev 12:10,Rom 5:16,Rom 5:17,Rom 10:10,Rom 5:9,2Co 7:10,Act 2:40,Act 5:31,Luk 2:30,Rev 7:10
- **[21474 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals that eschatological salvation received by faith occupies a *hub position* in the inner-being landscape: it is the event-state from which other inner-being characteristics radiate as effects or to which they flow as conditions. Forgiveness and conscience-cleansing are its immediate inner-being consequences (the guilty conscience is the terrain salvation transforms). Justification/righteousness-received is its juridical-relational dimension, co-present not prior. Repentance/will-turning is its entry threshold — the inner movement that precedes or accompanies reception. Peace is its affective signature. This means salvation in the evidence base is never a bare transaction: it arrives as a complex inner-being event, reorganising conscience, will, identity, and affection simultaneously. The hub character of salvation also explains why it is the largest sub-group (39%): it is co-present wherever these other inner-being movements are named.
- **[21475 · silent]** **[CHAR-1]** S — Co-occurrence patterns are significant and have been documented in T6.1.1–2. This prompt requires no further independent finding.
- **[21476 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows two consistent sequences. (1) *Antecedent sequence*: hearing/word-received → faith exercised → salvation received. This is most explicit in Eph 1:13 ("heard the gospel of salvation, believed, sealed with the Spirit"), Rom 10:9–10 (heart-belief and mouth-confession → saved), and Act 11:14 (proclaimed word → household saved). (2) *Consequent sequence*: salvation received → peace/joy/praise as outcome. Luk 7:50 (saved → "go in peace"), Rev 7:10 (redeemed → "salvation to our God"), Rev 19:1 (salvation → hallelujah), 2Cor 9:15 (gift → doxology). A third observable sequence is repentance → salvation in VCG-09: 2Cor 7:10 explicitly states "godly grief produces repentance leading to salvation."
  - _cites:_ Eph 1:13,Rom 10:9,Rom 10:10,Act 11:14,Luk 7:50,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,2Co 9:15,2Co 7:10
- **[21477 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The antecedent sequence (word → faith → salvation) is *causal* from the divine side but *receptive* from the human side: God's word and power produce salvation through the instrument of faith, not alongside it. The evidence explicitly excludes human origination (Eph 2:8 "not of yourselves"; Tit 3:5 "not by our righteous deeds"). The consequent sequence (salvation → peace/praise) is *constitutively developmental*: peace and praise are not merely correlated with salvation but are the inner-being states that salvation's reception necessarily generates. The repentance → salvation sequence is more ambiguous — 2Cor 7:10 frames it causally ("produces… leads to"), but Act 5:31 frames repentance as itself a gift of the exalted Savior, collapsing simple prior causation into divine initiative. The overall structure is: divine initiative → word proclaimed → faith as reception → salvation conferred → inner transformation (forgiveness, peace, justification) → doxological outcome.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:8,Tit 3:5,2Co 7:10,Act 5:31
- **[21478 · silent]** **[CHAR-1]** S — Sequential patterns are evidenced. See T6.2.1–2.
- **[21479 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows salvation producing at least three downstream inner-being states. (1) *Peace*: Luk 7:50 — "your faith has saved you; go in peace" — names peace as the immediate inner-being consequence of the saving encounter. (2) *Doxological joy and praise*: Rev 7:10, Rev 19:1, 2Cor 9:15, 2Ti 4:18 — the inner being of the saved erupts in gratitude and ascription of glory. (3) *Assurance and security*: Rom 5:9–10, Heb 7:25 — justification and perpetual intercession produce confident rest in eschatological security. The mechanism in each case is constitutive rather than merely sequential: salvation does not merely cause peace as an external consequence; peace is the *inner-being form* that salvation takes once received by one who was burdened and at enmity (Rom 5:10 "reconciled… enemies"). Similarly, praise is not an optional response but the natural inner-being expression of comprehensive rescue.
  - _cites:_ Luk 7:50,Rev 7:10,Rev 19:1,2Co 9:15,2Ti 4:18,Rom 5:9,Rom 5:10,Heb 7:25
- **[21480 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence names several upstream producers of salvation. (1) *Grace* is the primary originating cause: Eph 2:8 "by grace you have been saved"; Tit 3:5 "saved… by his mercy"; Act 15:11 "saved through grace." Grace is the unconditional divine initiative. (2) *Christ's atoning work* is the ground: Rom 5:9 "justified by his blood… saved from wrath"; Rom 5:10 "reconciled through his death." (3) *The proclaimed word* is the instrumental cause: Act 11:14 ("words by which you will be saved"); 1Cor 1:21 ("God was pleased through the foolishness of what was preached to save those who believe"); Jam 1:21 ("the implanted word which is able to save your soul"). (4) *Faith* is the receptive instrument through which grace reaches the inner being: Eph 2:8 "through faith." These four operate at different levels: grace is the originating cause, Christ's work the meritorious cause, the word the instrumental cause, and faith the receptive condition.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:8,Tit 3:5,Act 15:11,Rom 5:9,Rom 5:10,Act 11:14,1Co 1:21,Jam 1:21
- **[21481 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Two constitutive relationships are evidenced. (1) *Faith constitutes part of salvation's reception mechanism*: Eph 2:8 names faith not as external condition but as the inner-being mode through which salvation is appropriated — "through faith" is constitutive of "saved by grace." Faith is the inner-being form that salvation's reception takes. (2) *Salvation constitutes part of eschatological identity*: Eph 6:17 ("helmet of salvation") and 1Th 5:9 ("appointed to obtain salvation") indicate that salvation is a constitutive dimension of the believer's eschatological self-understanding — it is not merely something that happened but something one *is* now clothed with and defined by. (3) *Repentance and calling are constituent moments within salvation's inner-being event*: Act 2:38, 2Cor 7:10, and Act 2:40 show that repentance is not merely prior to salvation but is constitutively part of the inner-being turning that salvation involves.
  - _cites:_ Eph 2:8,Eph 6:17,1Th 5:9,Act 2:38,2Co 7:10,Act 2:40
- **[21482 · silent]** **[CHAR-1]** S — Causal and constitutive relationships are richly evidenced. See T6.3.1–3.
- **[21483 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Four vocabulary-sharing zones are visible in the evidence. (1) *Faith (pistis/pisteuō)*: the term appears throughout VCG-02 and VCG-04 as the reception-instrument of salvation, but faith as a characteristic in its own right (trust, belief, reliance) would constitute a distinct characteristic sharing this vocabulary root. (2) *Righteousness/justification (dikaiosynē/dikaioō)*: Rom 5:16–17 (dōrēma → justification), Rom 10:10 (heart-belief → justification), 2Pe 1:1 (righteousness of the Savior) — righteousness-receipt and justification share vocabulary with the salvation register. (3) *Repentance (metanoia)*: Act 5:31, Act 2:38, 2Cor 7:10 share the repentance vocabulary with any characteristic addressing inner turning. (4) *Peace (eirēnē)*: Luk 7:50 uses the peace-formula as salvation's inner-being outcome, linking to any characteristic treating peace as an inner state. (5) *Gift (dōrea/dōrēma/charis)*: VCG-03 shares this vocabulary with any characteristic treating grace-gift as inner-being reception.
  - _cites:_ Rom 5:16,Rom 5:17,Rom 10:10,2Pe 1:1,Act 5:31,Act 2:38,2Co 7:10,Luk 7:50
- **[21484 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The sōzō root (G4982) generates the entire primary vocabulary family of this characteristic: sōzō (verb, "save/rescue"), sōtēria (noun, "salvation"), sōtēr (agent noun, "savior"), sōtērion (adjectival noun, "saving/salvation"), and sōtērios (adjective). This single root generates all four grammatical forms present in the evidence and could in principle generate terms across adjacent characteristics treating physical healing (sōzō is used for healing at Luk 8:48 — "your faith has made you well," same word-form as "saved you" at Luk 7:50), preservation (Mat 24:22), and eschatological completion. The dōrea/dōrōn/dōrēma family from didōmi ("give") similarly generates vocabulary across characteristics treating gift-reception, Spirit-reception, and grace. The pistis/pisteuō root generates terms across faith, trust, and belief characteristics.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:48,Luk 7:50,Mat 24:22
- **[21485 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — Three conceptual relationships are disclosed. (1) The sōzō root's ambiguity between physical rescue and eschatological salvation (Luk 7:50 vs. Mat 8:25 "save us, we are perishing") reveals that the physical and eschatological registers share a common root conception: rescue from a state of danger by a more powerful agent. This means eschatological salvation is not an alien import but the root category's deepest extension. (2) The overlap of sōzō and pistis vocabulary across healing and eschatological salvation (e.g., "your faith has saved/healed you" in Luk 7:50 and 8:48) reveals that faith-as-reception is the same inner-being act whether the object is physical restoration or eschatological rescue — the act's quality does not differ by object. (3) The dōrea vocabulary shared between salvation-as-gift (Eph 2:8) and Spirit-as-gift (Act 2:38) reveals that salvation and Spirit-reception are not two separate characteristics but two aspects of a single gifted event — the Spirit is the inner-being arrival of salvation as gift.
  - _cites:_ Luk 7:50,Mat 8:25,Eph 2:8,Act 2:38
- **[21486 · silent]** **[CHAR-1]** S — Significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced. See T6.4.1–3.
- **[21487 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The closest adjacent characteristic is *justification/righteousness-received* (the forensic declaration of the sinner as righteous before God, grounded in Christ's atoning work). The resemblance is strong: both involve reception by faith, both are grounded in Christ's work, both reverse the inner-being state produced by sin, and both appear in the same verse clusters (Rom 5:9–10, 5:16–17; Rom 10:9–10; 2Pe 1:1). The precise distinction is this: *salvation* (sōzō/sōtēria) names the comprehensive rescue event — the transfer from danger to safety, from perishing to being kept — while *justification* (dikaioō/dikaiosynē) names the specific forensic standing produced by that rescue: the guilty party declared righteous. Salvation is the wider category; justification is one of its constitutive inner-being dimensions. Rom 5:9 makes the distinction explicit: "having been justified by his blood, we shall be saved from wrath" — justification is the basis, salvation the outcome.
  - _cites:_ Rom 5:5,Rom 5:9,Rom 5:10,Rom 10:9,Rom 10:10,2Pe 1:1
- **[21488 · finding]** **[CHAR-1]** E — The overlap between salvation and justification is densest in Rom 5:15–17 (VCG-03), where dōrea produces both justification (v.16) and life-reigning (v.17), and in Rom 10:9–10, where heart-belief produces justification while mouth-confession produces salvation. The precise boundary at Rom 10:9–10 is: *justification* is the inner-being verdict (heart), while *salvation* is the full outer-inner event of rescue including the public confession dimension (mouth). Justification is the forensic dimension of salvation's inner-being content; salvation is the broader rescue event that includes but exceeds the forensic. Similarly, the boundary between salvation and *repentance* (2Cor 7:10 "repentance leading to salvation") is that repentance is the inner-being turning movement while salvation is the state of rescue entered through that turning.
