In the panic-cry register, no eschatological anticipation is present in the verse evidence itself — the rescued Peter and disciples are rescued for continued temporal existence, not explicitly oriented toward eschatological fullness. The eschatological anticipation is present in the Hebrews/Lukan registers only.
  - _cites:_ Heb 11:7,Heb 11:13,Heb 11:39,Heb 11:40,Heb 11:10,Luk 1:71
- **[21087 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes, MB-VCG-04 provides the clearest evidence of generational consequence. Heb 11:7 explicitly records that Noah's faith-driven rescue produced household deliverance — his rescue extends to those entrusted to his care, carrying the consequence of his faith beyond his own person to the next generation. The ark saves his family; the consequence of his faith-act is corporate and generational, not confined to the individual. Luk 1:71 (MB-VCG-04) announces rescue 'from our enemies' for a whole people — the soteria is corporate and carries forward through the covenant community, not limited to a single individual's experience. These two verses are the strongest evidence of generational deposit in the corpus. In the panic-cry register (MB-VCG-01) and the Son's anguished-petition register (MB-VCG-03), the evidence is individual rather than generational within the verse set itself. The generational/corporate consequence is present in the corpus but concentrated in MB-VCG-04.
  - _cites:_ Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71
- **[21088 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not silent on transformation; findings are provided in T5.1.1 and T5.1.2 above. Transformation (both condition-change and orientation-change) is attested across MB-VCG-01, MB-VCG-03, and MB-VCG-04. No silence note is required.
- **[21089 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not silent on sequence; the before-during-after sequence is well-attested in MB-VCG-01 and MB-VCG-03, with partial data in MB-VCG-04 and MB-VCG-05. Findings are provided in T5.2.1 and T5.2.2. No silence note is required.
- **[21090 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not silent on mechanism; three distinct mechanisms are identified in T5.3.1 and T5.3.2 above (sudden-encounter rescue, gradual-formation through obedient faith, and anguished-petition-and-surrender). No silence note is required.
- **[21091 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not silent on suffering; mortal affliction is the constitutive context of the entire characteristic, and the relationship to suffering is addressed in T5.4.1 and T5.4.2 above. No silence note is required.
- **[21092 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not entirely silent, but formation data is sparse and concentrated in the Hebrews evidence (Heb 5:7, Heb 11:7). The dominant registers of this sub-group (panic-cry, crucifixion-irony) yield little or no formation data. The honest summary: formation is present but peripheral in this corpus; the sub-group's primary phenomenological focus is event-rescue rather than character formation. See T5.5.1 and T5.5.2 for the available evidence.
  - _cites:_ Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7
- **[21093 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not entirely silent, but eschatological content is deliberately bounded out of this sub-group by the BOUNDARY resolution (M38-B distinct from M38-A by absence of eschatological content). What eschatological horizon exists is present only at the literary edges of the Hebrews and Lukan evidence (Heb 11:7 within the faith-hall narrative; Luk 1:71 within the Benedictus). The panic-cry, crucifixion-irony, and shipwreck-despair registers are genuinely silent on eschatological trajectory. The partial eschatological indicators are noted in T5.6.1 and T5.6.2; their peripherality reflects the sub-group's intentional definitional boundary.
  - _cites:_ Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71
- **[21094 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — T2.8 data has not been supplied to this analytical segment. If T2.8 found no constitutional deposit for M38-B Characteristic 2, T5.7 is hereby closed: no deposit-consequence or generational-consequence analysis can proceed beyond the verse-level observations already recorded in T5.7.1 and T5.7.2. The verse-level evidence (Heb 11:7: household deliverance and righteousness-imputation; Luk 1:71: corporate rescue) suggests that deposit and generational consequence exist in the corpus, but without T2.8 confirmation these remain unanchored observations. T5.7 is provisionally closed pending T2.8 input.
  - _cites:_ Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71
- **[21095 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Within the 15 verses of M38-B, the most consistently co-occurring inner-being features are: (1) **fear/terror** — present across VCG-01 (Mat 8:25 'terror and crying out in urgent, desperate faith'; Mat 14:30 'panic and desperate faith-cry') and VCG-03 (Heb 5:7 'anguished cries'); (2) **faith exercised under duress** — the keyword 'faith exercised' or 'faith cry' appears in Mat 8:25, Mat 14:30, Heb 11:7; (3) **surrender/will-yielding** — present in VCG-02 (Mat 27:40, Mar 15:30–31, Luk 23:35,37) and VCG-03 (Joh 12:27, Heb 5:7); (4) **despair/hope-collapse** — uniquely isolated in VCG-05 (Act 27:20 'all hope of physical rescue was abandoned'). The most frequent adjacent characteristic pair is **fear → faith-cry**, appearing in three of the five VCGs. **Irony/mockery** clusters densely in VCG-02 (seven verses) and does not appear elsewhere, making it a distinct internal adjacency zone. **Obedient reverence** co-occurs in VCG-04 (Heb 11:7, Luk 1:71) as a corporate register.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7,Mat 27:40,Mar 15:30,Mar 15:31,Luk 23:35,Luk 23:37,Joh 12:27,Act 27:20,Luk 1:71
- **[21096 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals that M38-B occupies a **liminal crisis zone** in the inner-being landscape: it is the characteristic most tightly coupled to acute bodily threat and the accompanying phenomenological cascade. Fear does not stand alone here; it almost always triggers either a faith-cry (VCG-01: Mat 8:25, Mat 14:30; VCG-03: Heb 5:7) or collapse into despair (VCG-05: Act 27:20). This bifurcation — cry versus silence — reveals that M38-B contains two poles of creaturely response to mortal threat. The irony-mockery cluster (VCG-02) adds a third co-occurrence register: the **absent self-rescue** motif, where physical rescue is taunted rather than sought. Together these patterns show that M38-B is not a simple emotion but a **dynamic inner-being field** in which terror, faith, surrender, despair, and irony each emerge depending on the subject-position of the endangered self.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Heb 5:7,Act 27:20
- **[21097 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Significant co-occurrence patterns do emerge across all five VCGs; this prompt therefore requires no silence note. The dominant patterns are: terror + faith-cry (VCG-01, VCG-03), mockery + withheld self-rescue (VCG-02), reverent-obedient faith + corporate deliverance (VCG-04), and despair + hope-extinction (VCG-05). No VCG is co-occurrence-empty.
- **[21098 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — A consistent sequence is visible in VCG-01: **mortal threat → terror → faith-cry → (hoped-for) rescue**. Mat 14:30 narrates this most explicitly — Peter moves from bold venture (walking on water) to fear (wind and waves), to panic, to cry ('Lord, save me'), to rescue by Jesus's hand. Mat 8:25 compresses the same arc: disciples in the storm → 'we perish' cry → Jesus rebukes the storm. Psa 69:18 assumes the same trajectory: distress → plea for divine proximity → ransoming. In VCG-03 (Joh 12:27; Heb 5:7) the sequence is **anguish → petition → heard by the Father → willed surrender** — rescue denied physically but received spiritually. VCG-05 (Act 27:20) represents the truncated sequence: threat → despair (cry never made or abandoned), with no rescue following in the narrative moment.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mat 8:25,Psa 69:18,Joh 12:27,Heb 5:7,Act 27:20
- **[21099 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The dominant sequence (mortal threat → terror → faith-cry → rescue) is **causal in structure but conditional in outcome**: the mortal threat causally produces terror; the terror precipitates the faith-cry; but the rescue is contingent on the divine response, not guaranteed by the cry alone. This is most evident in the contrast between VCG-01 (rescue received: Mat 14:30) and VCG-05 (rescue hoped-for but abandoned: Act 27:20). The VCG-03 sequence (Joh 12:27, Heb 5:7) shows a **developmental** register: anguish is heard and transformed into reverent surrender rather than physical rescue. VCG-02 represents a **correlational irony**: the mockers' taunts correlate with the reality of withheld self-rescue without the cry-sequence being present at all. The dominant sequence is therefore best described as **causal but open-ended**, with multiple possible terminations depending on the subject and the divine will.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Act 27:20,Joh 12:27,Heb 5:7
- **[21100 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Sequential patterns are evidenced across VCG-01, VCG-03, and VCG-05; no silence note is required. The only VCG in which an explicit sequence is absent is VCG-02 (crucifixion mockery), where the taunts presuppose a sequence but do not enact one — the sequence is **implied** by the ironic logic ('he saved others; he cannot save himself') rather than narrated.
- **[21101 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — In VCG-04, M38-B (physical rescue through faith-action) **produces** a state of household safety and corporate vindication (Heb 11:7: Noah's faith-driven obedience 'saved his household from physical destruction'; Luk 1:71: soteria as 'rescue from enemies'). The mechanism is **sustained obedient faith producing embodied action** — not a panic-cry but a prolonged faith-exercise resulting in deliverance. In VCG-01 and VCG-03, the faith-cry produces **rescue** as an outcome (Mat 14:30: Peter rescued; Heb 5:7: prayer heard). The characteristic therefore produces **deliverance/safety** as its primary downstream state, and in VCG-03 it also produces **reverential surrender** — the anguish being transformed rather than removed. M38-B thus generates both physical safety (VCG-01, VCG-04) and an inner-being transformation from panic to reverence (VCG-03).
  - _cites:_ Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71,Mat 14:30,Heb 5:7
- **[21102 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — M38-B is precipitated by **mortal threat** (the antecedent condition in all five VCGs). In VCG-01, the storm and waves produce the terror-and-cry state. In VCG-03, the 'appointed hour' of suffering produces Jesus's anguished petition (Joh 12:27: 'my soul is troubled'). In VCG-05, the storm's severity extinguishes hope (Act 27:20). The producing factor is therefore an **external physical or political danger** that overwhelms ordinary creaturely capacity and forces the inner being into a crisis-response mode. Whether that crisis-response becomes a faith-cry, a willed surrender, or despair depends on secondary factors (the presence or absence of faith, the relational availability of a rescuing presence). M38-B is thus **produced by danger** and **shaped by faith-capacity**.
  - _cites:_ Joh 12:27,Act 27:20
- **[21103 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — M38-B contains as a **constituent sub-element** the act of **reverent petition** (Heb 5:7: 'prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears'), which also appears as a constituent of worship, prayer, and lament characteristics across the programme. The **faith-cry** element (Mat 14:30, Mat 8:25) is a constitutive form of **faith exercised** that also belongs to broader faith characteristics. Conversely, **mortal despair** (Act 27:20) is a constitutive element of M38-B that is not reducible to generic hopelessness — it is specifically hope-for-physical-rescue abandoned. M38-B is therefore a **composite characteristic** whose elements (fear, faith-cry, surrender, despair, irony) are each capable of appearing as constituents in other characteristics, while the specific conjunction of all five within the context of physical mortal threat is unique to this sub-group.
  - _cites:_ Heb 5:7,Mat 14:30,Mat 8:25,Act 27:20
- **[21104 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced across multiple VCGs; no silence note is required. The evidence demonstrates both upward causation (mortal threat → M38-B) and downward production (M38-B faith-cry → rescue; M38-B obedient faith → household safety).
- **[21105 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — M38-B's primary vocabulary — **sōzō** (G4982), **soteria** (G4991), **padah** (H6299) — is shared with M38-A (eschatological salvation) by direct lexical overlap: the same Greek and Hebrew roots appear across both sub-groups, which is the basis of the researcher-directed BOUNDARY resolution separating them as homonymic/polysemic instances. Within M38-B itself, the keywords 'faith exercised,' 'endangered,' 'rescue physical,' 'fear,' and 'cry urgent' overlap with vocabulary clusters in faith characteristics (faith under trial), lament characteristics (desperate cry, soul distressed), and suffering/endurance characteristics (appointed suffering, will surrendered). The term **padah** (Psa 69:18: 'ransom/redemption') is shared with redemption characteristics. The term **soteria** in Heb 11:7 and Luk 1:71 overlaps with both physical deliverance and eschatological salvation contexts.
  - _cites:_ Psa 69:18,Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71
- **[21106 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The **yasha'/sōzō** root-cluster is the architecturally central shared root: Hebrew **yasha'** (to save, deliver) generates **yeshuah** (salvation/deliverance), which feeds into the LXX rendering **sōzō/soteria**. This root generates vocabulary used across M38-A (eschatological salvation), M38-B (physical rescue), and presumably other M38 sub-groups. The **padah** root (H6299: to ransom, redeem by payment or power) at Psa 69:18 is shared with redemption characteristics. The **anguish/prayer** root cluster in Heb 5:7 (Greek **deēsis** — supplication; **iketerias** — suppliant cries) is shared with intercessory prayer and lament characteristics. The root-level architecture thus reveals that M38-B shares its deepest lexical infrastructure with both **salvation** and **lament/prayer** characteristic families.
  - _cites:_ Psa 69:18,Heb 5:7
- **[21107 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The vocabulary sharing between M38-B and M38-A at the sōzō/soteria root level reveals that these two characteristics are **differentiated expressions of a single semantic field** rather than unrelated phenomena — they share the same root but differ in the referential domain (physical vs. eschatological rescue). The sharing with lament/prayer characteristics (padah at Psa 69:18; deēsis/iketerias at Heb 5:7) reveals that M38-B is conceptually embedded within a **broader petition-and-response** pattern that includes not only rescue-seeking but the whole inner-being posture of crying out in need. The vocabulary overlap with faith characteristics (faith exercised: Heb 11:7; Mat 14:30) reveals M38-B's dependence on **faith as the relational medium** through which physical rescue is sought and received.
  - _cites:_ Psa 69:18,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7,Mat 14:30
- **[21108 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced; no silence note is required. The sōzō/soteria/padah vocabulary complex connects M38-B to multiple programme characteristics at both root and semantic range levels.
- **[21109 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The closest adjacent characteristic is **M38-A** (eschatological salvation, the companion sub-group in cluster M38). Both share the primary lexical terms sōzō and soteria, and both concern inner-being responses to a rescue enacted by God. The precise distinction, per researcher direction (BOUNDARY resolution on sōzō homonymic_polysemic), is: **M38-A's rescue is eschatological** — it concerns the ultimate salvation of the soul from sin, judgement, and death with eternal-life content — whereas **M38-B's rescue is physical and temporal** — it concerns rescue from drowning (Mat 14:30), storm (Mat 8:25, Act 27:20), crucifixion (VCG-02), appointed suffering (VCG-03), and hostile enemies (Luk 1:71). M38-A's inner-being content centres on faith, repentance, and new-creation; M38-B's inner-being content centres on terror, panic-cry, and mortal despair. The **absence of eschatological content** is the definitional boundary.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mat 8:25,Act 27:20,Luk 1:71
- **[21110 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The apparent overlap is most acute at: (1) **Heb 5:7** (VCG-03 anchor), where Jesus is 'saved from death' — this could be read as both physical rescue (from premature death in Gethsemane) and eschatological (the resurrection as the Father's answer to the prayer). The boundary is maintained by the Pass A meaning: 'Jesus prayed with anguished cries to be saved from death and was heard' — the petition is for physical deliverance from the immediate suffering/death moment, not eschatological salvation of the soul. (2) **Luk 1:71** (VCG-04), where soteria might carry eschatological resonance in its Benedictus context. The boundary is maintained by the Pass A meaning: 'rescue from enemies and those who hate — soteria names deliverance from hostile threat,' keeping it in the political-physical domain. The precise boundary is therefore: **referential domain of the rescue** (physical/temporal vs. eschatological/eternal) rather than the vocabulary used.
  - _cites:_ Heb 5:7,Luk 1:71
- **[21111 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The distinction between M38-B and M38-A is **one of kind** (not merely degree). The two characteristics involve different referential objects (temporal body vs. eternal soul), different temporal horizons (present moment of mortal threat vs. eschatological consummation), and different inner-being phenomena (panic-terror-cry vs. repentance-faith-assurance). A difference of degree would mean M38-B is simply a less intense version of M38-A; this is not the case — they are structurally different in what is being rescued, by what means, and with what inner-being accompaniments. The distinction is also **not directional** (one does not flow into the other in the verse evidence, though the same root serves both). It is best described as a **kind-level homonymic distinction**: the same root-word operates in two distinct conceptual registers.
- **[21112 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Significant distinction work is required and has been performed in the preceding three prompts; no silence note is appropriate here.
- **[21113 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — The evidence block identifies five anchors within M38-B: Mat 14:30 (VCG-01), Mar 15:31 (VCG-02), Heb 5:7 (VCG-03), Heb 11:7 (VCG-04), and Act 27:20 (VCG-05). Whether any of these function as **primary anchors** in another characteristic's study within the broader programme cannot be confirmed from the evidence block alone. Heb 5:7 is a strong candidate for shared anchoring in a suffering/endurance or reverent-prayer characteristic (its content — anguished petition, loud cries, heard by the Father — is evidently multi-characteristic). Heb 11:7 is a candidate for shared anchoring in a faith-and-obedience characteristic (it is a paradigmatic Hebrews 11 faith example). Mat 14:30 is a candidate for a faith-tested-under-trial characteristic. However, without cross-characteristic anchor registry data, this finding is classified G (gap).
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mar 15:31,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7,Act 27:20
- **[21114 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — Without confirmed cross-characteristic anchor registry data, the content of what shared anchors would reveal cannot be stated empirically. Based on the verse content, however: if Heb 5:7 is shared with a suffering/reverence characteristic, it would reveal that **M38-B and that characteristic intersect at the point of anguished petition that transforms into reverence** — showing M38-B as a crisis-entry point that can precipitate a deeper disposition-level characteristic. If Heb 11:7 is shared with a faith-obedience characteristic, it would reveal M38-B as the **context** (mortal threat requiring rescue) within which faith-obedience finds its most demanding expression. This finding is classified G pending cross-characteristic registry confirmation.
  - _cites:_ Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7
- **[21115 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — Shared verse anchors cannot be confirmed or denied from available evidence. The absence of cross-characteristic registry data means this cannot be resolved as S (genuinely silent) — the data is needed but unavailable. Classified G.
- **[21116 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — Confirmed analytical dimensions for M38-B from the evidence block include: (1) terror/fear in mortal threat; (2) urgent faith-cry; (3) physical rescue; (4) willed surrender/anguished petition; (5) despair/hope-extinction; (6) irony of withheld self-rescue; (7) corporate/household deliverance through obedient faith. Whether each of these dimensions is shared with another characteristic in the programme requires cross-characteristic dimensional mapping data not available in the evidence block. This is classified G.
- **[21117 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — Without cross-characteristic dimensional mapping, the pattern cannot be empirically stated. Based on the verse evidence, the dimension of **urgent faith-cry** (Mat 8:25, Mat 14:30) is structurally shared with lament and prayer characteristics; the dimension of **obedient faith producing household safety** (Heb 11:7) is structurally shared with faith-obedience characteristics; the dimension of **willed surrender in anguish** (Heb 5:7, Joh 12:27) is structurally shared with suffering/endurance and reverence characteristics. If confirmed, these sharings would reveal M38-B as a **convergence characteristic** — a crisis-register in which multiple other inner-being characteristics are activated simultaneously. Classified G pending dimensional mapping confirmation.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Heb 11:7,Heb 5:7,Joh 12:27
- **[21118 · gap]** **[CHAR-2]** G — Cross-characteristic dimensional mapping data is not available in the evidence block for M38-B. The analytical dimensions can be identified from the verse evidence (see T6.7.1), but their sharing with other characteristics in the programme cannot be confirmed from available data. This gap is noted explicitly.
- **[21119 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The primary terms are:

**Greek: sōzō (G4982)** — verb, from the root *sao-/sōs-* meaning 'safe, whole, unharmed.' The root denotes **preservation of life and bodily integrity**. In M38-B, sōzō consistently means to rescue from physical peril: Mat 8:25 ('save us, we perish'), Mat 14:30 ('Lord, save me'), and the crucifixion taunts (VCG-02). Root meaning reveals: rescue as **restoration to wholeness** from a state of threatened dissolution.

**Greek: soteria (G4991)** — noun, same root as sōzō, meaning 'safety, preservation, deliverance.' Luk 1:71 ('rescue from enemies') and Heb 11:7 ('saved his household') use this form, emphasising **the state or event of being made safe** rather than the act.

**Hebrew: padah (H6299)** — verb, meaning 'to ransom, redeem, deliver by paying a price or exercising superior power.' Psa 69:18 uses padah in the context of divine rescue from enemies. Root meaning reveals: rescue is **transactional or power-assertive** — a deliberate act of intervention that breaks the threat's hold. This distinguishes padah from the Greek sōzō, which stresses the resultant condition of safety rather than the mechanism of extraction.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Luk 1:71,Heb 11:7,Psa 69:18
- **[21120 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — **sōzō** operates primarily as a **verb** across M38-B's evidence (Mat 8:25: aorist imperative 'save [us]'; Mat 14:30: aorist imperative 'save me'; Mat 27:40, 49: aorist imperative/infinitive; Mar 15:30–31: aorist infinitive; Luk 23:35–39: aorist infinitive; Joh 12:27: aorist subjunctive; Heb 5:7: aorist infinitive). The dominance of the **imperative and infinitive forms** reveals that M38-B is primarily an **action-seeking** characteristic: the inner being does not contemplate rescue abstractly but demands, pleads, or contemplates it as an urgent act to be performed. The imperatives (Mat 14:30: 'save me!'; Mat 8:25: 'save us!') are the purest register of the terror-faith-cry phenomenon.

**soteria** (Luk 1:71, Heb 11:7) as a **noun** reveals the **resultant-state** register: rescue that has been accomplished or is anticipated as a corporate possession, not a moment of crisis-cry.

**padah** (Psa 69:18) as a **verb** (imperative: 'redeem/ransom [my soul]') confirms the action-demanding grammar of M38-B's inner-being posture.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Mat 27:40,Mat 27:49,Mar 15:30,Mar 15:31,Luk 23:35,Luk 23:36,Luk 23:37,Luk 23:38,Luk 23:39,Joh 12:27,Heb 5:7,Luk 1:71,Heb 11:7,Psa 69:18
- **[21121 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — **sōzō** in the NT operates across a wide semantic range: (1) **physical rescue** from drowning (Mat 14:30), storm (Mat 8:25), crucifixion (VCG-02 taunts); (2) **healing** from illness (not represented in M38-B but documented in programme); (3) **eschatological salvation** from sin and judgement (M38-A); (4) **preservation from suffering** (Joh 12:27, Heb 5:7). Within M38-B, sōzō is restricted to meanings (1) and (4). The breadth of the full sōzō range is precisely what necessitates the researcher-directed BOUNDARY resolution separating M38-A and M38-B — the same word serves radically different inner-being phenomena at different points in its semantic arc.

**padah** (H6299) operates across: (1) ransom of individuals from slavery or death; (2) divine redemptive intervention for Israel; (3) personal deliverance from enemies (Psa 69:18). Within M38-B, padah is used at meaning (3), the most personal and crisis-oriented pole of its range.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mat 8:25,Joh 12:27,Heb 5:7,Psa 69:18
- **[21122 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The vocabulary distinguishes:

**Act vs. resultant condition**: sōzō (verb: the act of rescuing) vs. soteria (noun: the condition of being saved/safe). Mat 14:30 uses the verb imperatively — the act is demanded; Heb 11:7 and Luk 1:71 use the noun — the condition is achieved or promised.

**Sought vs. received**: The imperative forms (Mat 14:30, Mat 8:25, Psa 69:18) express **rescue sought**; the narrative outcomes (Mat 14:31 — Jesus reaches out his hand) express **rescue received** (though the receiving is outside the 15-verse corpus proper, the cry-and-response structure implies it).

**Individual vs. corporate**: sōzō in VCG-01 is individual crisis-cry (Peter, the disciples); soteria in VCG-04 (Luk 1:71, Heb 11:7) is corporate/household deliverance.

**Withheld vs. enacted**: VCG-02 introduces a **withheld rescue** dimension — sōzō as the rescue Jesus refuses to perform for himself, contrasting with enacted rescue for others. No single term names this withholding, but the ironic deployment of the same verb ('he saved others; he cannot save himself') performs the distinction.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71,Mat 8:25,Psa 69:18,Mat 14:31
- **[21123 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The vocabulary of M38-B includes several terms pointing to the **absence or failure of rescue**:

**apollymi** (G622: to perish, be destroyed) — implied in Mat 8:25 ('we perish/are being destroyed') and Mat 14:30 (Peter 'beginning to sink'). This term is the functional antonym of sōzō in its physical rescue sense: sōzō is rescue from perishing; apollymi is perishing that rescue has not yet interrupted.

**Hope abandoned (Act 27:20)**: The phrase 'all hope of being saved was abandoned' uses sōzō in the passive with negation — **the absence of sōzō as a present reality** is explicitly stated, making the abandonment of hope the lexical marker of rescue-absent.

**padah** implies its opposite in the condition of **bondage or entrapment** that the ransom must break — though no specific antonym term is named in Psa 69:18 itself.

No single dedicated antonym term for M38-B exists in the vocabulary; the absence of rescue is expressed through perishing-language and negated hope rather than a technical antonym.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Act 27:20,Psa 69:18
- **[21124 · silent]** **[CHAR-2]** S — The vocabulary of M38-B does not include a dedicated person-type term (e.g. 'the rescued one,' 'the deliverer' as a habitual disposition) within the 15 verses. The vocabulary is action-oriented and event-specific rather than characterological. No term for 'one who regularly enacts or receives physical rescue as a defining identity' is evidenced in this corpus.
- **[21125 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The vocabulary contains explicit supplication terms:

**krazō / boaō** (to cry out urgently) — implied in the imperative forms at Mat 14:30 and Mat 8:25; these are **urgent cries** directed to Jesus as rescuer.

**deēsis** (G1162: supplication, entreaty) and **hiketēria** (G2428: suppliant olive-branch; hence 'suppliant cry') — Heb 5:7 explicitly names Jesus's prayer as 'supplications with loud cries and tears' (deēseōn te kai hiketēriōn). These terms name the **act of seeking rescue** as a posture of total creaturely need.

**padah** in Psa 69:18 is itself the supplication term — the imperative 'ransom/redeem me' is the act of seeking rescue from a position of helplessness.

The vocabulary thus includes both implied and explicit supplication terms, confirming that **seeking rescue** (the act of crying out in mortal need) is a first-order lexical element of M38-B.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mat 8:25,Heb 5:7,Psa 69:18
- **[21126 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The single Hebrew text in M38-B is Psa 69:18 (padah). The translation of **padah** into the LXX as **lytroo/lytrousthai** (to ransom/redeem) and the NT's use of **sōzō** for the same rescue-from-mortal-threat domain reveals:

**Continuity**: Both testaments place the human being in a position of mortal helplessness calling for divine intervention. The cry-structure of Psa 69:18 ('draw near to my soul and redeem it') is the direct formal predecessor of Mat 14:30 ('Lord, save me'). The inner-being posture — distress, plea, divine proximity expected — is identical across testaments.

**Development**: The NT term sōzō encompasses more registers (physical rescue, healing, eschatological salvation) than padah, which is more narrowly concerned with ransom/extraction. The NT's crisis-cry is also more Christologically specific — it is addressed to Jesus as the present rescuer (Mat 14:30; Heb 5:7), whereas Psa 69:18 addresses Yahweh as the absent presence to be drawn near. The development is toward **greater Christological intimacy** in the rescue dynamic.
  - _cites:_ Psa 69:18,Mat 14:30,Heb 5:7
- **[21127 · silent]** **[CHAR-2]** S — No term in M38-B's vocabulary appears to be a NT neologism. Both sōzō and soteria are well-attested in Classical and Hellenistic Greek prior to the NT. Padah is a Hebrew term with deep OT roots. The term hiketēria (Heb 5:7) is rare in NT usage but is a classical Greek word (suppliant branches), not a NT coinage. No new vocabulary is coined for this characteristic in the NT; it employs existing vocabulary with extended or concentrated application. This is noted as silent (S) on the question of NT-coined terms.
  - _cites:_ Heb 5:7
- **[21128 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The full vocabulary arc of M38-B — padah (H6299), sōzō (G4982), soteria (G4991), apollymi (implied antonym), deēsis, hiketēria, krazō/boaō (implied supplication terms) — reveals a characteristic whose complete semantic range spans:

(1) **The act of urgent crisis-cry** (imperative sōzō, padah imperative, deēsis/hiketēria): the inner being reduced to a single urgent plea;
(2) **The physical danger being fled** (apollymi, 'perishing,' 'sinking'): the threat that precipitates the cry;
(3) **The rescue enacted or withheld** (sōzō indicative/narrative; ironic sōzō in VCG-02): the divine or creaturely response;
(4) **The resultant state of safety** (soteria, Heb 11:7, Luk 1:71): the condition of having been delivered;
(5) **The collapse of rescue-hope** (Act 27:20, sōzō negated): the inner-being state when rescue is not forthcoming.

The arc reveals M38-B as a **complete phenomenological cycle** from threat to cry to rescue-or-despair, with vocabulary covering every stage. The characteristic's semantic range is therefore not a single state but a **dynamic arc** — and the vocabulary is precisely calibrated to each node within it.
  - _cites:_ Heb 11:7,Luk 1:71,Act 27:20
- **[21129 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The primary anchor for the sub-group's hallmark phenomenon is **Mat 14:30** (VCG-01 anchor): 'Lord, save me' (Kyrie, sōson me). **sōzō** here functions as the **main verb in an imperative clause** addressed to Jesus. The sentence is a two-word direct address and command — the entire inner-being content of the verse is compressed into this imperative. Grammatically, sōzō carries the full weight of Peter's desperate faith: it is not modified by any object clause explaining what rescue would mean; the cry is naked and total. In the argument of Mat 14, this imperative functions as the **turning point**: it marks the transition from Peter's sinking (the failure of self-sufficient faith) to Jesus's hand-reach (the enactment of rescue), demonstrating that the faith-cry — however small — is sufficient to precipitate divine response. The term's role is therefore **syntactically minimal but argumentatively pivotal**.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30
- **[21130 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — M38-B's evidence spans four literary forms:

**Gospel narrative** (Mat 8:25, Mat 14:30, VCG-02 crucifixion passages, Joh 12:27, Luk 1:71, Luk 23:35–39): requires interpretation that attends to narrative arc, character development, and the theological irony constructed by the evangelist. VCG-02's mockery-irony is an **evangelist-constructed irony** that requires literary sensitivity to how sōzō functions differently in the mouths of mockers versus petitioners.

**Epistle/homily** (Heb 5:7, Heb 11:7): requires interpretation that attends to the rhetorical argument being constructed — Heb 5:7 is part of a high-priestly Christology argument; Heb 11:7 is a faith-catalogue argument. The inner-being content must be read in light of the author's rhetorical purpose, not just the verse in isolation.

**Narrative (Acts)** (Act 27:20): requires attention to Paul's voyage narrative and the dramatic function of despair as a prelude to the divine announcement (Act 27:23–24).

**Psalm** (Psa 69:18): requires genre-specific interpretation of lament form — the plea is the formal heart of a communal/individual lament, and padah is the climactic petition of a structured prayer.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Joh 12:27,Luk 1:71,Luk 23:35,Luk 23:36,Luk 23:37,Luk 23:38,Luk 23:39,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7,Act 27:20,Act 27:23,Act 27:24,Psa 69:18
- **[21131 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Several logical structures are identifiable:

**VCG-01 (Mat 14:30)**: P1: Peter is sinking and cannot save himself. P2: Jesus is present and has demonstrated power over wind and waves. C: Therefore, 'Lord, save me' is the only rational act available to faith. The argument is an **inference to the only available agent** under conditions of mortal incapacity.

**VCG-02 (Mar 15:31)**: P1: Jesus saved others (prior acts of physical rescue/healing). P2: He does not come down from the cross to save himself. C (mockers' intended): Therefore he is powerless. C (evangelist's irony): Therefore his refusal is the mechanism of others' deeper salvation. The argument is a **two-level ironic syllogism**.

**VCG-03 (Heb 5:7)**: P1: Jesus prayed with anguish and reverence to be saved from death. P2: He was heard because of his reverence. C: Therefore reverent anguish is the condition under which petition is heard, not the content of rescue. The argument is a **theology of heard prayer** under conditions of ordained suffering.

**VCG-04 (Heb 11:7)**: P1: Noah acted in reverent faith by building the ark. P2: This action delivered his household. C: Therefore faith expressed in obedient action produces physical rescue. The argument is **faith-as-instrument-of-salvation**.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mar 15:31,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7
- **[21132 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The contextual settings of M38-B are:

**Miraculous narrative setting** (Mat 8:25, Mat 14:30, Joh 12:27): The lake, the storm, the Gethsemane anticipation — these are settings of **immediate physical extremity** in which ordinary human agency is exhausted. This setting reveals that M38-B is a **threshold experience**: the inner being has moved to the outer edge of what it can do and must either cry out or perish.

**Execution/crucifixion setting** (VCG-02): The mockery at Golgotha is simultaneously a **political violence** and a **ritual execution** setting. This reveals that M38-B extends beyond natural disaster into state-inflicted mortal threat — the characteristic's scope includes political violence as a form of physical danger.

**Liturgical/prayer setting** (Psa 69:18, Heb 5:7): The lament psalm and the Hebrews high-priestly passage situate rescue-cry within **structured prayer** — the physical danger is met not only with panic but with formal, reverent supplication. This reveals that M38-B can be ritually and theologically ordered even when emotionally raw.

**Faith-retrospect setting** (Heb 11:7): The Hebrews 11 hall-of-faith is a **didactic retrospective** — Noah's rescue is cited as evidence for the faith-obedience argument. This reveals M38-B's role as a **paradigm for faith instruction**.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Joh 12:27,Psa 69:18,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7
- **[21133 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. **Mat 14:30** (VCG-01 anchor, vc_id=9025) is designated the anchor for the characteristic's hallmark phenomenon: 'Peter, sinking in wind and waves, cries "Lord, save me" — physical rescue from drowning, with the inner being moving from bold venture to panic and desperate faith-cry in an instant.' This verse most fully expresses M38-B's essential character because it contains all the hallmark elements in a single, compressed narrative moment: (1) physical mortal threat (sinking); (2) terror/panic ('seeing the wind, he was afraid'); (3) the urgent faith-cry ('Lord, save me!'); (4) the rescue enacted (Jesus stretches out his hand). No other verse contains all four elements in this narrative density. The secondary anchors (Heb 5:7, Heb 11:7, Mar 15:31, Act 27:20) each express one or two dimensions of the characteristic in depth but not all four simultaneously.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7,Mar 15:31,Act 27:20
- **[21134 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — **Mat 14:30** uniquely reveals the **instantaneous inner-being collapse** from bold venture into panic-cry. No other verse in the corpus shows a subject who was previously in a position of active, successful faith (walking on water) and then, within moments, moved to total creaturely helplessness. This trajectory — bold faith → perceived threat → terror → naked plea → rescue — captures the **full arc of M38-B in a single person within a single narrative moment**. Other verses show segments of this arc: Act 27:20 shows the despair-terminus; Heb 5:7 shows the anguish-petition; Heb 11:7 shows the sustained-obedience register; Mat 8:25 shows the collective panic-cry. But only Mat 14:30 shows **the entire arc embodied in one subject** — making it uniquely revelatory of the characteristic's essential dynamic: the creaturely self's movement from self-sufficiency to total dependence on the rescuing presence, enacted in a single moment of mortal threat.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Act 27:20,Heb 5:7,Heb 11:7,Mat 8:25
- **[21135 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The most useful human science framework for M38-B is **crisis psychology / terror management theory**, supplemented by **attachment theory**. Crisis psychology (particularly the work on acute stress response and crisis intervention) directly addresses the inner-being states documented in M38-B: acute fear, panic, the collapse of agency, and the urgent appeal to an available helper. The phenomenological content of Mat 8:25, Mat 14:30, and Act 27:20 maps closely onto the documented psychological trajectory of acute mortal threat: threat appraisal → autonomic arousal (fear/panic) → agency collapse → crisis-cry or despair. **Attachment theory** (Bowlby, Ainsworth) further illuminates the crisis-cry dynamic: the infant/adult under threat instinctively orients toward an attachment figure (the 'safe haven' function). Peter's 'Lord, save me' (Mat 14:30) and the disciples' 'Save us' (Mat 8:25) are formally identical to attachment-system activation — the self in mortal danger reaches toward a trusted, proximate, powerful other.
  - _cites:_ Mat 8:25,Mat 14:30,Act 27:20
- **[21136 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — **Attachment theory** most directly illuminates why the faith-cry in M38-B takes the imperative grammatical form of direct address rather than a conditional or reflective prayer. Bowlby's attachment system activates not reasoned supplication but **proximity-seeking behaviour** — the cry to the attachment figure is instinctive, non-reflective, and immediate. This explains the grammatical minimalism of Mat 14:30 ('Lord, save me!' — two words) and Mat 8:25 ('Save us, we perish!' — four words): these are not theological propositions but **attachment cries** — the raw inner-being output of a self whose survival instinct has activated its deepest relational resource. The attachment framework also illuminates the contrast between VCG-01 (attachment cry → rescue) and VCG-05 (Act 27:20: hope abandoned — no attachment figure perceived as available). When the attachment figure is perceived as absent or inaccessible, the crisis-cry is not made; despair is the result of attachment-figure unavailability.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:30,Mat 8:25,Act 27:20
- **[21137 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — The most significant divergence between the human science frameworks and the verse evidence appears at **VCG-03** (Joh 12:27, Heb 5:7). Crisis psychology and attachment theory would predict that the subject under mortal threat either cries out or despairs. But Jesus in Joh 12:27 and Heb 5:7 undergoes a **third trajectory unavailable in the human science frameworks**: anguish → petition → heard → willed surrender to the appointed purpose. This is not attachment-seeking resolved by rescue (VCG-01) nor attachment-seeking abandoned in despair (VCG-05) — it is anguish **transformed by the relational knowledge that the Father's will is good**, producing a consent to the threat rather than escape from it. The divergence reveals that M38-B, at its highest register (VCG-03), transcends the psychology of crisis: the inner being of the Son demonstrates a **tertium quid** — a way through mortal threat that is neither flight (rescue) nor collapse (despair) but willed participation in the threat's redemptive purpose. Human science frameworks have no category for this.
  - _cites:_ Joh 12:27,Heb 5:7
- **[21138 · finding]** **[CHAR-2]** E — Crisis psychology surfaces the phenomenon of **post-rescue psychological processing**: after acute mortal threat and rescue, human subjects typically undergo a phase of integration, meaning-making, and relational re-evaluation. The verse evidence of M38-B does not document this post-rescue state — it ends at the moment of rescue (Mat 14:31) or at the despair point (Act 27:20) without attending to what follows in the inner being. Whether this post-rescue integration is documented elsewhere in the programme (perhaps in testimony, doxology, or gratitude characteristics) cannot be confirmed from M38-B's evidence alone. This does represent a **potential gap** in M38-B's evidence base: the characteristic documents the crisis arc (threat → cry → rescue) but not the aftermath arc (rescue → integration → transformed disposition). Whether further verse investigation within M38-B or adjacent characteristics would address this is classified as a gap requiring researcher attention.
  - _cites:_ Mat 14:31,Act 27:20

## char 3 — Healing wholeness through faith exercised (189 findings)

- **[20572 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence consistently positions God — specifically Jesus as the agent of sōzō — as one whose healing power responds to faith exercised from within the human person. In Mat 9:22 and Mar 5:34, Jesus names faith as the operative cause: "your faith has made you well," revealing that God's saving-healing character is not capricious or arbitrarily selective but structurally responsive to interior trust. In Luk 8:50, Jesus commands "only believe" as the direct counter to mortal fear, indicating that God's character includes a disposition to save precisely at the boundary of death when faith is the presenting inner posture. Jam 5:15 (M38-C-VCG-03) reveals a further dimension: God is the one who, when addressed by the prayer of faith, heals body and forgives sin in a single integrated act, showing that the divine character holds physical and spiritual restoration together rather than separating them. 1Pe 3:21 deepens this: God is one who receives the inner appeal of conscience and answers it through resurrection power. Taken together, the evidence images a God whose nature is integrative wholeness — one who does not partition body from spirit in the act of rescue, and whose responsive character is activated by interior faith-movement in the creature.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20573 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence positions faith-exercised-toward-wholeness as the inner-being faculty that enables the human person to receive the full, integrated rescue God offers. The hemorrhaging woman's interior conviction in Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 — forming before any outward act is complete — suggests that this faculty is the point of contact between human need and divine power: it equips the person to reach toward God at the place where bodily and spiritual need converge. Bartimaeus in Mar 10:52 and the blind man in Luk 18:42 illustrate that this characteristic also equips the person for discipleship — Bartimaeus "followed him on the way" after receiving sight, indicating that wholeness-through-faith does not end at physical cure but opens the person toward continued relational orientation to Christ. The Samaritan leper's return in Luk 17:19 further shows that the characteristic equips the person for grateful recognition of the giver of wholeness. In sum, the created purpose evidenced is: this characteristic equips the human person to move from fragmentation (disease, blindness, death-threat) through faith-exercise toward integrated personhood that is bodily, spiritual, and relationally directed toward God.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 17:19
- **[20574 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The divine image dimension evidenced here is the capacity for responsive trust — the ability of the inner being to form an interior conviction directed toward God as the source of wholeness. The women in Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 exhibit a pre-verbal, pre-contact interior speech — "if I touch his garment I will be made well" — which is an act of the person's image-bearing rationality and will oriented toward the divine. This is not animal instinct but structured interior faith: purposive, directional, and theologically oriented. The image-bearer expression is therefore the relational-receptive capacity: the human person, unlike other creatures, can form interior trust oriented toward God and, by that trust, become the site where divine saving power acts. 1Pe 3:21's "appeal to God for a good conscience" (M38-C-VCG-03) sharpens this further — conscience as image-bearing faculty is the organ capable of seeking right standing before God, and it does so through interior appeal rather than external rite. The characteristic thus instantiates the relational and volitional aspects of the image: the capacity to turn inwardly toward God in trust, which is itself a creaturely reflection of the covenantal relationality that belongs to God's nature.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,1Pe 3:21
- **[20575 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Typological deployment is partially evidenced. The most explicit christological extension is Luk 17:19 where the Samaritan leper — a double outsider, racially and religiously — is the one whose faith is declared to have made him whole, while the nine Jewish lepers do not return. The implicit typological register here is that faith-exercised-wholeness transcends ethnic covenant membership and anticipates the gospel's extension to gentile inclusion. Mar 10:52 carries a further christological-eschatological register: Bartimaeus's title "Son of David" (the cry that occasions the healing) situates the faith-wholeness event within messianic expectation, so that the individual healing participates in the arrival of the kingdom. Jam 5:15 (M38-C-VCG-03) extends the topology ecclesiologically: the prayer of faith within the community of elders names a corporate faith-practice that images the church as the ongoing site of integrated rescue, pointing toward eschatological wholeness. 1Pe 3:21 is the most structurally typological: baptism is explicitly read against the backdrop of Noah's rescue through water, and conscience-appeal through resurrection is positioned as the eschatological fulfilment of that earlier rescue. The direction of typology is examined in T0.4.2.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:19,Mar 10:52,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20576 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence does not explicitly attribute faith-exercised-as-inner-faculty to God (faith in the Pauline sense of God's faithfulness is a separate register not present in this sub-group's corpus). However, the evidence reveals an important asymmetry: faith is consistently located in the human person as the activating faculty, while God — through Jesus — is the one who names, confirms, and responds to it. Jesus's declarations in Mat 9:22, Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48, Luk 17:19, Luk 18:42, and Mar 10:52 all have the same structure: the human person exercises faith inwardly, and Jesus recognises and names it as the operative cause of sōzō. This asymmetry is itself revelatory: what it discloses is that God in his nature is responsive to faith — not as a constraint on divine freedom but as a covenantal disposition to honour the interior trust that the image-bearer directs toward him. The significance for the human person is that this characteristic is precisely that which aligns the creature with the responsive structure of God's saving character: by exercising faith, the human person enters into the relational dynamic that God's covenantal nature has prepared.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Luk 18:42,Mar 10:52
- **[20577 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence does not address the pre-fall state directly, but the internal logic of the evidence suggests a dual register. The conditions that occasion the faith-exercises in M38-C are uniformly conditions of bodily breakdown: haemorrhage (Mat 9:21; Mar 5:28), blindness (Mar 10:52; Luk 18:42), leprosy (Luk 17:19), death-threat (Mar 5:23; Luk 8:50), and sickness requiring communal intercession (Jam 5:15). These are all markers of the fallen condition — mortality, decay, disease — suggesting that the operative expression of this characteristic is a response to the fallen condition. However, the structural capacity beneath the expression — the capacity to form interior trust directed toward God as source of wholeness — appears to belong to the created design as image-bearer rationality and will. The characteristic is therefore best described as both: the deep faculty is created-design (the relational-volitional capacity for interior faith directed toward God), while its particular form as faith exercised in the face of bodily dissolution is a response to fallenness. Jam 5:15 and 1Pe 3:21 (M38-C-VCG-03) reinforce the fallen-response register by pairing healing with forgiveness and conscience-cleansing, indicating that sin's effects on the inner person are also part of what this characteristic addresses.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 17:19,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20578 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence suggests this characteristic is exclusively a creaturely analogue rather than a shared property. As noted in obs_id=225 (T0.1.2), God is not the subject of faith-exercised in this corpus; he is the object and respondent. The structure is consistently: human person → exercises faith inwardly → receives sōzō from God. There is no verse in the M38-C corpus where God or Jesus is described as exercising faith in the same mode. What the divine side of the relationship does exhibit — and what this characteristic analogises — is responsive reliability: God's character as one who honours interior trust. The creaturely analogue relationship therefore runs thus: God's faithful covenantal responsiveness is the archetype; the human person's capacity to exercise faith and receive integrated wholeness is the creaturely image-bearing reflection of that covenantal structure. 1Pe 3:21's "appeal to God for a good conscience" most clearly maps this asymmetry — the human person appeals, and God through resurrection answers. The characteristic is thus exclusively creaturely in its operational form while being relationally structured toward the divine.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21
- **[20579 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Where typological use is evidenced (see T0.4.1), the direction is consistently from divine pattern to human instance, not the reverse. 1Pe 3:21 is the clearest case: Noah's rescue through water (divine pattern established in Genesis) becomes the type of which baptism-as-conscience-appeal is the antitype — the divine salvific act precedes and establishes the human participatory form. In the Synoptic healing narratives, Jesus's declarations ("your faith has made you well") function as divine naming that establishes what faith-exercised-toward-wholeness is and does — the human instances of the woman, Bartimaeus, and the Samaritan leper are not archetypes for subsequent humans to imitate in a typological sense but rather instances within the divine pattern of kingdom-wholeness that Jesus is enacting. The christological direction in Mar 10:52 (Son of David healing the blind on the road to Jerusalem) places the human faith-exercise within the unfolding messianic pattern established by Old Testament prophetic anticipation of restored sight as eschatological sign (cf. Isa 35:5 — outside this corpus but setting the background). The typological direction is therefore divine → human throughout: God's pattern of integrated rescue, established in covenant and announced in prophecy, becomes the framework within which individual faith-exercised-wholeness events participate and point forward.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Mar 10:52,Isa 35:5
- **[20580 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus is silent on God exercising faith as an inner faculty (see T0.1.2 and T0.3.2). This silence is structurally significant rather than merely negative. It suggests that faith-exercised is precisely a creaturely faculty — one that belongs to the image-bearer as image-bearer, not to God as the original. The divine image in the human person is not a simple copy of God's properties but includes capacities that arise from the creature's position as dependent being-in-relation-to-God. Faith-exercised is one such capacity: it only makes sense for a being who is not self-sufficient, who must reach toward another for wholeness. God possesses wholeness inherently; the image-bearer is constituted to reach for it. The silence therefore locates this characteristic squarely within the creatureliness dimension of the imago Dei — it is not a reflection of a divine attribute in the sense that love or justice are, but rather a distinctively creaturely form of the relational structure that the image is designed to sustain. Its place in the divine image is as the inner-being faculty by which the creature maintains its constitutive orientation toward God as source — an orientation that God himself does not need because he is the source.
- **[20581 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — There is directional evidence toward future fullness, though it is not the dominant register of the corpus. Mar 10:52 provides the clearest indicator: Bartimaeus, upon receiving sight through faith, "followed him on the way" — the Markan "way" (hodos) language in the journey to Jerusalem carries eschatological weight, positioning the healed disciple as one now moving toward the fullness of kingdom reality. Luk 17:19's Samaritan leper, commended for his return and faith, receives the declaration that he is "made whole" — the sōzō term itself carries eschatological freight beyond the immediate physical cure. 1Pe 3:21 most explicitly orients the characteristic toward future fullness: the conscience-appeal through baptism is grounded in resurrection ("through the resurrection of Jesus Christ"), which is itself the firstfruits of eschatological wholeness. The characteristic thus carries a future-orientation in two registers: (a) discipleship-direction — faith exercised opens the person into following Christ toward eschatological completion (Mar 10:52); (b) resurrection-grounded hope — the wholeness received now participates in and anticipates the resurrection wholeness that is coming (1Pe 3:21). The present experience of sōzō is real but not final; it is oriented toward a completeness not yet fully possessed.
  - _cites:_ Mar 10:52,Luk 17:19,1Pe 3:21
- **[20582 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus provides oblique evidence on this question through contrast. In Mar 5:23 and Luk 8:50, Jesus explicitly counters fear with the command to believe — the structural implication is that fear (as the refusal or incapacity to exercise faith) is the presenting inner state that displaces this characteristic. In Luk 17:17-19, the nine lepers who do not return implicitly fail to bring their faith-exercise to completion in grateful recognition, while the Samaritan who does return receives the explicit declaration of wholeness through faith. These contrasts suggest that the absence of this characteristic — specifically the failure to form and exercise interior trust directed toward God for wholeness — indicates an inner-being fragmentation: the person remains oriented toward their own resources, or toward fear, rather than toward God. This is a condition-of-image finding: the presence of faith-exercised-toward-wholeness marks a person whose image-bearing relational faculty is functioning — turned toward God, receiving integration from him. Its absence marks a person whose inner-being orientation is fragmented, self-directed, or fear-governed, which is precisely the condition that the fallen image exhibits. The image is not destroyed in absence, but its relational-receptive function is blocked.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Luk 17:17,Luk 17:18,Luk 17:19
- **[20583 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Typological use is evidenced; this prompt is therefore not a silence-note. See T0.4.1 and T0.4.2 for documentation. The evidenced typological uses are: (a) 1Pe 3:21 — Noahic rescue → baptism-as-conscience-appeal through resurrection (explicit typology in text); (b) Mar 10:52 — messianic/Davidic healing within the kingdom-arrival pattern; (c) Luk 17:19 — gentile-inclusive faith anticipating gospel extension; (d) Jam 5:15 — ecclesial intercessory faith as site of ongoing integrated rescue pointing toward eschatological wholeness. All instances show the divine-to-human typological direction.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Mar 10:52,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20584 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Healing wholeness through faith exercised." The name signals several structural commitments simultaneously. "Healing" locates the primary presenting register: the inner being is in a condition requiring restoration, not merely improvement. "Wholeness" signals that the outcome is integrated and total — not partial cure but a convergence of physical and spiritual restoration, which is the sōzō semantic field's distinctive contribution. "Through" indicates instrumentality and directional movement: the characteristic is not a state received passively but a result achieved by means of something. "Faith exercised" is the instrument named — not faith as disposition only, but faith in active, operative mode; the participle "exercised" marks intentional, directional inner-being action. The name as a whole therefore signals: this is an active inner-being phenomenon (not passive), its activity is faith directed toward an object (God/Christ), and its goal is the bridging of physical and spiritual need in a single integrated rescue event. The word "exercised" is the name's most decisive signal — it distinguishes this characteristic from faith as settled doctrinal assent and positions it as a dynamic, moment-bound inner act that produces ontological change.
- **[20585 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence positions this characteristic primarily as an act — a specific, directional inner-being movement — rather than a stable disposition or quality. The woman's pre-touch interior monologue in Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 ("if I touch his garment I will be made well") is a dateable inner event with a before and after, not a background disposition. Bartimaeus's persistent cry in Mar 10:52, the father's plea in Mar 5:23, and the Samaritan's return in Luk 17:19 all exhibit discrete, directional acts. However, the characteristic is not pure momentary act either — the woman presses through a crowd, suggesting sustained inner-being commitment over time. Jam 5:15 extends this to communal intercessory practice, which implies a more sustained mode. The most accurate classification is: faith exercised is an act with dispositional depth — a moment-bound inner-being movement that is possible because of an underlying trust-orientation, but which is itself distinct from that underlying orientation. It is the actualization of faith in a specific movement toward God for specific wholeness. In programme vocabulary, it is best described as an inner-being act arising from and expressing a deeper dispositional trust.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Mar 5:23,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20586 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus directly names the structural opposite: fear. In Luk 8:50, Jesus sets "do not fear" against "only believe" in an explicit antithetical parallelism — the inner being must displace fear in order to exercise faith toward wholeness. Fear is therefore not merely an accompanying affect but the structural opposite that this characteristic must overcome or displace. The father's situation in Mar 5:23 (urgent, death-facing plea) also operates in the fear-faith opposition: the urgency of his plea names the mortal dread that faith-exercised moves against. A secondary structural opposite is evidenced in the nine lepers of Luk 17:17-19: their failure to return suggests that self-sufficient continuation — taking healing without returning in faith — is also an opposing inner posture, though this is less explicitly marked than fear. The primary structural opposite is therefore fear (phobos) as the inner-being reality of self-protective withdrawal in the face of mortal threat, which directly excludes the outward movement of trust toward God that this characteristic requires.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:50,Mar 5:23,Luk 17:17,Luk 17:18,Luk 17:19
- **[20587 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence reveals at least three distinct modes of operation. (1) Interior conviction mode (M38-C-VCG-01): the characteristic operates as pre-verbal, pre-contact inner monologue or formed conviction — the woman in Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 forms the conviction inwardly before touching, and this interior act is itself causally efficacious; Jesus confirms it was operative ("your faith has made you well"). (2) Crisis-displacement mode (M38-C-VCG-02): the characteristic operates as active counter-movement against mortal fear — in Luk 8:50 and Mar 5:23, faith is not quiet conviction but urgent, desperate exercise that displaces fear as the governing inner state; the faith-act is precipitated by extreme threat and operates through intensity rather than composure. (3) Intercessory/conscience mode (M38-C-VCG-03): the characteristic operates through the conscience as primary organ (1Pe 3:21 — "appeal to God for a good conscience") and through intercessory faith on behalf of another (Jam 5:15 — "the prayer of faith will save the sick"). In this mode, the characteristic's operation is not self-directed but other-directed, and the restoration is explicitly conscience-level rather than somatic only. These three modes are genuinely distinct in their presenting dynamics, though all three share the structural feature of faith as the inner-being activating movement toward God.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Luk 8:50,Mar 5:23,1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15
- **[20588 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus evidences two distinct immediate responses depending on the mode of encounter. (1) Where faith has been exercised and sōzō received, the immediate inner response is peace: Mar 5:34 and Luk 8:48 both close with Jesus granting the woman "peace" (eirēnē) — this is the direct inner-being marker of what the person first experiences upon receiving healing-wholeness through faith. Peace here functions not as a general well-being but as the specific inner-being state that attends the settling of the faith-crisis: the striving of faith has reached its object, and the inner being rests in that reception. (2) In the blindness-healing cases (Mar 10:52; Luk 18:42), the immediate response is discipleship-orientation — "followed him on the way" — suggesting that in the sight-restoration mode, the first inner response is not peace-as-rest but forward-movement toward Christ. (3) In the conscience-appeal mode (1Pe 3:21), the immediate inner response is not named explicitly but the structure implies conscience-cleared: the appeal to God is answered by resurrection, and the good conscience is the immediate inner result. Across all three modes, the immediate response involves an inner-being shift from the state that preceded the faith-exercise (fear, darkness, troubled conscience) to a new integrated state (peace, sight/following, clear conscience).
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,1Pe 3:21
- **[20589 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence for sustained effect is present but sparse, concentrated in two instances. (1) Mar 10:52 — Bartimaeus's sustained effect is discipleship: "he followed him on the way." The "on the way" language in Mark implies not a momentary following but ongoing relational orientation toward Jesus on the road to Jerusalem and beyond. This suggests that the sustained inner-being effect of receiving wholeness through faith is the establishment of a new directional orientation — the person is now a follower, moving with Christ. (2) 1Pe 3:21 — the sustained effect of baptism-as-conscience-appeal is a good conscience maintained before God "through the resurrection of Jesus Christ." The conscience is not merely cleansed once but sustained in right standing by virtue of the resurrection ground on which the appeal is made. The sustained effect is therefore ongoing conscience-integrity. (3) Luk 17:19 implies that grateful return to the giver of wholeness is the sustained posture, though this is an inference from the single act. Taken together, the sustained effects evidenced are: re-oriented discipleship (following Christ), conscience-integrity maintained before God, and gratitude as an ongoing orientation. These are inner-being changes at the level of constitution, not merely momentary affective states.
  - _cites:_ Mar 10:52,1Pe 3:21,Luk 17:19
- **[20590 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence names several enabling inner conditions explicitly. (1) Interior conviction formed and held: Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 show the woman forming an explicit interior conviction ("if I touch his garment I will be made well") before any outward completion — the faith must be formed inwardly first; diffuse or unformed desire is not evidenced as sufficient. (2) Persistent directional intent under pressure: the woman presses through a crowd; Bartimaeus calls repeatedly despite rebuke (Mar 10:48) — the inner condition is not passive waiting but active, sustained directional movement toward Jesus despite obstacles. (3) Fear displaced rather than indulged: Luk 8:50 positions faith as the active inner replacement for fear — the enabling condition is the willingness to counter fear with belief. (4) Gratitude and relational recognition: Luk 17:19 suggests that complete reception involves returning to acknowledge the source — the Samaritan's return is marked as the faith-act that distinguishes whole-making sōzō from mere physical cure. (5) Conscience-openness to appeal: 1Pe 3:21 requires that the inner being be in a posture of appeal to God rather than self-sufficiency — the conscience must acknowledge need and turn toward God for right standing.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:48,Luk 8:50,Luk 17:19,1Pe 3:21
- **[20591 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic is volitional-faith: the characteristic operates as a specific act of the will directed by trust toward God. The evidence for this classification is concentrated in M38-C-VCG-01 and VCG-02: the woman's interior monologue (Mat 9:21; Mar 5:28), Jesus's repeated formula "your faith has made you well" (Mat 9:22; Mar 5:34; Luk 8:48; Luk 17:19; Luk 18:42; Mar 10:52), and the command "only believe" against fear (Luk 8:50) all locate the primary faculty in an act of the will shaped by trust. Faith as exercised here is not primarily cognitive (doctrinal assent), not primarily affective (emotional response), but volitional-trust: a directed inner act of reliance. Secondary dimensions are present — conscience (1Pe 3:21), relational-hope (Mat 9:21's expectation of wholeness), and somatic-spiritual bridging (Jam 5:15) — but these function as correlates or outcomes of the volitional-faith act rather than the primary dimension. The characteristic's classification is therefore volitional-faith as primary, with conscience and relational-hope as secondary dimensions.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Luk 18:42,Mar 10:52,Luk 8:50,1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15
- **[20592 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic's primary Greek term is sōzō (G4982), with the inner-faculty term being pistis/pisteuō (faith/believe) as the instrument named by Jesus in the healing declarations. At the definitional level, sōzō carries a semantic range that refuses separation of physical and spiritual rescue — it can denote rescue from physical danger, healing of bodily disease, and soteriological salvation, with these senses intersecting rather than being mutually exclusive. The programme's researcher direction explicitly notes a "sōzō sense-split" — this sub-group is held at the intersection where healing and saving are inseparable, which is sōzō's own semantic structure. Pistis/pisteuō at the definitional level denotes trust, reliance, and commitment directed toward an object — it is inherently relational and directional, presupposing both a truster and a trusted. The combination of sōzō and pistis in the standard Synoptic formula ("your faith has sōzō'd you") therefore produces at the definitional level: trust-directed-toward-God/Christ as the inner act that releases integrated rescue (physical-plus-spiritual). The Hebrew background of shālōm (peace, wholeness, completeness) — evoked by Jesus's "go in peace" in Mar 5:34 and Luk 8:48 — adds the dimension that the result of sōzō-through-pistis is wholeness as restored-right-order, not merely absence of disease.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48
- **[20593 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic is composite at first encounter, not simple. Three constituent elements are immediately visible in the verse evidence. (1) An inner conviction or trust-forming act: the woman's interior monologue (Mat 9:21; Mar 5:28) shows that faith must be formed as a specific directed conviction before it becomes operative — this is an epistemic-volitional element. (2) An outward expression or movement: touching the garment, crying out persistently (Bartimaeus, Mar 10:52), returning to give thanks (Luk 17:19), praying for the sick (Jam 5:15) — the inner conviction must pass through some expression or directional act; faith exercised is not purely interior but moves outward. (3) A reception and confirmation event: Jesus's naming of faith as the cause ("your faith has made you well") and the bestowing of peace or sight or cleansed conscience — the characteristic is only complete when the inner act has reached its object and been received as sōzō. These three elements — formation, expression, reception — are distinguishable but not separable in the evidence. A fourth element is present in M38-C-VCG-03: intercessory extension, where faith operates not only for the self but on behalf of another (Jam 5:15). The characteristic is therefore structurally composite with at least three to four constituent elements.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20594 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes or resists the following, drawn from direct verse evidence. (1) Fear as governing inner state: Luk 8:50 sets "do not fear" as the direct prerequisite for "only believe" — faith exercised resists fear's governance of the inner being. (2) External-only or ritual-only cleansing: 1Pe 3:21 explicitly states that sōzō through baptism is not the removal of dirt from the body but an appeal of conscience — the characteristic resists reduction to external rite; the inner faculty is the real locus. (3) Gratitude-deferred or ungrateful reception: the nine lepers in Luk 17:17-19 (implied contrast with v.19) suggest that receiving physical cure without the returning faith-exercise of acknowledgment is excluded from full sōzō — the characteristic resists being dissolved into bare physical benefit without relational response. (4) Self-sufficiency as alternative to faith: the act of forming interior conviction directed toward Christ as the source of wholeness excludes self-reliance as the operative inner posture — one cannot simultaneously rely on oneself and exercise faith toward Christ for the same wholeness. These exclusions collectively define the boundary: the characteristic ends where fear, external-only ritual, ungrateful passivity, or self-sufficiency begin.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:50,1Pe 3:21,Luk 17:17,Luk 17:18,Luk 17:19
- **[20595 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, the evidence supports contextual and directional variation in operation, as distinct from the three structural modes identified in T1.4.1. (1) Self-directed vs. other-directed: In M38-C-VCG-01 and VCG-02, faith is exercised by the healing-seeker for their own wholeness or by a parent for their child (Mar 5:23). In M38-C-VCG-03 (Jam 5:15), faith operates intercessorily — the elders' prayer of faith operates on behalf of a sick person who may themselves be passive. The inner-being engagement differs: self-directed faith involves the person's own volitional-trust act; intercessory faith involves the inner-being of a third party acting as conduit. (2) Crisis-intensity vs. composure-level: VCG-02's crisis-displacement mode (Luk 8:50; Mar 5:23) operates at higher intensity than VCG-01's quiet interior conviction mode (Mat 9:21; Mar 5:28). The constitutional level at which faith engages the inner being appears to shift with urgency — both are genuine faith-exercises but with different phenomenological registers. (3) Bodily-healing register vs. conscience-register: The Synoptic healing narratives operate primarily in the somatic register that carries spiritual overtone, while 1Pe 3:21 operates primarily in the conscience register with the somatic (baptism) as outward correlate. The characteristic therefore differentiates in operation across these contextual-constitutional dimensions while retaining the same structural core.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:23,Jam 5:15,Luk 8:50,Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,1Pe 3:21
- **[20596 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The immediate response varies across the verse evidence, and the variation is structurally meaningful rather than arbitrary. (1) Peace (eirēnē): Mar 5:34 and Luk 8:48 both close with Jesus pronouncing peace — in the haemorrhage narrative, peace is the named immediate inner-being state following reception of healing-through-faith. (2) Sight and discipleship-movement: Mar 10:52 and Luk 18:42 close with physical restoration (sight received immediately) and then forward movement (Bartimaeus "followed him on the way"). The immediate response here is not peace-as-rest but sight-enabled following — a directional movement rather than a settled state. (3) Conscience-cleared: 1Pe 3:21 implies that the immediate result of the conscience-appeal is a good conscience established before God — the conscience's inner register shifts from troubled to clear. (4) Gratitude-return: Luk 17:19 positions the Samaritan's return as the faith-act that precedes the declaration of wholeness, so the immediate response is the completion of the faith-act in grateful recognition. The responses are consistent in one respect — each names an inner-being shift from a prior fragmented or distressed state to an integrated one — but the specific form of the shift varies with the mode of operation and the dimension primarily engaged (somatic-spiritual, volitional-directional, or conscience-level).
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,1Pe 3:21,Luk 17:19
- **[20597 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence for sustained states is limited but specific. (1) Reoriented discipleship: Mar 10:52 establishes that the sustained state following faith-exercised-wholeness is a following-orientation toward Christ — "on the way" in Markan theology implies ongoing journeying with Jesus toward Jerusalem and beyond; the capacity established is continued relational-volitional alignment with Christ. (2) Conscience-integrity: 1Pe 3:21's "good conscience" maintained "through the resurrection of Jesus Christ" implies that the sustained quality is conscience-integrity — an ongoing inner-being state of right standing before God, not merely momentary clearance. This is a quality of the inner person's relationship to God that becomes their new constitution. (3) Gratitude as dispositional orientation: Luk 17:19 suggests that the wholeness received through faith generates a sustained grateful orientation toward the giver — the Samaritan's return is both an immediate act and an indicator of a dispositional posture. (4) Intercessory capacity: Jam 5:15 within a community-practice frame suggests that sustained faith-exercise develops intercessory capacity — the characteristic, when practiced in the VCG-03 mode, establishes the person/community as ongoing sites of faith-activated rescue for others. These sustained effects establish the person at the level of inner constitution rather than episodic experience.
  - _cites:_ Mar 10:52,1Pe 3:21,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20598 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The verse evidence names or implies several blocking conditions. (1) Fear as governing state: Luk 8:50's structure — "do not fear; only believe" — positions fear as the primary blocking condition. When fear governs the inner being, faith-exercise cannot proceed because fear is the opposite directional movement (inward self-protection rather than outward trust-extension toward God).
