(2) Failure to bring faith to completion in grateful recognition: Luk 17:17-19 implies that the nine lepers who received physical cure but did not return failed to receive full sōzō-wholeness. The blocking condition here is the inner posture of taking benefit without relational acknowledgment — a form of inner-being self-sufficiency that receives the physical without opening to the spiritual register. (3) Reliance on external rite as substitute for inner appeal: 1Pe 3:21 explicitly blocks the distortion of treating baptism as physical cleansing rather than conscience-appeal — the blocking/distorting condition is ritualism that does not engage the conscience's inner faculty. (4) Crowd-pressure or social discouragement: Bartimaeus is rebuked and told to be silent (Mar 10:48) — while he persists, the implicit blocking condition is social pressure to suppress the faith-cry; those who capitulate to such pressure would not exercise the faith that activates sōzō. These blocking conditions identify the inner-being vulnerabilities that prevent reception.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:50,Luk 17:17,Luk 17:18,Luk 17:19,1Pe 3:21,Mar 10:48
- **[20599 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The classification of this characteristic's primary dimension as volitional-faith (established in T1.8.1) is supported by the following specific evidence. (1) The standard Synoptic formula "your faith has made you well" (Mat 9:22; Mar 5:34; Luk 8:48; Luk 17:19; Luk 18:42; Mar 10:52) directly attributes the operative cause to an inner-being faculty — faith — that is consistently located in the person exercising it. The formula names faith as the instrument, not emotion, not knowledge, not bodily action alone. (2) The interior monologue in Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 shows faith operating as a formed volitional act — "I will be made well" — which is a directional willing, not merely feeling or thinking. (3) The command "only believe" in Luk 8:50 positions believing as a volitional act that can be commanded — emotions cannot be commanded directly; will can. This supports volitional primacy. (4) 1Pe 3:21's "appeal to God for a good conscience" identifies the operative inner faculty as conscience-mediated volitional petition — the word "appeal" (eperōtēma) carries the sense of a formal directed request, which is an act of the will. (5) Jam 5:15's "prayer of faith" similarly identifies the operative mode as volitional-directed-trust expressed through prayer. All evidence converges on volitional-faith as the governing dimension.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Luk 18:42,Mar 10:52,Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Luk 8:50,1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15
- **[20600 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The name "Healing wholeness through faith exercised" carries all three implications noted in the prompt. (1) Directional: "through" establishes a vector — the characteristic moves from a starting condition (brokenness, disease, fear, troubled conscience) through faith as the mechanism toward wholeness. The name is not static but describes a trajectory with an originating point, a mediating act, and a destination. (2) Relational: "faith exercised" is inherently relational — faith has an object; it is directed toward someone. Though the name does not name the object (Christ), the verse evidence makes clear that this faith is always exercised toward Jesus as the source of sōzō. The name's faith-exercised language presupposes a truster-trusted relationship. (3) Constitutional: "wholeness" — not "cure" or "recovery" — signals that the result affects the person's constitution, not merely their somatic condition. Wholeness is an integrated ontological state. "Healing" paired with "wholeness" prevents reduction to merely spiritual salvation (the characteristic does include physical cure) while "wholeness" prevents reduction to merely physical cure. The name therefore orients the enquiry from the outset toward a trajectory that is directional (movement through faith), relational (faith toward an object-person), and constitutional (the result is integrated personhood), all three dimensions being necessary for a correct understanding of what the characteristic is and does.
- **[20601 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Best working description, drawn from the cumulative verse evidence: This characteristic is a composite inner-being act — constitutionally located at the intersection of will, trust, and conscience — by which a person in a condition of physical or spiritual fragmentation forms an explicit, directed interior conviction of reliance upon Christ for integrated wholeness, and through that inner act receives a sōzō event that bridges bodily healing and spiritual restoration. The faculties primarily engaged are: (a) the volitional faculty (the act of believing/trusting is an act of the will directed toward Christ); (b) the cognitive-formative faculty (an interior conviction is formed, sometimes as explicit inner speech as in Mat 9:21); (c) the conscience (as the organ of moral-spiritual standing before God, engaged particularly in Jam 5:15 and 1Pe 3:21). The impact on the person operates at three levels: (a) immediate — peace, sight, conscience-cleared, or gratitude-return; (b) sustained — reoriented discipleship, conscience-integrity, capacity for intercessory faith; (c) constitutional — the person is repositioned from fragmented self-sufficiency or fear toward integrated wholeness-in-relation-to-Christ. The characteristic is not a passive condition but an active, crisis-precipitated or Spirit-prompted act that, when complete, leaves the person constitutionally altered.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20602 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Boundary definition from the verse evidence. (1) This characteristic is not soteriological salvation as standalone M38-A or M38-B event: the programme's researcher direction (sōzō sense-split) explicitly holds this sub-group distinct from general salvation (M38-A) and physical-only rescue (M38-B). Where sōzō denotes spiritual salvation without explicit physical healing context, or physical rescue without faith-as-named-activator, it belongs to a different sub-group. (2) It is not faith as general disposition or background virtue: this characteristic is faith exercised — activated, operative, directional — not faith as settled doctrinal orientation or character trait. Where faith names a person's general orientation to God without the specific movement toward wholeness, it is a different phenomenon. (3) It is not medical healing or natural recovery: the characteristic requires sōzō (not iaomai or therapeuō alone) with faith explicitly named as activating cause; healing that occurs without this faith-attribution does not belong to this characteristic. (4) It is not ritual as external act: 1Pe 3:21 explicitly marks the boundary — baptismal water as external physical cleansing does not belong to this characteristic; it is the interior conscience-appeal that constitutes the sōzō-event. (5) It is not fear-motivated superstition: the woman's touch is not magical thinking but formed interior conviction directed toward a specific person (Jesus), which is the structural difference between faith-exercised and mere ritual contact.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21
- **[20603 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, the verse evidence documents a speech-based mode, though it operates at two distinct levels. (1) Inner speech mode: Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 show the woman's faith operating as interior monologue — "if I touch his garment I will be made well" — which is a verbally-formed interior conviction, not merely affective hope. The inner speech structures the faith-act, giving it propositional content and directional commitment. This inner speech is causally prior to the outward act and is what Jesus names ("your faith has made you well") — the faith was operative as formed inner speech before any touch. (2) Outer cry mode: Bartimaeus in Mar 10:52 cries out verbally — "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me" (v.47-48, preceding v.52) — as the faith-speech that occasions the healing. The outer cry is the external expression of inner faith, and Jesus responds to it as the indicator of faith. The communicative function in both modes is the same: speech (inner or outer) articulates the conviction, identifies the object of trust, and thereby enacts the faith-exercise. Speech is not decorative but constitutive of this mode — the forming of the words (inner or outer) is the formation of the faith-act. Jam 5:15 adds a third speech mode: the communal prayer of faith spoken by elders over the sick, where spoken intercession is the operative vehicle of healing-through-faith.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Jam 5:15
- **[20604 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Silences in the immediate response evidence are as follows. (1) Mar 5:23 (VCG-02): Jairus's plea for his dying daughter documents the faith-exercise (urgent plea) but the healing of the daughter (Luk 8:50 parallel) is narrated in the following pericope; the immediate inner response of Jairus himself upon receiving healing for his daughter is not explicitly named in either verse of the M38-C corpus (Mar 5:23; Luk 8:50). The corpus captures the faith-exercise moment and the command to believe but not the inner-being state of Jairus post-healing. (2) Jam 5:15 (VCG-03): The verse describes the prayer of faith and its result (the sick will be saved; sins forgiven) but does not name the immediate inner-being response of the healed person. The intercessory mode of this verse leaves the recipient's inner response unnarrated. (3) 1Pe 3:21 (VCG-03): The conscience-appeal through baptism is described structurally but the immediate inner experience of the person who makes this appeal and receives the good conscience is not phenomenologically narrated in the verse; the sustained state (good conscience) is named but the moment of reception is not described.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20605 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, the sustained effect differs from the immediate response in both register and duration. (1) In the haemorrhage narrative, the immediate response is peace (Mar 5:34; Luk 8:48) — this names the inner-being state at the moment of reception. The sustained effect evidenced from the Bartimaeus parallel is discipleship-orientation (Mar 10:52 — "followed him on the way") — a long-term relational-directional constitution that goes beyond the immediate peace-experience. The immediate response is a state (peace); the sustained effect is an orientation (following). These are different in kind: one is receptive and settling, the other is active and directional. (2) In the conscience-appeal mode (1Pe 3:21), the good conscience appears to be both immediate result and sustained state — it is both what is received and what is maintained through the resurrection ground. Here the distinction between immediate and sustained is less sharp. (3) For intercessory faith (Jam 5:15), no immediate inner response is named for the healed person (see T1.5.3 silences), but the sustained effect of community-practiced intercessory faith would be the establishment of the community as a site of ongoing integrated rescue — a capacity-building sustained effect that exceeds the individual healing event. The general pattern is: immediate response = reception of specific inner-being shift (peace, sight, clear conscience); sustained effect = constitutional reorientation (discipleship, conscience-integrity, intercessory capacity) that endures beyond the precipitating event.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Mar 10:52,1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15
- **[20606 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus provides partial evidence for this through contrast cases. (1) The nine lepers (Luk 17:17-19): those who receive physical cure but do not return with faith-in-gratitude encounter the possibility of sōzō-wholeness but do not receive it in its full register. Their inner-being state is one of benefit-reception without relational acknowledgment — they remain in a posture of self-continuation after healing rather than return-and-recognition. The verse does not name their inner state as fear or hostility, but as ungrateful self-directedness. (2) The crowd that rebukes Bartimaeus (Mar 10:48): those who tell him to be silent are in a state of social conformity that suppresses faith-cry — they encounter the possibility of faith-exercised but remain in a normalised expectation that healing is not available or appropriate. Their inner state is one of resigned normalcy rather than faith-urgency. (3) Luk 8:50 implies that those who remain in fear rather than believing encounter the saving-healing possibility but cannot receive it — their inner state is fear-governed. (4) 1Pe 3:21 implies that those who treat baptism as external rite rather than conscience-appeal encounter the form of the characteristic without its inner reality — their state is formalistic externalism, with conscience unengaged. The common thread is: the person who does not receive the characteristic remains in a state of inner fragmentation — fear-governed, self-directed, socially conformist, or externally-focused — without the inner-being movement toward God that reception requires.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:17,Luk 17:18,Luk 17:19,Mar 10:48,Luk 8:50,1Pe 3:21
- **[20607 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Secondary dimensions are evidenced and do not compete with the primary volitional-faith classification but supplement it. (1) Conscience dimension: 1Pe 3:21 and Jam 5:15 both engage the conscience as a primary inner-being organ — "appeal to God for a good conscience" (1Pe 3:21); sins forgiven alongside healing (Jam 5:15). The conscience dimension is real and irreducible in M38-C-VCG-03, but it appears as the inner-being locus that faith-exercised restores rather than as the operating faculty of faith itself. Conscience is the dimension addressed; volitional-faith is the dimension that addresses it. (2) Relational-hope dimension: the woman's interior conviction (Mat 9:21; Mar 5:28) contains an anticipatory hope — "I will be made well" — which is a hope-oriented projection of the inner being toward future reception. This is a secondary relational-hope dimension active in the faith-formation stage. (3) Somatic-spiritual bridging dimension: the characteristic consistently bridges physical and spiritual registers, which means that the somatic dimension of the inner person (or its analogue — the person's embodied condition) is engaged alongside the volitional-spiritual. This is not a competing classification but a distinctive feature that marks this characteristic as embodied-faith, not disembodied mysticism. None of these secondary dimensions displace volitional-faith as primary; each names an aspect of what faith-exercised engages or produces rather than an alternative primary faculty.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15,Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28
- **[20608 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The twelve verses in M38-C (VCG-01, VCG-02, VCG-03) do not explicitly assign this characteristic to the *spirit* level by name. No verse uses pneuma to locate faith or healing-wholeness at the spirit stratum. The operative faculty named throughout is faith (pistis), and the locus described is the heart and conscience (Mar 5:28 "inner conviction"; Jam 5:15 "prayer of faith"; 1Pe 3:21 "appeal to God for a good conscience"). Spirit-level location as a distinct anatomical register is not affirmed in this corpus.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20609 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence locates the characteristic at the soul level indirectly but substantially. Mar 5:28 (vc_id=9013) describes the hemorrhaging woman's "inner conviction" operating before any outward act, placing faith in the inward personal faculty. Mat 9:21 (vc_id=9010) presents the woman's interior faith-speech ("if I touch his garment I will be made well") as a soul-level self-persuasion. Luk 8:48 (vc_id=9017) and Mar 5:34 (vc_id=9014) confirm that the resultant "peace" is granted to the inner person. Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) names conscience as the organ restored, and 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) grounds salvation in an interior appeal (eperōtēma) to God for a good conscience — both pointing to soul-level location. The characteristic's primary locus is the interior personal faculty of faith and conscience.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mat 9:21,Luk 8:48,Mar 5:34,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20610 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Heart-location is implied rather than lexically explicit across M38-C. Mar 5:28 (vc_id=9013) is glossed as "faith working in the heart before any word is spoken," placing interior conviction in the heart. Mat 9:21–22 (vc_id=9010–9011) show an interior faith-speech and its confirmation — faith operating at the level of deepest personal trust, which the corpus's own meanings describe as "heart trusting" (kw on vc_id=9011, 9013). The heart is thus the implied seat of the activating faith, though no verse in this VCG set uses the word kardia explicitly to name that location.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mat 9:21,Mat 9:22
- **[20611 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — No verse in M38-C explicitly locates this characteristic in the mind (nous, dianoia, or phronēma). The operative faculty throughout is faith (pistis) and conscience (syneidēsis in 1Pe 3:21). The woman's inner faith-speech (Mat 9:21, Mar 5:28) might carry a cognitive colouration, but the corpus meanings do not assign it to the mind faculty. Mind-level location is not evidenced.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28
- **[20612 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. Two soul-level sub-faculties beyond heart and mind appear. First, *conscience* is explicitly named: 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) defines baptism's saving efficacy as "an appeal to God for a good conscience," and Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) pairs healing of body with forgiveness/cleansing of sin, implicating conscience as the inward register of that forgiveness. Second, *urgency / desperate will* appears in VCG-02: Mar 5:23 (vc_id=9012) locates an inner-being posture of anguished parental longing ("earnest faith and parental love seeking life"), and Luk 8:50 (vc_id=9018) identifies fear as the inner-being state that faith displaces — marking the affective-volitional sub-region of the soul as active in this characteristic.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50
- **[20613 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, with specific textual warrants. The hemorrhaging woman's touch of Jesus's garment (Mar 5:28, vc_id=9013; Mat 9:21, vc_id=9010) makes the *hand* and the act of physical contact the concrete medium through which interior faith reaches toward sōzō. Bartimaeus's restored *eyes* (Mar 10:52, vc_id=9016; Luk 18:42, vc_id=9020) and the blind man's sight-restoration make the visual organ the explicit site of healing. The Samaritan leper's cleansed *skin* (Luk 17:19, vc_id=9019) and the dying girl's restoration to life (Mar 5:23, vc_id=9012; Luk 8:50, vc_id=9018) extend the body-part linkage to skin and the whole living body. In Jam 5:15 the "sick" person's body is the named site of the prayer of faith's effect.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mat 9:21,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 17:19,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15
- **[20614 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The primary direction is *soul/heart-to-body*: interior faith (formed in the heart before any word is spoken, Mar 5:28, vc_id=9013) activates the healing of the physical organ. Jesus consistently names faith as the causal factor operating from within the person outward ("your faith has made you well," Mat 9:22, vc_id=9011; Mar 5:34, vc_id=9014; Mar 10:52, vc_id=9016; Luk 8:48, vc_id=9017; Luk 17:19, vc_id=9019; Luk 18:42, vc_id=9020). However, a secondary *body-to-soul feedback* is present: the physical act of touch (Mar 5:28), the act of crying out (Mar 10:52), and the physical return/prostration of the Samaritan (Luk 17:19) are bodily expressions that accompany and complete the soul's movement — suggesting bidirectional interaction rather than a purely one-way flow.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Luk 18:42
- **[20615 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The corpus is silent on any sustained constitutional deposit, generational consequence, or DNA-level effect. The healings in M38-C are discrete salvific events rather than ongoing practices whose cumulative effect is described. No verse addresses whether repeated exercise of faith-activated healing leaves a lasting constitutional imprint. This finding feeds into T5.7.
- **[20616 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence points to a *convergent* origin. On the human side, faith is formed within the person — the woman's interior conviction pre-exists Jesus's word (Mat 9:21, Mar 5:28), and Bartimaeus's persistent cry arises from within himself. On the divine side, the saving act is explicitly attributed to Christ's power (sōzō named in every anchor verse) and, in Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024), to God who heals through the prayer of faith. In 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) the appeal is directed *to* God through Christ's resurrection, grounding the saving act in divine agency. The characteristic therefore originates at the intersection of internally-generated faith and externally-bestowed divine power — neither purely from within the person nor purely imposed from outside.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20617 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, a clear downward-then-manifesting movement is evidenced. Interior faith (formed at the heart/soul level) moves outward through the body (the touch of the garment, the cry of Bartimaeus) and occasions a physical healing, which then feeds back into the soul as peace (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) or into restored relational posture (Mar 10:52, discipleship following). In Jam 5:15 and 1Pe 3:21 the movement extends further: from intercessory faith (inner being of intercessor) → prayer (speech act) → healing of body → cleansing of conscience (inner being of the sick). The constitutional movement thus runs: soul/heart → bodily act → bodily healing → soul/conscience restored — a multi-level circuit.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Mar 10:52,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20618 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The evidence does not indicate that the characteristic originates primarily at the spirit level. The named operative faculty is faith exercised within the human interior (heart, conscience, inner conviction), and the saving source is Christ/God acting from outside. No verse in M38-C deploys pneuma or explicitly assigns the origin of faith-activated healing to the human spirit stratum.
- **[20619 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Soul-level location reveals that faith exercised as healing-wholeness is a deeply *personal and interior* event. The woman's pre-touch inner monologue (Mat 9:21, vc_id=9010; Mar 5:28, vc_id=9013) shows that the activating conviction is formed in the inmost personal register before any observable act. Peace granted after healing (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) confirms that the soul — not only the body — is the ultimate beneficiary. In 1Pe 3:21 and Jam 5:15 conscience is the inmost organ that salvation touches and clears, indicating that the characteristic's deepest reach is the personal interior. Soul-level location thus reveals this characteristic as irreducibly interior: physical healing is the outward sign of an inner transformation.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15
- **[20620 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Heart-location, where evidenced (Mar 5:28, vc_id=9013; Mat 9:21–22, vc_id=9010–9011), reveals that faith-activated healing engages the *integrating and trusting* function of the heart. The keyword "heart trusting" on vc_id=9011 and 9013 indicates that the heart's trust-capacity is the specific sub-function operative here — not merely cognitive assent or emotional feeling, but a settled orientation of personal reliance directed toward Jesus. This pre-verbal, pre-action conviction ("before any word is spoken," Mar 5:28 gloss) shows the heart operating as the source of determination and will, integrating knowing ("if I touch his garment"), willing (she presses through the crowd), and confident expectation (she will be made well).
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mat 9:21,Mat 9:22
- **[20621 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — Since mind-level location is not evidenced in this corpus (see T2.4.1), no finding can be drawn from it. The characteristic does not appear to operate primarily through the discursive or analytical functions of the mind. This silence may itself be significant: faith-activated healing in M38-C precedes verbal articulation and rational deliberation, suggesting the characteristic's operative core is sub-cognitive.
- **[20622 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Two sub-locations were identified in T2.5.1: *conscience* and the *affective-volitional* sub-region. Conscience (1Pe 3:21, vc_id=8992; Jam 5:15, vc_id=9024) reveals that the characteristic's ultimate inner-being reach is moral and relational standing before God: sōzō is not complete until the conscience is addressed. This distinguishes M38-C from mere physical cure. The affective-volitional sub-region (Mar 5:23, vc_id=9012; Luk 8:50, vc_id=9018) — urgent parental love and fear displaced by faith — reveals that the characteristic can be activated under conditions of mortal crisis, where the soul's affective register (grief, fear, love) is the presenting inner-being posture that faith must overcome or redirect. Taken together, these locations reveal that the characteristic spans the full affective-moral range of the soul.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50
- **[20623 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Scripture's body-part linkages in M38-C are primarily *mediating* and *confirmatory*. The touch of the garment (Mar 5:28, Mat 9:21) is a *mediating* act: the physical contact is the vehicle through which interior faith reaches its object, not the cause of healing. Jesus's question "who touched me?" (Mar 5:30, context of vc_id=9013) confirms that the body-act is the medium, not the source. The restored eyes (Mar 10:52, Luk 18:42) and cleansed skin (Luk 17:19) are *confirmatory* and *expressive*: they make the inner sōzō visible and publicly attestable. In Jam 5:15 the sick body is the *occasion* for a prayer that also addresses the soul — the body-link is therefore *indicative* of a deeper need. Scripture's use of body parts here consistently frames them as witnesses or channels of an inward reality, not autonomous sites of saving power.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mat 9:21,Mar 5:30,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20624 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The primary soul-to-body direction, with secondary body-to-soul feedback (established in T2.7.1), has two consequences for understanding the characteristic. First, it confirms that the inner being (faith, heart-trust) is genuinely *causal* and not merely accompanying — Jesus's attribution formula ("your faith has made you well," six times across the synoptics) makes inner faith the named operative cause, not the bodily act. Second, the bodily expression of faith (touch, cry, prostration) is not incidental but functionally *completing*: it is the point at which interior conviction becomes directed action, which itself opens the circuit of sōzō. This means the characteristic is neither purely inner (quietist) nor purely outer (ritualist) but constitutionally integrated, requiring the soul-body axis to be engaged for the full healing-wholeness event to occur.
- **[20625 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — No evidence in M38-C addresses whether sustained or repeated exercise of faith-activated healing leaves a constitutional deposit in the body. The corpus records discrete healing events. No verse describes cumulative bodily change, altered physical constitution, generational blessing, or heritable transformation resulting from the characteristic. The silence is consistent and complete. This finding feeds into T5.7.
- **[20626 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence reveals a *dual* origin structure. (1) Human interior: faith is formed within the person's own inner being prior to any external prompting in most M38-C cases (Mat 9:21, Mar 5:28, Mar 10:46–47). The woman's interior monologue and Bartimaeus's self-initiated cry both represent faith generated from within. (2) Divine enabling: the actual sōzō event is always attributed to Christ's power, and in Jam 5:15 and 1Pe 3:21 the saving/healing is explicitly God's act in response to faith. These two origins are not competitive; the corpus presents them as structurally necessary to each other — faith without the divine respondent is inert, and divine power here is consistently accessed through faith as the activating human contribution. The origin is therefore irreducibly dual.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:46,Mar 10:47,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20627 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The sequence evidenced across M38-C VCG-01 and VCG-02 follows a consistent four-stage pattern: (1) *Formation*: interior faith/conviction arises in the heart or conscience of the seeker (Mat 9:21, Mar 5:28, Mar 5:23, Luk 8:50). (2) *Expression*: faith moves through the body — touch, cry, prostration, prayer (Mar 5:28, Mar 10:47, Luk 17:16, Jam 5:15). (3) *Reception*: sōzō descends as physical restoration (Mat 9:22, Mar 5:34, Mar 10:52, etc.). (4) *Consolidation*: the inner being is confirmed in peace, discipleship, or cleansed conscience (Mar 5:34 "go in peace"; Mar 10:52 "followed Jesus"; 1Pe 3:21 "good conscience"). VCG-02 compresses stages 1–2 into a crisis posture where fear and faith co-exist before the sōzō word arrives. The pattern is consistent and directional, not reversible.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Mar 10:47,Luk 17:16,Jam 5:15,Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,1Pe 3:21
- **[20628 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — Since spirit-level location is not evidenced for this characteristic in M38-C (see T2.1.1 and T2.1.2), no finding can be drawn about what such location would reveal. The depth and nature of the characteristic is instead evidenced at the soul level (heart, conscience, faith-faculty) as documented in T2.2.1 and T2.5.1.
- **[20629 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Evidence is not silent: soul-level location is positively evidenced. See T2.2.1 and T2.2.2. Faith as the interior activating faculty, conscience as the inner organ restored, and peace as the inward state granted — all locate the characteristic firmly at the soul level. No silence note is required.
- **[20630 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Evidence is not silent: heart-location is implied and supported by the corpus meanings. See T2.3.1 and T2.3.2. The keywords "heart trusting" (vc_id=9011, 9013) and the gloss "faith working in the heart before any word is spoken" (vc_id=9013) together establish heart as the implied seat of this characteristic. No silence note is required.
- **[20631 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The evidence is silent on mind-level location. No verse in M38-C assigns the characteristic to nous, dianoia, or any explicit mind-faculty. The characteristic's inner-being locus is the heart and conscience, not the discursive or analytical mind. This silence is noted explicitly.
- **[20632 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Evidence is not silent: conscience and the affective-volitional sub-region are both positively evidenced as soul sub-locations for this characteristic. See T2.5.1 and T2.5.2. No silence note is required.
- **[20633 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Body-part links are positively evidenced. See T2.6.1 and T2.6.2. No silence note is required.
- **[20634 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — A body-characteristic link is positively evidenced with clear directionality. See T2.7.1 and T2.7.2. No silence note is required.
- **[20635 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The evidence is silent on constitutional deposit in the body. No verse in M38-C describes any lasting bodily imprint, generational consequence, or cumulative constitutional change resulting from the sustained exercise of faith-activated healing. This silence is noted explicitly and feeds into T5.7.
- **[20636 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Partial variation is observable. In VCG-01 (individual healing narratives), faith originates within the seeking person (interior monologue, persistent cry) and the divine response is immediate and personal. In VCG-02 (crisis/death context, vc_id=9012, 9018), faith is commanded from outside by Jesus ("only believe"), introducing an element of externally-prompted origin not present in VCG-01. In VCG-03 (Jam 5:15, 1Pe 3:21), the origin shifts further: faith is exercised corporately (intercessory prayer by elders) or mediated through a rite (baptism), suggesting that in communal/ecclesial contexts the characteristic's origin is partly institutional and partly inward. The dual structure (human faith + divine power) remains constant, but the *trigger* of faith shifts from spontaneous interior conviction to commanded belief to mediated corporate intercession across the three VCGs.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20637 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Constitutional movement is positively evidenced. See T2.10.1 and T2.10.2. The four-stage sequence (formation → expression → reception → consolidation) is documented and directional. No silence note is required.
- **[20638 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The evidence is silent on spirit-level location for this characteristic. No verse in M38-C assigns faith-activated healing-wholeness to the human spirit (pneuma) as a distinct constitutional stratum. This silence is noted explicitly.
- **[20639 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, with a specific inner-sense register. The woman's pre-touch interior speech (Mat 9:21, vc_id=9010; Mar 5:28, vc_id=9013) shows an interior *perception* of Jesus's healing capacity — a knowing by apprehension that precedes rational argument. Bartimaeus's repeated cry "Son of David, have mercy on me" (context of Mar 10:52, vc_id=9016) presupposes a spiritual perception that Jesus is the Messianic healer. The Samaritan leper's return (Luk 17:19, vc_id=9019) presupposes perception that his healing is not random but sourced in Jesus. The operative inner sense is not physical sight (paradoxically, several healees are blind or touch-mediated) but a form of *spiritual discernment* — an inner apprehension of Jesus's identity and salvific power that makes faith possible.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Luk 17:19
- **[20640 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, minimally but structurally. The woman's inner speech "if I touch his garment I shall be made well" (Mat 9:21, vc_id=9010) is a conditional proposition — a cognitive inference from knowledge of Jesus to expected outcome. This represents a basic cognitive act: she *understands* something about Jesus that grounds her expectation. Similarly, the father's plea (Mar 5:23, vc_id=9012) implies a cognitive grasp that Jesus can do what physicians cannot. In Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) the connection between prayer of faith, healing, and forgiveness requires cognitive integration of physical and spiritual registers. However, the characteristic does not require extended reasoning; the cognitive element is the minimal *belief-content* that anchors faith (a propositional apprehension of Jesus's power), not deliberate theological analysis.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:23,Jam 5:15
- **[20641 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Partially and inferentially. The interior convictions operative in M38-C presuppose that the faith-seekers have prior knowledge of Jesus's healing acts or reputation (the woman has heard reports about Jesus — Mark 5:27, background context of vc_id=9013; Bartimaeus calls on the "Son of David," implying remembered messianic expectation). Memory of prior testimony about Jesus functions as the background substrate that makes the interior conviction of M38-C possible. However, the verses themselves do not explicitly name memory as the operative faculty; its role is logically implied rather than textually stated. In 1Pe 3:21 the "resurrection" invoked is a remembered/proclaimed event that grounds the baptismal appeal. The engagement is real but sub-surface.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21
- **[20642 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, markedly so, especially in VCG-02. Mar 5:23 (vc_id=9012) explicitly glosses the father's inner being as "earnest faith and parental love seeking life" — love and urgency are the affective register driving the plea. Luk 8:50 (vc_id=9018) identifies fear as the affect that Jesus commands faith to displace. In VCG-01, peace received (Mar 5:34, vc_id=9014; Luk 8:48, vc_id=9017) is an affective state marking the inner-being outcome of sōzō — the healing is felt as peace in the soul. Gratitude is noted as an affective response in Luk 17:19 (vc_id=9019, kw: 'gratitude'). Affect is not merely epiphenomenal but is the inner-being climate within which faith is formed and exercised, and peace is the affective seal of the wholeness received.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19
- **[20643 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The corpus does not evidence engagement of the creative faculty (imagination, origination) as a distinct operative element in faith-activated healing-wholeness. The woman's interior conviction is better characterised as perception and trust than as imagination. No verse in M38-C invokes the creative capacity of the inner being. This faculty appears to be silent here.
- **[20644 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, and volition is structurally central. Faith in M38-C is not passive reception but an active choice to press through a crowd (Mar 5:27–28, context vc_id=9013), to call out persistently against social opposition (Mar 10:47–48, context vc_id=9016), to make a desperate plea (Mar 5:23, vc_id=9012), to return and give thanks (Luk 17:15–16, context vc_id=9019), and to offer intercessory prayer (Jam 5:15, vc_id=9024). Each of these is a volitional act — a determined choice to pursue the sōzō object. Luk 8:50 (vc_id=9018) is the sharpest instance: Jesus commands "only believe" — faith is here a volitional response to a command, placing volition at the cusp between divine imperative and human choice. The characteristic cannot be separated from the will's engagement.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:27,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:47,Mar 10:48,Mar 5:23,Luk 17:15,Luk 17:16,Jam 5:15,Luk 8:50
- **[20645 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, strongly. The agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — is extensively engaged. The woman initiates the approach and touch (Mat 9:21, vc_id=9010; Mar 5:28, vc_id=9013). Bartimaeus initiates and persists in his cry (Mar 10:52, vc_id=9016). The father makes an urgent plea (Mar 5:23, vc_id=9012). The Samaritan turns back and prostrates himself (Luk 17:19, vc_id=9019). The elders anoint and pray (Jam 5:15, vc_id=9024). The baptismal candidate makes an "appeal to God" (1Pe 3:21, vc_id=8992). In every case the faith-seeker is an active agent initiating or completing a directed act toward Jesus/God. Jesus's confirmation formula ("your faith has made you well") retroactively identifies the person's agency as the humanly operative cause — not passive waiting but directed initiative.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:52,Mar 5:23,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20646 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Partially. Moral evaluation is not the primary faculty operative in the individual synoptic healings of VCG-01 and VCG-02, where the presenting need is physical. However, VCG-03 introduces a moral evaluation dimension: Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) states "if he has committed sins, he will be forgiven," implying that the person must have moral awareness of sin for forgiveness to be relevant. 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) grounds the saving appeal in the desire for "a good conscience" — conscience being the organ of moral self-evaluation before God. The characteristic thus engages moral evaluation specifically at the point where physical healing opens onto spiritual forgiveness: the person's inner being must be capable of recognising moral standing as a dimension of the wholeness sought.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20647 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, explicitly and definitionally in VCG-03. 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) is the clearest instance: baptism saves "as an appeal to God for a good conscience through the resurrection of Jesus Christ" — conscience is the named inner organ that salvation addresses and clears. The meaning gloss states: "salvation touches and clears the conscience before God." Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) pairs healing and forgiveness, with the body-and-conscience pairing establishing that the sōzō event in this characteristic reaches the conscience as its spiritual terminus. In VCG-01 and VCG-02 the conscience is not explicitly named, but the "peace" received (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) can be read as a conscience-state — the inner being no longer alienated but at rest before God. The conscience is definitively engaged at the characteristic's deepest reach.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48
- **[20648 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, though partially. Conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — appears most clearly in the act of returning to give thanks (Luk 17:19, vc_id=9019, kw: 'gratitude'): the Samaritan leper's return is a conscientious act that combines moral awareness (recognition of benefit received), volition (choosing to return), and action (prostrating himself before Jesus). The elders' intercessory prayer in Jam 5:15 similarly integrates moral awareness (concern for sin's possible role in illness), volition (choosing to pray), and action (anointing and praying). The characteristic thus engages conscientiousness at the response-to-sōzō stage rather than the initiating stage — it is the conscientious person who completes the wholeness circuit through grateful, responsive action.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20649 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, centrally. The relational capacity is the structural context within which the entire characteristic operates. Faith in M38-C is always *directed toward a person* (Jesus, God), not toward an impersonal mechanism. The woman seeks Jesus specifically (Mat 9:21, Mar 5:28). Bartimaeus addresses Jesus by name and title (Mar 10:47). The father pleads with Jesus for his daughter (Mar 5:23). The Samaritan returns to give thanks *to Jesus* (Luk 17:19). The prayer of faith in Jam 5:15 is addressed to God. The baptismal appeal in 1Pe 3:21 is "to God." In every case, faith-activated healing is constitutionally relational: the inner faculty of faith is directed toward a relational other (Christ/God), and the outcome (peace, discipleship, forgiven conscience) is a restored or deepened relational standing. The characteristic cannot operate outside the relational axis.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:47,Mar 5:23,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20650 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic primarily *enables and deepens* spiritual perception. In the individual healing narratives, the faith-seekers already possess a perceptive grasp of Jesus's identity before the healing event; the healing deepens that perception retroactively (the Samaritan sees Jesus differently after his cleansing; Bartimaeus follows Jesus with open eyes). In Luk 8:50 (vc_id=9018), Jesus's command "only believe" works *against* the perceptive report (the girl is dead) — faith here partially *bypasses* the evidence of physical perception in favour of spiritual discernment. This suggests that when fear-driven perception of mortal reality threatens faith, the characteristic holds the spiritual perception in tension with and priority over the physical. The characteristic does not impair perception but reorders its authority.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:50
- **[20651 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic both *enables* minimal cognitive content (the belief-proposition about Jesus's power) and *partially bypasses* extended cognition. Faith in M38-C does not wait for full theological understanding before acting — the woman acts on a conditional inference (Mat 9:21) without a developed christology. Luk 8:50 commands faith precisely when the cognitive data (death of the child) would rationally preclude hope. Jam 5:15 and 1Pe 3:21 require cognitive engagement with the connection between body-healing and conscience-forgiveness, representing a more developed cognitive integration. The characteristic thus *enables* a basic cognitive grasp that grounds faith and *bypasses* the cognitive processing that would counsel despair, while in more mature expressions it *deepens* cognitive integration of physical and spiritual registers.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20652 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *enables* the deployment of memorial knowledge of Jesus as background substrate (prior testimony about Jesus recalled by faith-seekers), but the corpus does not evidence memory being deepened, bypassed, or impaired as a distinct consequence of the characteristic. The engagement with memory is enabling and foundational rather than transformative. No verse describes a change in the memory faculty resulting from faith-activated healing.
- **[20653 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *transforms* affect: it does not eliminate it but redirects and fulfils it. In VCG-02, fear is not simply bypassed but is actively displaced by faith under command (Luk 8:50, vc_id=9018) — the affect of fear is replaced by the affect of trust. Parental love and urgency (Mar 5:23, vc_id=9012) are not suppressed but are channelled through faith toward a redemptive end. Peace received as outcome (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) represents an *enabling and deepening* of positive affect — the soul is brought into a settled affective state of wholeness. Gratitude (Luk 17:19) shows affect *deepened* by the sōzō event. The characteristic does not impair affect; rather, it is the faculty through which distressed affect (fear, grief, urgency) is transformed into restful, grateful affect (peace, discipleship).
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:50,Mar 5:23,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19
- **[20654 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The corpus is silent on any enabling, deepening, bypassing, or impairing of the creative faculty through faith-activated healing-wholeness. No evidence in M38-C addresses this relationship.
- **[20655 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *enables and strengthens* volition. The evidence shows willpower actively engaged and carried through against obstacles: the woman presses through a crowd despite her ritual uncleanness (Mar 5:28); Bartimaeus continues crying out despite being silenced by the crowd (Mar 10:47–48, context vc_id=9016); the father pleads on behalf of another person with urgent determination (Mar 5:23). Luk 8:50 commands volition ("only believe") as a counter-act to fear — here the characteristic directly addresses the volitional capacity, commanding it to override an emotionally compelling alternative. Faith as presented in M38-C does not bypass the will (it is not imposed) but *strengthens* it — the person's will is fully engaged and its exercise is the activating mechanism of the sōzō event. No evidence of impairment.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:28,Mar 10:47,Mar 10:48,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50
- **[20656 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic strongly *enables* agency. As noted in T3.7.1, every instance in M38-C involves the faith-seeker as an active initiator. Jesus's confirmation formula ("your faith has made you well") *deepens* the person's sense of agency retroactively — they are told that their inner act was causally operative in the healing. This divine confirmation of human agency is remarkable: Jesus does not say "I healed you" but "your faith has saved you," attributing causation to the person's inner faculty. This deepens rather than overrides human agency, presenting the characteristic as one that elevates the person's inner-being capacity to be a genuine secondary cause in the sōzō event. No bypassing or impairment of agency is evidenced.
- **[20657 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *deepens* moral evaluation at the point where physical healing opens onto spiritual forgiveness. Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) — "if he has committed sins, he will be forgiven" — invites the sick person and the praying elders to hold open the possibility that sin has contributed to illness, requiring moral self-evaluation. 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) grounds the saving appeal in the desire for "a good conscience" — meaning that the person has evaluated their conscience before God and desires it to be cleared. The characteristic does not impair or bypass moral evaluation; rather, the wholeness it delivers is partly constituted by the fulfilment of moral evaluation (conscience satisfied, sin forgiven). No bypassing or impairment is evidenced.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20658 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *enables and clears* the conscience. In 1Pe 3:21 (vc_id=8992) the saving event is specifically the restoration of a good conscience — sōzō reaches its inner terminus in conscience-cleansing. In Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) forgiveness accompanies healing, meaning the conscience that was burdened by sin is released. The characteristic thus *enables* a good conscience to exist where a troubled one did before. There is no evidence of the characteristic impairing or bypassing conscience; rather, conscience is the deepest stratum that the sōzō event must reach for healing-wholeness to be complete. Peace granted (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) may be understood as a conscience at rest, further supporting this pattern.
  - _cites:_ 1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48
- **[20659 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *enables and expresses* conscientiousness. The Samaritan leper's return in Luk 17:19 (vc_id=9019) is the paradigmatic instance: moral awareness of gift received + volitional return + physical prostration = a complete conscientious act. Jesus distinguishes this one returning leper from the nine who did not return, suggesting that the wholeness of sōzō is more fully realised in the person who completes the conscientious circuit. Jam 5:15 (vc_id=9024) requires the elders to act with integrated moral awareness (acknowledging sin's possible role), volition (choosing to pray), and action (anointing) — a conscientious process. The characteristic does not impair conscientiousness; in its fullest expressions it *calls conscientiousness forth* as the appropriate inner-being response to sōzō received.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20660 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The characteristic *enables and deepens* relational capacity. The relational outcome of faith-activated healing is consistently a strengthened or newly initiated relationship with Jesus: Bartimaeus "followed Jesus" (Mar 10:52, vc_id=9016) — discipleship as deepened relational engagement; the Samaritan returned to give thanks and was told "your faith has made you well" — relational recognition sealed the wholeness. Peace received (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) names a relational state (no longer at enmity or distance, but at rest before Jesus). The baptismal appeal of 1Pe 3:21 is "to God" — establishing a cleared-conscience relational standing before the Father. The characteristic does not impair or bypass relational capacity; it is inherently a relational event, and its deepest effect is the restoration or inauguration of genuine personal connection with Christ and God.
  - _cites:_ Mar 10:52,Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,1Pe 3:21
- **[20661 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pattern of perception engagement in M38-C reveals that this characteristic is *trans-sensory* in its perceptive requirement. The faith-healings consistently involve persons whose physical perception is compromised (blind Bartimaeus, vc_id=9016, 9020; the hemorrhaging woman who cannot see the source of power, vc_id=9013) or irrelevant (leper, dying child), yet whose inner spiritual perception is highly active. This inverse pattern — physical sense impaired or bypassed, spiritual apprehension operative — reveals that faith-activated healing-wholeness depends on and exercises an *inner* perceptive faculty (discernment of Jesus's identity and power) that is independent of, and in some cases prior to, restored physical perception. The characteristic therefore reveals that the deepest perceptive capacity is not sensory but relational-spiritual.
- **[20662 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pattern reveals that this characteristic operates at the *threshold* of cognition: it requires enough cognitive content to be intentional (a belief-proposition about Jesus's power) but is not dependent on extended cognitive processing. The fact that faith precedes Jesus's healing word in most cases (Mat 9:21, Mar 5:28) and is confirmed rather than created by Jesus's statement reveals that the characteristic's cognitive content is *anticipatory and minimal* — a prior grasp that generates expectation rather than a post-hoc interpretation. The bypassing of cognition in Luk 8:50 (faith commanded against the cognitive data of death) further reveals that the characteristic is *supra-cognitive*: it can hold its belief-content against contrary evidence. This distinguishes faith-activated healing from mere rational hope or calculated expectation.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Luk 8:50
- **[20663 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The partial and inferential engagement with memory (T3.3.1, T3.3.2) reveals that this characteristic is *dependent on received testimony* but not primarily a memorial act. The background presence of prior knowledge of Jesus as substrate suggests that faith-activated healing cannot arise ex nihilo — it requires that something has been heard, seen, or told about Jesus that memory holds available. Yet the characteristic's activating moment is not memorial recall but present-tense conviction and action. Memory thus functions as the *enabling precondition* rather than the operative faculty. This reveals that the characteristic stands at the intersection of received testimony (memorial substrate) and present-tense personal faith (operative faculty) — a structure consistent with the broader Pauline "faith comes from hearing" frame, even where that frame is not lexically present in M38-C.
- **[20664 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pattern of affect engagement across M38-C reveals that this characteristic does not require affective calm as its precondition but can operate through and transform a wide range of affective states. Faith is exercised in conditions of desperation (Mar 5:23), fear (Luk 8:50), chronic suffering (Mar 5:28), marginalisation (Mar 10:46), and leprosy-induced exclusion (Luk 17:19). The characteristic is therefore *affectively robust*: it does not require the person to achieve emotional equilibrium before faith becomes possible. Rather, the distressed affective state becomes the occasion for faith's exercise, and peace/gratitude is the affective transformation that follows sōzō. This reveals that the characteristic bridges the most extreme negative affective states (mortal fear, chronic anguish) and the most positive (peace, wholeness), with faith as the interior faculty that makes the transit.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Mar 5:28,Mar 10:46,Luk 17:19
- **[20665 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The consistent non-engagement with the creative faculty across M38-C reveals that this characteristic does not require or produce imaginative origination as part of its operation. The inner-being faculty engaged is trust directed toward a known person (Jesus/God), not the generative production of new inner-being content. This silence reveals that faith-activated healing-wholeness is a *receptive and relational* characteristic rather than an originating or creative one — it receives and trusts rather than imagines and creates.
- **[20666 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The consistent and strong engagement of volition across all three VCGs reveals that faith-activated healing-wholeness is *constitutively volitional*: it cannot occur without the person's determined exercise of will. This is theologically significant: the characteristic does not represent a passive divine imposition but a genuine human willing-toward-God that is confirmed and honoured by divine response. The fact that Jesus attributes causation to the person's faith ("your faith has made you well") rather than to his own sovereign act reveals that the volitional structure of the characteristic is not incidental but definitional. Simultaneously, the command "only believe" (Luk 8:50) shows that volition is addressable and commandable — it is not self-generated absolutely but can be evoked by authoritative word. The pattern reveals a *commanded-yet-genuine* volition: real human choice operating in response to divine invitation.
  - _cites:_ Luk 8:50
- **[20667 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pattern of consistent, strong, and divinely-affirmed agency engagement across M38-C reveals that this characteristic exemplifies a distinctive biblical anthropology: human inner-being agency is genuinely operative as a secondary cause within a divinely-effected event. Jesus's repeated attribution formula establishes that the person's inner-being act (faith exercised) is the named human mechanism through which divine saving power flows. This is not a diminution of divine sovereignty (sōzō is always ultimately God's act) but an elevation of human agency: the person is constitutionally capable of directing an inner-being faculty in such a way that it becomes the receiving and activating conduit of divine wholeness. The pattern reveals that the characteristic is *co-agential* — divine and human agency operating in structured complementarity, with faith as the hinge.
- **[20668 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The partial engagement with moral evaluation — present in VCG-03 but largely absent from VCG-01 and VCG-02 — reveals that moral evaluation is not the *activating* faculty of this characteristic but its *deepening* faculty. The individual synoptic healings do not require the faith-seeker to evaluate their moral standing before faith can be exercised — faith is directed at physical healing, and the spiritual register is implied or supplementary. In Jam 5:15 and 1Pe 3:21, the characteristic's reach has expanded to explicitly include moral evaluation as part of the wholeness sought. The pattern thus reveals a developmental structure: the characteristic begins (in VCG-01/02) as faith-toward-physical-healing with spiritual overtone, and matures (in VCG-03) into a faith that explicitly addresses moral standing. Moral evaluation is the endpoint-deepening of the characteristic, not its starting point.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20669 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pattern reveals that conscience is the *ultimate inner-being terminus* of this characteristic. In VCG-01 and VCG-02 the conscience is implicitly addressed through peace (Mar 5:34, Luk 8:48) but not named. In VCG-03 (1Pe 3:21, Jam 5:15) it is explicitly named as the inner organ that sōzō clears and restores. This progressive revelation — from implied peace (soul at rest) to explicitly cleared conscience — reveals that healing-wholeness in M38-C is not complete at the physical level but has a necessary inner-being terminus in conscience-restoration. The pattern further reveals that faith-activated wholeness is not merely therapeutic (body restored) but forensic and relational (conscience cleared before God). The full characteristic is not present unless conscience is reached. The non-engagement of conscience in VCG-01 and VCG-02 does not contradict this; rather, the peace granted in those texts is the experiential form of the conscience-at-rest that VCG-03 names explicitly.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,1Pe 3:21,Jam 5:15
- **[20670 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pattern reveals that conscientiousness is an *integrating response-faculty* through which the fullness of sōzō is expressed and confirmed. Where conscientiousness is present (Luk 17:19, Jam 5:15), the healing-wholeness circuit is completed by a person who integrates moral awareness, will, and action in response to what has been received. Where it is absent (the nine lepers, context of Luk 17:19), healing is received but the wholeness-circuit is arguably incomplete — a point Jesus himself marks. This reveals that the characteristic, while activated by faith, finds its fullest human expression in conscientious response: the person who receives sōzō and conscientiously responds to it enters more completely into the inner-being state of wholeness that the characteristic names. Conscientiousness is thus the *completion faculty* of M38-C.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15
- **[20671 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The pervasive and consistent engagement of relational capacity across all three VCGs and all twelve verses reveals that this characteristic is *constitutively relational*: it cannot be abstracted from the person-to-person encounter between the faith-seeker and Jesus/God. Faith in M38-C is never generic trust in a principle or force; it is always specifically directed toward a named person (Jesus, "Son of David," God). The characteristic's outcomes (peace, discipleship, cleansed conscience, good standing before God) are all relational states — they describe a changed quality of connection between the person and the divine. The pattern thus reveals that the deepest structure of healing-wholeness through faith exercised is *relational repair and deepening*: physical healing is the visible sign of a relational reality (the person is now in right standing with the source of life), and the wholeness of sōzō is ultimately constituted by the quality of that relational bond. The characteristic's inner-being depth is therefore identical with the depth of relational capacity engaged.
- **[20672 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence strongly supports God-to-human operation, mediated through Jesus as divine agent. In Mat 9:22 and Mar 5:34, Jesus speaks healing into existence ("your faith has made you well"), functioning as the active divine source of the sōzō event. In Mar 10:52 and Luk 18:42, Jesus restores sight to Bartimaeus and the blind man, again naming faith as the instrument but himself as the giver. Luk 8:50 is particularly explicit: Jesus commands "only believe, and she will be made well," positioning himself as the one who will accomplish the rescue from death — the human's faith opens the door, but the saving act flows from Jesus as divine agent. In Jam 5:15, the prayer of faith heals, but the Lord is implicitly the healer who raises up the sick person. In 1Pe 3:21, salvation comes through resurrection (grounded in Christ's), not human effort — God through Christ is the source. The pattern across M38-C-VCG-01, VCG-02, and VCG-03 is consistent: God in Christ is the originating agent of wholeness, and the human's faith is the receptor-channel through which divine action flows.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20673 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence reveals a primarily responsive basis: God in Christ responds to faith as the presented condition, but the response is neither mechanical nor merely transactional. In Mat 9:22, Jesus "turned" and saw the woman — the response is personal and relational before the pronouncement. In Mar 5:34, "your faith has made you well" — faith is named as the operative factor, making the extension conditional upon faith exercised. Similarly Mar 10:52 and Luk 18:42 both credit faith as the factor Jesus acknowledges. Yet in Luk 8:50 (VCG-02), Jesus does not merely respond to already-present faith but commands it: "only believe" — suggesting that God's extension of healing wholeness can precede and summon faith rather than simply reward it, introducing a covenantal-initiative dimension. Jam 5:15 adds a corporate dimension: the prayer of faith as condition for healing and forgiveness. The evidence does not support unconditional extension of healing wholeness; faith is consistently named as the operative inner condition. The basis is best characterised as responsively covenantal — God in Christ responds to faith, but also calls forth the faith to which he will respond.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15
- **[20674 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence reveals a disposition of active willingness toward the whole person, not merely the body. In Mar 5:34, Jesus does not merely cure but sends the woman away "in peace" (eis eirēnēn) — the inner-being dimension is deliberately addressed. In Luk 8:48, the same peace-grant appears, confirming that Jesus's disposition extends to the inner state, not only the physical condition. In Luk 17:19, the Samaritan leper's faith-wholeness is distinguished from the other nine's physical cure by Jesus's explicit word — God's extension through Christ is directed toward the whole person whose inner being (faith, gratitude, return) is engaged. Bartimaeus's immediate following of Jesus (Mar 10:52) is noted by the narrative as a response to total restoration, suggesting that Jesus's healing intends discipleship-relation, not merely physical relief. Jam 5:15 confirms that God's extension encompasses forgiveness of sin alongside bodily healing, showing divine disposition toward comprehensive restoration. The cumulative picture across all three VCGs is a disposition of generous, holistic benevolence toward the whole human person — body, conscience, and relational standing before God.
  - _cites:_ Mar 5:34,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Mar 10:52,Jam 5:15
- **[20675 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence is not silent on God-to-human operation; it is extensively evidenced across all three VCGs as established in obs_id=324, 325, and 326 above. No silence notation is required.
- **[20676 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence shows clear human-toward-God movement across multiple registers. In Mat 9:21 (VCG-01), the woman's interior conviction — "if I touch his garment I shall be made well" — is a movement of faith directed toward Jesus as divine agent; her press through the crowd is a bodily enactment of this God-ward seeking. In Mar 5:28, the same interior conviction operates as a pre-verbal act of trust directed toward Jesus. In Mar 5:23 (VCG-02), Jairus falls at Jesus's feet with a desperate plea for his dying daughter — a posture of supplication. In Luk 8:50, the command "only believe" implies that faith is the instrument of human movement toward God in the crisis moment. In Luk 17:19, the Samaritan leper's return and prostration before Jesus is explicitly a movement of grateful worship, and Jesus distinguishes it as the act of faith that saves. In Jam 5:15 (VCG-03), intercessory prayer is the form of human movement toward God, with the prayer of faith as the vehicle through which healing and forgiveness flow. In 1Pe 3:21, baptism functions as "an appeal to God for a good conscience" — a formal covenantal movement of the inner person toward God. Human-to-God movement is thus evidenced in modes of seeking, supplication, interior conviction, intercessory prayer, and covenantal appeal.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Luk 17:19,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20677 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence reveals faith as the primary and consistently named inner posture. In Mat 9:22, Mar 5:34, Mar 10:52, Luk 8:48, Luk 17:19, and Luk 18:42, Jesus's formula "your faith has made you well" names faith as the operative inner posture without which the saving event would not occur. The character of that faith is further specified by the individual accounts: the woman of Mat 9:21 and Mar 5:28 exhibits quiet interior conviction, formed before any word is spoken; Bartimaeus (Mar 10:52, Luk 18:42) exhibits persistent, publicly expressed, socially costly faith — he cries out despite rebuke. Jairus (Mar 5:23) exhibits urgent, desperate supplication. The Samaritan leper (Luk 17:19) adds gratitude and return-worship as components of the faith-posture that distinguishes wholeness from mere cure. In Luk 8:50, "only believe" positions faith as the counter-posture to fear — the required inner orientation is trust deliberately held against dread. Jam 5:15 names the "prayer of faith" — implying confident, believing intercession rather than anxious petition. Across the evidence, the required inner posture is faith characterised by conviction, persistence, and willingness to direct the whole self toward Jesus as the source of wholeness.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:22,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Luk 18:42,Mat 9:21,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:23,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15
- **[20678 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the persons involved treat Jesus as directly accessible, personally responsive, and wholly sufficient for their need — a relational posture that implies recognition of divine authority and benevolence. The woman of Mat 9:21 acts without being told to; her inner faith-speech demonstrates a relationship structured by confident expectation of Jesus's healing power. Bartimaeus's persistence despite social rebuke (Mar 10:52) reveals a relationship in which the person's confidence in Jesus exceeds social pressure and shame. The Samaritan leper's return (Luk 17:19) reveals that the healed person understands the relationship as one requiring acknowledgment and gratitude — not merely transactional but covenantal in its response. Jairus's prostrated supplication (Mar 5:23) reveals a relationship in which the person casts all other resources aside and presents themselves in utter dependence. In 1Pe 3:21, the inner appeal to God for a good conscience reveals a relationship in which the person's most intimate inner state — conscience — is held before God as the site of salvation's work. The consistent picture across M38-C is that the human-to-God movement in this characteristic expresses a relationship of transparent need, personal trust, and recognition of divine sufficiency.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mar 10:52,Luk 17:19,Mar 5:23,1Pe 3:21
- **[20679 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence is not silent on human-to-God operation; it is thoroughly evidenced across all three VCGs as established in obs_id=328, 329, and 330 above. No silence notation is required.
- **[20680 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence shows person-to-person extension most directly in Jam 5:15 (VCG-03): the prayer of faith exercised by elders or community members on behalf of the sick person is the mechanism by which healing and forgiveness come. Here the giving is intercessory — one person's faith-act carries saving effect for another who may not be able to exercise faith independently. In Mar 5:23 (VCG-02), Jairus's desperate faith-supplication is exercised on behalf of his daughter — a parent's faith operating as a giving act toward another's need. The broader synoptic narratives (VCG-01) are primarily individual (each person exercises their own faith), but even there the characteristic exhibits a giving dimension in that Jesus extends the confirming word — "your faith has made you well" — which is a gift of recognition and completion that seals the healing. The evidence does not show laypeople routinely giving this characteristic to one another in the synoptic material; the person-to-person giving is concentrated in intercessory prayer (Jam 5:15) and parental advocacy (Mar 5:23).
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,Mar 5:23
- **[20681 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — In Jam 5:15, the enabling inner condition is named explicitly: the "prayer of faith" — the giver must be operating in believing prayer, not merely ritualistic petition. The implication is that the intercessor must themselves be in right standing (Jam 5:16 context, though not in the M38-C corpus, confirms this pattern). In Mar 5:23, Jairus's inner orientation is urgency combined with earnest faith — he has exhausted other options and presents himself fully before Jesus. The enabling inner condition is total reliance: no divided trust, no alternative recourse. In both cases, the giver's inner posture mirrors the individual faith-posture evidenced in VCG-01: conviction of Jesus's sufficiency, willingness to press the need before him without holding back. The evidence does not describe cynical, detached, or rote extension of this characteristic producing genuine results; the inner condition of the giver appears to be integral to the effectiveness of the giving.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,Jam 5:16,Mar 5:23
- **[20682 · gap]** **[CHAR-3]** G — The M38-C evidence block does not address this question directly. No verse in VCG-01, VCG-02, or VCG-03 explicitly states a prior reception or formation required before a person can extend healing-wholeness-through-faith to another. Jam 5:15 implies the intercessor must be a righteous person (faith is the operative condition), but the text does not specify what that person must previously have received or undergone. This is a genuine analytical gap in the M38-C evidence.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15
- **[20683 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence is not fully silent: Jam 5:15 and Mar 5:23 provide evidence for the giving direction as noted in obs_id=332 and 333. However, the evidence is partial and concentrated in intercessory and parental contexts rather than generalised person-to-person giving.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,Mar 5:23
- **[20684 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence shows reception primarily as Jesus-to-individual, but within the M38-C corpus there is evidence of person-mediated reception in Jam 5:15: the sick person receives healing and forgiveness through the prayer of faith offered by others, not solely through their own individual faith. In the synoptic narratives, the reception is direct and immediate — the moment Jesus pronounces "your faith has made you well," the sōzō is received. Mar 5:34 describes the woman being told to "go in peace" — the reception is sealed by Jesus's word. Mar 10:52 notes that Bartimaeus received his sight "immediately" — the reception is instantaneous upon Jesus's confirming word. In Mar 5:23 (VCG-02), the daughter is the recipient of a healing sought by her father — she receives without herself exercising faith in the narrative as described. Reception of this characteristic, when mediated through another's faith (Jam 5:15, Mar 5:23), operates as an overflow of the other's trust toward the recipient's need.
  - _cites:_ Jam 5:15,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Mar 5:23
- **[20685 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — Where the recipient is themselves the faith-exerciser (Mat 9:21-22, Mar 5:28, 34, Mar 10:52, Luk 8:48, Luk 17:19, Luk 18:42), the enabling condition is the interior conviction and active trust already evidenced in T4.2.2 above. In Luk 8:50 (VCG-02), fear is explicitly identified as the blocking condition: Jesus says "do not fear, only believe" — fear and faith are set as mutually exclusive inner postures, with fear blocking and faith enabling reception. In the case of Jairus's daughter (Mar 5:23, Luk 8:50), the recipient is passive (dying or dead), which suggests that when the recipient cannot exercise faith personally, another's faith can open the door to reception. The evidence does not describe an active inner blocking posture in M38-C beyond the fear identified in Luk 8:50.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mat 9:22,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Luk 8:48,Luk 17:19,Luk 18:42,Luk 8:50,Mar 5:23
- **[20686 · silent]** **[CHAR-3]** S — The M38-C evidence block does not present a case where a person encounters this characteristic and explicitly fails to receive it. The ten lepers in Luk 17:11-19 receive physical healing, but only the Samaritan returns — the nine do not encounter the characteristic and refuse it, they simply do not return for the deeper sōzō (Luk 17:19). This is suggestive but does not provide a direct description of the inner-being state of the non-receiver. No verse in VCG-01, VCG-02, or VCG-03 explicitly describes the inner state of non-reception within this characteristic's corpus.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:11,Luk 17:12,Luk 17:13,Luk 17:14,Luk 17:15,Luk 17:16,Luk 17:17,Luk 17:18,Luk 17:19
- **[20687 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence is not silent on reception; it is well-evidenced across VCG-01 and VCG-02 and partially in VCG-03 as noted in obs_id=336 and 337. No silence notation is required.
- **[20688 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence includes at least one explicit example of this characteristic operating across covenantal and ethnic distance. In Luk 17:19, the returning leper is a Samaritan — outside the Jewish covenant — and Jesus singles him out as the one whose faith brought the full sōzō, not merely physical cure. The nine (presumably Jewish) lepers received the physical cure but did not receive the explicit sōzō-with-faith-ascription. This suggests the characteristic operates regardless of, and perhaps conspicuously across, relational and covenantal distance. Within existing bonds: Jairus (Mar 5:23) is a synagogue ruler, within the covenant community, and his faith operates effectively. The woman of Mat 9:21-22 and Mar 5:28, 34 is within the covenant community. Bartimaeus (Mar 10:52, Luk 18:42) is a Jewish beggar, also within the community. The evidence does not show the characteristic operating less effectively across relational difference; if anything, the Samaritan leper example (Luk 17:19) suggests a heightened commendation of faith exercised from outside the expected relational bond.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:19,Mar 5:23,Mat 9:21,Mat 9:22,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42
- **[20689 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The Luk 17:19 evidence is definitive: the Samaritan leper is outside the Jewish covenant and receives the fullest expression of this characteristic — Jesus explicitly attributes sōzō to his faith. This establishes that the characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries. The woman with the haemorrhage (Mar 5:28-34) and Bartimaeus (Mar 10:52) are within the covenant, confirming that the characteristic also operates within covenantal contexts. Jam 5:15 is addressed to Christian communities (a new covenant context), confirming covenantal operation. 1Pe 3:21 is explicitly baptismal — a covenantal entry act. The evidence thus shows this characteristic operating both within and across covenantal boundaries, with faith as the operative factor rather than covenantal standing.
  - _cites:_ Luk 17:19,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:29,Mar 5:30,Mar 5:31,Mar 5:32,Mar 5:33,Mar 5:34,Mar 10:52,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20690 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence suggests inclusion is determined by faith exercised rather than by identity, status, or relational proximity. The included in the M38-C corpus are: a socially marginalised woman (haemorrhage, ritual uncleanness — Mat 9:21-22, Mar 5:28-34), a synagogue ruler (Jairus — Mar 5:23), a blind roadside beggar (Bartimaeus — Mar 10:52, Luk 18:42), a Samaritan leper (Luk 17:19), a dying child (Luk 8:50 by extension), sick members of a faith community (Jam 5:15), and those undergoing baptism (1Pe 3:21). The range is deliberately diverse: gender, class, ethnicity, health status, and social standing vary across the corpus. No exclusion is stated or implied in this characteristic's evidence on grounds of identity. The operative factor for inclusion is consistently faith as the inner-being activating condition.
  - _cites:_ Mat 9:21,Mat 9:22,Mar 5:28,Mar 5:29,Mar 5:30,Mar 5:31,Mar 5:32,Mar 5:33,Mar 5:34,Mar 5:23,Mar 10:52,Luk 18:42,Luk 17:19,Luk 8:50,Jam 5:15,1Pe 3:21
- **[20691 · finding]** **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence is not silent on relational boundaries; it addresses them through the Samaritan leper example and the diversity of recipients as noted in obs_id=340, 341, and 342. No silence notation is required.
