M38-A's variation tracks with the existential register of the encounter (quiet fulfillment, urgent alarm, joyful recognition, astonishment) — different emotional textures of the same fundamental turn toward Christ. M38-B's variation tracks with whether rescue is being received, denied, or mocked — the same sōzō-language appears in entirely different phenomenological registers (terror-cry, mockery-taunt) that produce entirely different immediate responses. M38-C's variation is structured: peace where healing is the central gift (haemorrhage narrative), following-movement where sight is (Bartimaeus), conscience-cleared where moral restoration is primary. The immediate response in M38-C correlates with the specific dimension of wholeness received. M38-D's variation is the most instructive in the cluster: "forgiven" (spare, positional) contrasts with shame-and-silenced-contrition (Eze 16:63), vocation-readiness (Isa 6:7), and contrition-before-declaration (Luk 18:13) — showing that the same atonement-received event produces markedly different immediate inner-being expressions depending on the person's moral-spiritual context. M38-F's variation tracks with the relationship between the soul and the salvation being encountered (exultation when personally received, mourning when absent, sobriety when promised). M38-G's variation maps onto distinct temporal-contextual registers (joy at arrival, motivational gratitude in retrospect, intercessory appeal in urgency, deepened trust in experience). The cluster finding is that immediate-response variation is systematic and meaningful rather than arbitrary: the variation tracks the position of the encountering soul relative to the salvific event — receiving, remembering, awaiting, or witnessing its denial — and each position generates a distinct but coherent immediate response.
- **[21920]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Comparing sustained effects across the seven characteristics reveals that M38 salvation as a cluster produces four consistent long-term inner-being outcomes regardless of which specific characteristic is in view. First, identity-stabilization: M38-A produces settled eschatological security, M38-B produces faith-heir identity, M38-C produces conscience-integrity as new constitution, M38-D produces cleared relational access to God, M38-F produces identity as heir of salvation, M38-G produces stable ransomed selfhood. The formation of a stable identity grounded in God's salvific act is the most universal sustained effect across M38. Second, moral-volitional reorientation: all seven characteristics establish some form of changed will-direction — toward perseverance (A), toward deepened dependence (B), toward continued following (C), toward evil-avoidance (D), toward comprehensive mediatorial attentiveness (E), toward righteous conduct (F), toward ethical generosity (G). Salvation as a cluster reshapes the will in every mode. Third, perseverance capacity: A, B, C, F, and G all explicitly evidence the capacity to continue under pressure as a sustained effect — the five crisis or temporal characteristics all develop endurance. M38-D and M38-E are the characteristics in which perseverance is less foregrounded, suggesting that the condition-based and mediatorial characteristics emphasize settled standing over forward endurance. Fourth, intercessory capacity: M38-D (vocation and service from cleared conscience), M38-E (crisis-readiness for self-placement between wrath and community), M38-G (memorial as ground for intercessory appeal), and M38-F (communal sustaining capacity) all establish some form of outward-oriented service or prayer as a sustained outcome. Salvation in M38 is not merely personal benefit but generates mediatorial and intercessory orientation as a characteristic sustained effect.
- **[21921]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The blocking conditions across the seven characteristics converge more tightly than the enabling conditions and reveal the cluster's three primary internal threats. The first blocking condition appearing across the widest range of characteristics is self-reliance or self-sufficiency: M38-A (wealth, works, truth-refusal), M38-B (faithless demand, presumption), M38-C (fear as self-protective contraction, taking benefit without relational acknowledgment), M38-D (pride, self-justification), M38-F (self-reliant striving), M38-G (deceptive self-direction refusing ransom). Six of seven characteristics identify self-directed inner orientation as a primary block. The second blocking condition is despair-as-closure: M38-A (passive neglect), M38-B (despairing closure), M38-F (despair-collapse under deferred hope). Three characteristics specifically name the extinction of hope as a blocking condition — distinct from self-reliance but equally effective as a prevention. The third is persistent, unacknowledged sin or defiance: M38-D (entrenched sin closing the atonement pathway), M38-G (deceptive rebellion against offered ransom), M38-A (willful truth-refusal). M38-E's blocking conditions are structurally distinct: presumptuous uncovered mediation, wrong identity-markers, deliberate high-handed sin outside the system's scope. These are not creaturely failure-to-receive conditions but mediatorial failure-to-enact conditions. M38-C's unique blocking condition in the cluster is gratitude-deferred reception — taking physical benefit without relational acknowledgment. This is the only blocking condition in the cluster that operates after initial reception rather than before it, suggesting that the full characteristic can be obstructed even after partial engagement. No other characteristic evidences this mid-process blockage. The cluster reveals that M38 salvation is blocked at three points: before engagement (self-sufficiency, defiance), during engagement (despair, distorted posture), and after initial reception (M38-C's ungrateful self-continuation).
- **[21922]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The supporting evidence for each characteristic's primary dimension classification reveals which faculty the verse evidence most consistently names, commands, or presupposes. The cluster finding is that direct naming of inner faculties in the verse evidence is unevenly distributed: M38-A and M38-C have the most explicit faculty-naming (faith, heart-believing, confessing, interior conviction, "your faith has made you well"), with the volitional-faith classification supported by repeated direct attribution. M38-B's affective-volitional classification is supported by the most explicit affective vocabulary in the cluster — "was afraid," "my soul is troubled," "loud cries and tears" — which appears across multiple distinct verse groups rather than only in edge cases. M38-D's conscience classification is supported by the most consistent outcome vocabulary: "forgiven," "guilt removed," "sin covered" — all of which are conscience-register terms appearing in eleven consecutive Levitical verses, making this the most lexically dense single-faculty evidence in the cluster. M38-E's volitional-executive classification is supported by the structural character of all five VCGs: every group organizes around what the priest does rather than what the priest feels or believes. The enacted sequence is the evidence. M38-F's volitional-affective evidence is the most dispersed across its VCGs — both volitional (waiting, directing, pleading) and affective (mourning, exulting, groaning) evidence appears in every VCG, making the dual classification well-distributed. M38-G's volitional-memorial classification is supported by the most explicitly command-structured evidence in the cluster: Deu 15:15, Deu 24:18, Mic 6:4 all issue direct imperatives grounded in remembered ransom, making the memorial-generating-will structure the explicit logical form of the key texts. The cluster-wide evidence supports volitional primacy across six of seven characteristics while M38-D's conscience classification stands as the one case where the evidence primarily describes something undergone rather than something enacted.
- **[21923]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Every one of the seven findings affirms all three implications — directional, relational, and constitutional — as present in the respective name. This universal affirmation is itself a cluster-level finding: M38 salvation is, across all its characteristics, inherently directional (involving movement), inherently relational (requiring at least two parties), and inherently constitutional (touching or restructuring the fundamental nature of the person). However, the specific content of each implication differs by characteristic in revealing ways. Directional implications partition along the temporal axis: M38-B, M38-C, and M38-D all name forward-movement toward a destination (rescue, wholeness, cleared conscience); M38-G names backward orientation toward a constituting past event; M38-F names forward orientation toward a not-yet future; M38-A names downward reception from divine initiative; M38-E names simultaneous bidirectionality (toward God and toward community). Relational implications partition along the participant axis: M38-A, M38-B, M38-C, M38-F name a two-party relationship (creature-God, endangered-rescuer, sick-healer, waiter-Savior); M38-E names a three-party structure (mediator between God and community); M38-D names a guilt-bearing fault-line within the two-party God-creature relationship; M38-G names the ransomed-ransomer dyad as constitutive of identity. M38-E's three-party structure is unique in the cluster and orients its enquiry differently from the beginning: analysis must track the mediator's inner being in its orientation toward two parties simultaneously rather than one. Constitutional implications show the sharpest divergence: M38-A's constitutional implication is eschatological re-constitution (person transferred into new destiny); M38-G's is identity-constitution (person permanently defined by ransom received); M38-C's is integrated wholeness (the person's constitution as whole rather than fragmented); M38-E's is vocational constitution (the mediator is who they are by virtue of their assigned representative function). The constitutional implication is most profound in M38-G and most vocational in M38-E — two distinct senses of "what a person fundamentally is."
- **[21924]** **[CLUSTER]** E — When the seven working descriptions are placed in comparison, the cluster reveals both the shared architecture and the irreducible distinctiveness of each characteristic. All seven descriptions locate the characteristic at the intersection of multiple faculties rather than within a single faculty — this is a universal finding. But the specific intersection differs. M38-A: will-conscience-mind-affection in received condition. M38-B: affective-volitional in crisis-activated transient condition. M38-C: will-cognitive-conscience in composite act. M38-D: moral-relational conscience as passively received condition. M38-E: volitional-executive as enacted vocation. M38-F: volitional-affective as compound orientation. M38-G: volitional-memorial as sustained condition. Three characteristics describe a passively received condition as their constitutional home (A, D, G); three describe an active act or enacted vocation as their constitutional form (B, C, E); one describes a compound orientation sustaining itself over time (F). The impact-on-person vocabulary across the seven working descriptions produces a composite picture of M38 salvation's total anthropological effect: the person who participates in M38 as a cluster is transferred from condemnation to justification (A), rescued from mortal extremity (B), integrated in body and spirit (C), cleared in conscience (D), covered by mediation they did not personally enact (E), oriented toward future salvation that sustains them now (F), and constituted in identity by a ransom they did not earn (G). The seven impacts are additive rather than redundant: no two descriptions describe the same anthropological effect. The working descriptions together constitute a comprehensive inner-being theology of salvation that moves from condition-of-person (D) through act-of-person (A, B, C) to vocation-of-other (E) to orientation-of-person (F) to identity-of-person (G) — covering the full scope of what it means for a human inner being to inhabit M38 salvation.
- **[21925]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The boundary definitions across the seven characteristics are designed to separate genuinely adjacent phenomena, and the comparison reveals which adjacencies are most contested and where the cluster's internal borders are most carefully managed. The most heavily demarcated boundary in the cluster is between M38-A (eschatological salvation) and M38-B (physical rescue), M38-C (healing wholeness), and M38-F (anticipated salvation): the eschatological characteristic must repeatedly distinguish itself from temporal, physical, and prospective forms of the same sōzō vocabulary. This boundary work is the most frequent across the cluster. The second most contested boundary is between M38-D (conscience cleansed) and M38-C (healing wholeness) on one side — both address inner-being restoration through an atonement/faith mechanism — and M38-E (priestly machinery) on the other, since ka.phar appears in both D and E but in different directional registers. The programme's explicit boundary resolution (ka.phar Register A vs. Register B) is the clearest formal boundary marker in the cluster. Boundaries that are least contested: M38-E ends where the priest steps out of the mediatorial role; M38-G ends where memory is merely cognitive rather than identity-constituting. These are relatively stable. The most significant cluster-level boundary finding is that M38-A, M38-B, and M38-C all share the sōzō root and yet are explicitly not each other. The programme has drawn three distinct intra-root boundaries within a single Greek verb — a methodological commitment to sub-lexical precision that the comparison makes visible. The boundary between M38-F and M38-A is also structurally important: M38-F is not salvation already received but salvation oriented toward. The temporal boundary — between hope and possession — is the sharpest conceptual divide in the cluster and is maintained at every relevant point across the seven findings.
- **[21926]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Six of seven characteristics evidence a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — only M38-E (priestly mediation machinery) is primarily enacted rather than voiced, though even M38-E contains a speech-adjacent mode in Moses's spoken declaration of mediatorial intent (Exo 32:30). The universal presence of speech across the cluster is a finding: M38 salvation consistently generates, requires, or is constituted partly by verbal expression. However, the function of speech differs significantly across characteristics. In M38-A, speech is constitutive: "confessing with the mouth" (Rom 10:9-10) and "calling on the name" (Act 2:21) are not expressions of a pre-existing salvation but acts that partly constitute its reception — the confession completes and publicly enacts the inner volitional turning. In M38-B, speech is the mechanism: the crisis-cry is the act of the characteristic — voiced petition is not accompanied by some other primary act but is itself the characteristic's operation in speech-form. In M38-C, speech operates on two levels: inner speech (formed interior conviction) and outer cry (Bartimaeus) — making M38-C the only characteristic in the cluster with explicit inner-speech as a distinct operative mode. In M38-D, speech is priestly-declarative: the priest's pronouncement of forgiveness constitutes the forgiven state rather than describing it — a performative speech act. In M38-F, speech is address-to-God and communal-petition: it both expresses and sustains the hope-posture simultaneously. In M38-G, speech takes three forms: intercessory prayer deploying redemptive memory, praise and song as overflow, and proclamation of salvation to others. A significant convergence: M38-A, M38-B, M38-D, and M38-G all include speech directed toward God as petition, confession, or memorial-invocation. This cluster-wide pattern of speech-toward-God as a constitutive mode confirms that M38 salvation is irreducibly relational and requires vocal-relational expression — it does not operate in a purely interior silent register.
- **[21927]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Every characteristic evidences at least some zones of silence on immediate response, and the pattern of silence is itself informative at the cluster level. The most consistently silent zone across all relevant characteristics is the procedural-institutional and the cosmically-framed: M38-A's gift-language VCG (VCG-03) and scope-of-salvation VCG (VCG-08) are silent on immediate response; M38-B's mockery VCG (VCG-02) records no immediate inner response of the mocked or the mockers; M38-C is silent on Jairus's inner response post-healing and on the healed person's response in the intercessory mode (Jam 5:15); M38-D's entire Levitical atonement-outcome cluster (11 verses) records only positional outcomes without any phenomenological description; M38-E is silent on the high priest's felt experience during the Day of Atonement's solitary work; M38-F is silent on VCG-02 and VCG-04 (cosmically-framed salvation texts); M38-G is silent on the eschatological ransom texts (VCG-05) and the substitutionary mechanics texts (VCG-08). The pattern reveals that verse evidence tends to be silent on immediate response precisely where the focus is on: (1) the scope or mechanism of salvation rather than the person encountering it, (2) the mocking or adversarial perspective rather than the receiving perspective, (3) the intercessory or corporate mode rather than the personal experiential mode, and (4) the eschatological or cosmic register rather than the present-existential register. The cluster's silences are therefore not random lacunae but systematic gaps at the junctions between the objective-structural and the subjective-experiential. M38-E's silence on the high priest's inner experience during the solitary Day of Atonement work is the most striking single silence in the cluster: the moment of maximum mediatorial weight in all of Israel's cultic life is phenomenologically unnarrated. The evidence describes what happens structurally but not what it costs or feels.
- **[21928]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Every finding across the seven characteristics affirms that the sustained effect differs from the immediate response, and the cluster-wide comparison reveals a consistent structural pattern in how that difference operates. The immediate responses, across all seven characteristics, are characterized by intensity, movement, and transition: the crisis-cry, the peace-settling, the weight-taking, the will-turning, the joy-erupting, the fear-displacing, the contrition-striking. They are all kinetic — the inner being is in passage from one state to another. The sustained effects, by contrast, are uniformly characterized by stability, habituation, and dispositional depth: settled assurance (A), faith-heir identity (B), conscience-integrity (C), cleared relational access (D), habituated solidarity-consciousness (E), cosmic-scale security (F), stable ransomed selfhood (G). The shift from immediate to sustained is therefore a shift from kinesis to stasis, from acute to chronic, from event to character. Three characteristics show a specific additional quality in this transition: M38-A shows deepening (assurance replaces initial faith-urgency, not merely continuing it); M38-C shows direction-change (peace at reception becomes following-orientation sustained); M38-D shows qualitative moral integration (positional forgiveness becomes dispositional evil-avoidance and vocation-readiness). These three show that the sustained effect is not merely a settled version of the immediate response but is ontologically different from it — the person in sustained possession of M38-C is a disciple-follower, not simply a settled version of the faith-exerciser. M38-E's finding explicitly confirms this cluster pattern: the immediate response is weight-taking in the moment of atonement enactment; the sustained effect is the habituated solidarity-consciousness and crisis-readiness formed over years of repeated mediatorial service. The immediate and sustained are the same characteristic in different temporal register: kinetic at entry, dispositional at depth.
- **[21929]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The seven findings collectively construct a composite portrait of the non-receiving inner being within M38. The comparison reveals that non-reception takes two structurally distinct forms across the cluster, and that these forms are not evenly distributed. The first form is active resistance: M38-A names willful truth-refusal and self-protective clinging; M38-D names defiance-and-contempt and persistent entrenched sin; M38-G names deceptive self-direction that refuses offered ransom; M38-B names faithless demand treating the rescuer as instrument. In this form, the inner being encounters salvation and actively turns away — not through ignorance but through some form of self-protective, self-reliant, or deceptive will-orientation. The second form is passive closure: M38-B names despairing resignation (Act 27:20); M38-F names the self-reliant laborer who produces futility; M38-C names ungrateful self-continuation (the nine lepers). In this form, the inner being either collapses inward (despair) or continues outward without acknowledgment — not aggressively resistant but functionally closed. The comparison reveals that M38-A, M38-D, and M38-G concentrate the most explicit active-resistance language, while M38-B, M38-C, and M38-F evidence more passive-closure states. M38-E is the exceptional case: its non-reception analysis focuses not on the community member who fails to receive the characteristic's benefit but on the mediator who fails to enact it properly — giving the characteristic a unique inward-facing non-reception profile that concerns the agent rather than the recipient. A significant cluster observation: M38-C uniquely evidences a partial-non-reception state — the nine lepers receive physical cure but not full sōzō. This is the only characteristic across all seven in which the inner-being of the non-receiver has already received something of the characteristic. Partial reception with relational non-completion is a distinct non-reception mode evidenced only in M38-C.
- **[21930]** **[CLUSTER]** E — All seven characteristics evidence secondary dimensions, and no finding claims that secondary dimensions compete with or displace the primary classification. The cluster-wide finding is therefore that M38 salvation is multi-dimensional in every characteristic, with a consistent primary and enriching secondaries in each case. However, the secondary dimensions themselves show important patterns of recurrence and distribution. The relational dimension appears as a secondary in M38-B, M38-C, and M38-E — in all three cases as the relationship with the divine other being constitutive to but not primary in the characteristic. The cognitive-noetic dimension appears as secondary in M38-A, M38-C, M38-F, and M38-G — in all four cases serving the primary volitional or memorial dimension by supplying the content (promise comprehended, conviction formed, delay interpreted, ransom remembered) that the will then acts upon. The cognitive dimension is consistently instrumental to the volitional primary across the cluster: mind serves will in M38. The affective dimension appears as secondary in M38-A, M38-C, M38-D, M38-G, and M38-F — in five characteristics. In M38-F the affect is so prominent it is named as co-primary (volitional-affective). In M38-G it is named as secondary but rich. In M38-D uniquely, the affective secondary includes shame-and-contrition as a proper sustained affective state rather than merely peace or joy. The conscience dimension appears as secondary in M38-A, M38-C, and M38-F, but as primary only in M38-D. This asymmetry confirms that the conscience is engaged across the cluster — M38 salvation is not conscience-indifferent in any characteristic — but that M38-D is the characteristic specifically constituted by conscience-transformation. The absence of competing secondaries across all seven findings suggests that M38's dimensional structure is genuinely layered rather than fractured: the primary and secondary dimensions work together rather than pulling against each other in every instance.
- **[21931]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Across seven characteristics, explicit spirit-level location is sparse but not absent: three characteristics produce one identified instance each (Char-1: 1 Cor 5:5; Char-6: Luk 1:47; Char-7: Psa 31:5), while four characteristics (Char-3, -4, -5, and partially -2) surface no explicit pneuma/rûaḥ location at all. What surfaces in comparison is a structural asymmetry: the characteristics that do reach spirit-level do so at points of extremity or culmination — extreme discipline (Char-1), the moment of salvation-received triggering deepest personal exultation (Char-6), and total self-surrender grounded in ransom (Char-7) — rather than as the characteristic's ordinary constitutional home. Characteristics operating primarily through ritual mechanism (Char-5), conscience-clearing (Char-4), and faith-activated healing (Char-3) show no spirit-level signature at all, suggesting that procedural and mediating functions of salvation operate below the spirit register's threshold. The cluster as a whole reveals that spirit-level location in M38 is not a universal marker of salvific depth but appears selectively, at points where the self is either preserved at its irreducible minimum (Char-1), surrendered at its deepest center (Char-7), or flooded with joy at salvation's arrival (Char-6). Spirit-level engagement marks intensity and ultimacy, not ubiquity, across this cluster.
- **[21932]** **[CLUSTER]** E — All seven characteristics register at the soul level, making soul-location the single universal constitutional marker across the entire M38 cluster. The evidence, however, reveals two distinct modes of soul-engagement. In Char-1, -3, -6, and -7 the soul is the receptive and experiential center: it receives salvation as its telos (Char-1: 1 Pe 1:9), forms interior faith (Char-3), sustains hope as a lived inner posture (Char-6), and is the primary object and subject of ransom (Char-7). In Char-2, -4, and -5 the soul appears as the endangered or guilt-burdened entity that requires external intervention: the soul in mortal distress crying out (Char-2), the soul targeted for atonement-covering (Char-4: Lev 17:11), and the community-soul carrying accumulated guilt needing annual discharge (Char-5). This bifurcation reveals a structural logic within the cluster: some characteristics address the soul as deficient and burdened, others engage it as the receiving and flourishing center of salvific action. The cluster's consistency at the soul level indicates that salvation in M38 is irreducibly personal and inner-being directed — no characteristic operates exclusively at external, ritual, or cognitive levels without also registering in the soul's own experience. Soul-level location is the constitutional floor of the entire cluster.
- **[21933]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Heart-location is present across five of seven characteristics but follows a gradient of explicitness. Char-1 and Char-3 show the strongest and most explicit heart-location: saving faith is constitutively heart-located (Rom 10:9–10; kardia named directly), and healing-faith operates from a "heart trusting" interior conviction formed before any word is spoken. Char-6 and Char-7 show implicit but functionally substantiated heart-location: hope-sustained-waiting engages volitional, affective, and moral registers that are heart-functions even without the lexeme, and Deuteronomic memory-of-ransom operates through heart-willing and motivation. Char-4 surfaces indirect evidence — heart-directed-toward-God as the qualifying condition for atonement-received (2Ch 30:18) — placing the heart at the threshold rather than the center of the characteristic. Char-2 and Char-5 are explicitly silent: the fear-faith cry in mortal danger and the priestly atonement machinery both operate without any heart-location language across their full verse corpora. The cluster-level finding is that heart-location correlates with the degree to which a characteristic requires personal interior agency — believing, trusting, willing, remembering. Characteristics that are structurally received from outside (Char-5 priestly machinery) or primarily soul-wide in register (Char-2 mortal-danger cry) do not recruit the heart's integrating function.
- **[21934]** **[CLUSTER]** S — Mind-location is the most sparsely attested constitutional register across the cluster. Only Char-1 and Char-6 surface genuine mind-level engagement: Char-1 through wisdom-formation (2 Ti 3:15), cognitive openness (Act 7:25), and knowledge of truth (1 Ti 2:4); Char-6 through prophetic inquiry (1 Pe 1:10), revelatory impact on mortality-fear (2 Ti 1:10), and the helmet-of-salvation's head-protecting function (1Th 5:8). Five of seven characteristics are explicitly silent on mind-location. The cluster-level inference is not that mind is irrelevant to salvation but that the characteristics of M38 are constitutionally anchored well below the discursive-cognitive level — in soul, conscience, and will. Where mind does appear, it functions receptively (receiving wisdom, grasping revelation, maintaining sobriety) rather than generatively. The mind's comparative absence across Char-2, -3, -4, -5, and -7 signals that salvation in this cluster is pre-cognitive in its operative core: it does not require the mind to generate it, only to receive and hold it. Mind-location marks a secondary instrumental register, not a primary constitutional home for any characteristic in the cluster.
- **[21935]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Beyond heart and mind, three soul-subset locations emerge with consistent cross-characteristic evidence: conscience, will, and the holistic nepeš/psychē as undifferentiated personal-life center. Conscience is the most widely shared subset: it appears as the named operative register in Char-3 (1 Pe 3:21, Jam 5:15), Char-4 (recurring keyword "conscience cleansed" across Lev 4–6), Char-1 (Act 16:30), and is functionally present in Char-5's inner-being outcomes. The will appears distinctively in Char-1 (perseverance and surrender of self-protective interest, M38-A-VCG-05/06), Char-6 (moral alignment with anticipated salvation, Isa 56:1), and Char-3 (urgent volitional faith under crisis). The holistic psychē/nepeš as the undifferentiated whole-personal-self appears prominently in Char-2 and Char-7: Char-2's soul-in-mortal-distress and Char-7's soul-as-primary-object-of-ransom both resist reduction to any single sub-faculty and instead engage the person's entire animate life. Char-5 alone surfaces no distinct soul-subset location. The cluster-level finding is that conscience is the most consistently shared sub-level register of salvation across M38 — it is the inner faculty where the transition between guilt-burdened and covered/forgiven is experienced most specifically — while will and nepeš represent the volitional and existential poles of this same inner-being terrain.
- **[21936]** **[CLUSTER]** E — All seven characteristics evidence some form of body-link, though the specificity and function of those links varies significantly. The most anatomically precise links appear in Char-1 (mouth: Rom 10:9–10; head/helmet: Eph 6:17), Char-3 (hand/touch, eyes, skin — multiple specific organs each as the site of healing), Char-4 (lips: Isa 6:7; hand: Lev 1:4; blood: Lev 17:11), and Char-5 (head anointing: Lev 14:18, 29; full body bathing: Lev 16:24). Char-2, -6, and -7 have less anatomically precise links: Char-2 uses tears/voice and the physically imperilled body as expressive/indicative links; Char-6 connects to the head through the helmet metaphor and to the body through somatic groaning; Char-7 identifies the lips as the expressive organ of ransomed praise (Psa 71:23) and crowning joy as a body-referenced image. The cluster-level observation is that body-part linkage is universal but asymmetric: characteristics operating through ritual mechanics (Char-4, -5) and through physical healing (Char-3) have the most specific and multiply-attested body-part engagement, while characteristics operating through inner-being posture (Char-6, -7) have the most diffuse and expressive body links. The body is never constitutionally absent from M38 salvation but always serves a subsidiary function relative to the inner-being locus.
- **[21937]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The directionality of body-soul links is not uniform across the cluster, and the variation is theologically informative. Three distinct patterns emerge. First, soul-to-body (outward expression): Char-1 (heart-faith expresses through mouth-confession), Char-3 (interior faith moves through touch/cry to healing), Char-6 (inner hope protects the head; inner grief expresses as somatic groaning), and Char-7 (ransomed soul generates lip-praise) all run this direction. Second, body-to-soul (inward feedback): Char-2 (mortal bodily danger triggers soul-terror and faith-cry) shows this direction exclusively, and Char-3 evidences a secondary body-to-soul feedback (the physical touch and cry complete the inner conviction's movement). Third, body-mediating-to-soul (externally enacted transformation): Char-4 (hand-laying, coal-to-lips, blood) and Char-5 (head anointing, priestly bathing) both show the body as the surface through which an external priestly/divine act effects inner conscience-change — a mediating direction distinct from either expression or feedback. The cluster reveals that salvation in M38 is constitutively embodied in all three modes simultaneously, but no single direction governs the whole cluster. The mediating direction is unique to the atonement characteristics (Char-4, -5), suggesting that the incarnational structure of atonement requires a distinct body-logic not present in the other characteristics.
- **[21938]** **[CLUSTER]** S — All seven characteristics are silent on constitutional bodily deposit, DNA-level consequence, or generational somatic change. The silence is complete and consistent across the entire cluster. Where generational dimensions appear — household salvation (Char-1: Act 16:31), Noah's household (Char-2: Heb 11:7), corporate atonement (Char-4: Deu 21:8; Dan 9:24), annual reset cycle (Char-5: Lev 16:34), salvation enduring to generations (Char-6: Isa 51:8), and memorial ritual perpetuating ransom-memory (Char-7: Exo 13:15) — these are consistently framed as covenantal standing, communal reception, relational status, or commanded ritual practice rather than somatic deposit. Char-5's annual-reset structure may implicitly contradict accumulation: the recurring requirement for atonement is itself evidence against a deposit that persists constitutionally. The cluster-level finding for T5.7 processing is: M38 salvation characteristics operate at the soul, conscience, and relational levels; they produce inner-being state changes and covenantal standing, but no characteristic in this cluster generates evidence of lasting constitutional imprint in bodily design. This is a consistent, cluster-wide gap rather than an incidental silence in any single characteristic.
- **[21939]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Divine external origination is the dominant and near-universal pattern across the cluster. Six of seven characteristics locate origin primarily or exclusively in God's initiative: Char-1 (Eph 2:8 — "not of yourselves, it is the gift of God"), Char-4 (Psa 65:3; 1 Jo 4:10 — God atones, God sends), Char-5 (Lev 8:34 — divinely commanded), Char-6 (1 Pe 1:5 — guarded by God's power), Char-7 (every pa.dah instance names God as subject of ransoming). The partial exception within this pattern is Char-3, which presents a dual-origin structure: faith is formed within the person prior to external prompting (the woman's interior monologue; Bartimaeus's self-initiated cry), while the actual sōzō is attributed to Christ's power. Char-2 shows the most complex origin picture, with creaturely-situational triggering (external threat generating inner panic-response) alongside divinely-ordained contexts (Heb 5:7; Heb 11:7). The cluster-level finding is that origination in M38 is structurally theocentric across all characteristics — even where human interior activity precedes the observable divine act (Char-3), the saving outcome is attributed entirely to God. The human inner-being contributes the mode of reception (faith, will-surrender, plea) but never the source. This asymmetry between divine origin and human reception mode is a defining structural feature of the cluster as a whole.
- **[21940]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Constitutional movement is evidenced across all seven characteristics, and the comparison reveals two dominant directional patterns with a third distinct pattern. The first and most common pattern is outside-in-then-out: divine external initiative enters the inner being (soul, spirit, conscience), transforms it, and then issues outward through body and ethical action (Char-1, Char-4, Char-5, Char-7). The second pattern is threat-in-to-cry-out: external physical threat registers in the soul as distress, which generates a volitional inner cry directed outward toward God (Char-2). The third pattern is interior-faith-to-body-to-inner-being-confirmation: inner conviction moves through a bodily act to produce a healing that then consolidates as inward peace or restored conscience (Char-3). Char-6 shows a recursive rather than linear pattern: divine promise received as hope → sustained inner posture → expressed in body and affect → renewed by ongoing faith. The cluster-level observation is that constitutional movement in M38 salvation is never unidimensional: every characteristic crosses at least two constitutional levels, and several (Char-1, -3, -7) can be traced across three or four levels in sequence. The movement is consistently oriented toward increasing interiority and depth as its telos — the soul's reception, the conscience's clearing, the spirit's self-surrender — even when the initiating point is external and the concluding expression is bodily.
- **[21941]** **[CLUSTER]** E — No characteristic across the cluster originates primarily at the spirit level. The comparative analysis confirms a consistent pattern: in every case where spirit-level language appears (Char-1: 1 Cor 5:5; Char-6: Luk 1:47; Char-7: Psa 31:5), the spirit is the destination, expression, or depth-point of the characteristic rather than its source. The spirit receives salvation at the point of extremity (Char-1), exults in response to salvation received (Char-6), or is surrendered in trust on the basis of prior ransom (Char-7). Four characteristics (Char-2, -3, -4, -5) show no spirit-level language at all. The cluster as a whole locates origin consistently outside the person (divine initiative) or at the soul-heart-conscience level (interior human reception). Spirit-level is a terminal depth register — the innermost point salvation may reach — not a generative source. This finding has architectural significance: it suggests that the human spirit in M38 is not the producer of salvific characteristics but the deepest layer they can penetrate. The cluster's unanimity on this point (no characteristic originates at spirit-level) distinguishes the spirit as receiver/responder from heart and soul as the primary sites of active engagement with divine salvific initiative.
- **[21942]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Across all seven characteristics, soul-level location consistently reveals that each characteristic addresses the person at the center of their lived, experienced existence rather than at a peripheral, functional, or cognitive surface. The comparative analysis surfaces a layered set of revelations. First, salvation is existentially total: when the soul is the locus (1 Pe 1:9 for Char-1; Lev 17:11 for Char-4; Joh 12:27 for Char-2; Psa 71:23 for Char-7), the whole animate self is implicated — not a faculty or function but the person's living core. Second, the characteristic cannot be externalized without remainder: priestly ritual (Char-5) and conscience-atonement (Char-4) that might appear purely ceremonial are shown by soul-level location to address a deeply personal existential burden (Lev 16:30's accumulated guilt-weight). Third, soul-level location binds hope, rescue, faith, and gratitude together as unified modes of the same personal-center's response: the soul that waits (Char-6), cries in danger (Char-2), believes for wholeness (Char-3), and remembers its ransom (Char-7) is in each case the same animating self engaging salvation from different angles of need. The cluster-level revelation is that M38 salvation is constitutively anti-abstract — it always inhabits the nepeš/psychē as personal, subjective, experienced reality.
- **[21943]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Where heart-location is evidenced across the cluster, it consistently engages the heart's integrating function — but the specific sub-function recruited varies by characteristic in a revealing pattern. Char-1 engages heart-as-trusting: saving faith is a deep trust-commitment rather than cognitive assent, making the heart the organ of determinative relational alignment with God (Rom 10:9–10). Char-3 engages heart-as-source-of-conviction: the settled interior trust formed before any word or action makes the heart the wellspring of faith-activated healing. Char-6 engages all three primary heart functions simultaneously — willing (orientation toward God as source), feeling (grief at absence, exultation at presence), and moral awareness (aligning conduct with anticipated salvation). Char-7 engages heart-as-motivator: memory of ransom lodged in the heart drives the will toward justice and generosity (Deu 15:15). Char-4 engages heart-as-directedness: the heart set toward God is the qualifying condition for receiving atonement even under ritual irregularity (2Ch 30:18). Char-2 and Char-5 are silent. The cluster-level finding is that heart-integration in M38 is not monochromatic: different characteristics recruit different aspects of the heart's synthesizing capacity, and the heart functions as both source (Char-3), receptor (Char-1), director (Char-4), motivator (Char-7), and comprehensive integrator (Char-6). The heart is the constitutional organ of personal coherence in salvation, not merely the organ of emotion.
- **[21944]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Where mind-location is evidenced (Char-1 and Char-6), it reveals the mind's function as receptive and protective rather than generative. Char-1 engages the mind as the faculty that must be opened to receive salvific wisdom (2 Ti 3:15; Act 7:25) — the mind does not produce salvation but provides the cognitive infrastructure through which divinely-communicated salvific truth is received and held. Char-6 adds a second dimension: the mind as the faculty of sustained cognitive vigilance under eschatological hope (1Th 5:8's helmet-protection), and as the arena in which prophets conducted intense searching inquiry about salvation's timing (1 Pe 1:10). The five characteristics silent on mind-location collectively suggest that the mind's absence does not indicate absence of intelligence in salvation but rather that salvation's operative mechanism reaches below discursive reasoning to conscience, soul, and will. The cluster-level revelation from mind-location is thus contrastive as much as positive: what appears at mind-level (wisdom-reception, vigilance-maintenance) is secondary and stabilizing; what happens below mind-level (conscience-clearing, soul-ransom, spirit-surrender) is primary and constitutive. The mind in M38 salvation functions as a guardian and receiver, not a generator.
- **[21945]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Three distinct soul-subset locations surface across the cluster with cross-characteristic significance: conscience, will, and nepeš-as-whole-self. Conscience is the most theologically precise shared subset: it appears as the functionally named interior register of guilt and its removal in Char-3, -4, and implicitly in Char-5, and as the awakened inner faculty in Char-1. What conscience-location reveals across the cluster is that salvation in M38 is not merely positional but phenomenological — the inner being actually experiences the transition from guilt-burdened to forgiven, and this experience is registered at the conscience level as a distinct moral-relational state. The will appears in Char-1 (sustained endurance and self-surrender), Char-3 (urgent volitional faith under crisis), and Char-6 (moral alignment with eschatological anticipation), revealing that salvation is not received passively but requires active volitional engagement — the will must orient, persevere, or release. The holistic nepeš in Char-2 and Char-7 reveals the opposite: that salvation also addresses the person below the level of any faculty-specific exercise, reaching the animate self in its undifferentiated totality. The cluster-level synthesis is that conscience marks the quality of the transition, will marks the mode of engaged reception, and nepeš marks the breadth of what is addressed — together these soul-subsets describe salvation as simultaneously moral (conscience), volitional (will), and existential (nepeš).
- **[21946]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Across the cluster, Scripture's body-part links serve four distinguishable functions that cluster differently by characteristic. Mediating links predominate in the atonement characteristics (Char-4: hand-laying, blood, coal-to-lips; Char-5: head anointing, priestly bathing): here the body is the enacted surface through which an external divine act effects inner-being transformation, and the body cannot be removed without dissolving the mechanism. Expressive links predominate in Char-2 (tears and voice making anguish audible), Char-6 (somatic groaning giving communal form to unfulfilled longing), and Char-7 (lips voicing ransomed-soul joy): here Scripture uses the body to make interior states visible, credible, and communal. Confirmatory links appear in Char-3 (restored eyes, cleansed skin, healed body making inner sōzō publicly attestable). Protective-functional links appear in Char-1 and Char-6 through the helmet-of-salvation metaphor: here the inner salvific reality actively guards a bodily-organ capacity (head, sobriety). The cluster-level finding is that Scripture's body-part linkage is never decorative — each function either enacts (mediating), publicizes (expressive/confirmatory), or defends (protective) the inner-being salvific reality. The body is never the primary constitutional home of any characteristic but is always a necessary participant in the characteristic's full operation.
- **[21947]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The directional diversity across the cluster produces a cluster-level consequence: M38 salvation cannot be understood through a single constitutive model of inner-outer relation. Three different body-soul relational logics coexist. Where soul-to-body direction dominates (Char-1, -3, -6, -7), the consequence is that salvation's authenticity and completeness are verified by its outward expression — mouth-confession, healing touch, somatic longing, lip-praise — without the body replacing the inner-being as the primary register. These characteristics are interior in origin but incomplete without embodied expression. Where body-to-soul direction appears (Char-2 and Char-3's secondary feedback), the consequence is that physical reality participates as a genuine precipitant of inner-being salvific engagement — the body's danger generates the soul-cry; the body's touch completes the inner conviction's movement. This prevents a spiritualized reduction of salvation that ignores creaturely physical existence. Where body-mediating-to-soul direction governs (Char-4, -5), the consequence is the most significant: salvation's inner-being effect cannot be reached by bypassing the enacted bodily mechanism — the incarnational, sacrificial logic of atonement requires the body as its necessary medium. Together, these directional findings establish that M38 salvation is constitutively integrated: none of its seven characteristics can be fully instantiated in the inner being alone without the body's participatory role.
- **[21948]** **[CLUSTER]** S — No positive evidence across any of the seven characteristics supports a constitutional bodily deposit, DNA-level consequence, or generational somatic change from sustained operation of M38 salvation characteristics. The supporting and contradicting analysis across the cluster consistently reaches the same conclusion: what generational language appears is uniformly covenantal, relational, or ritual rather than somatic. Char-7's Exo 13:15 (intergenerational memorial ritual) and Char-6's Isa 51:8 (salvation enduring to generations) both describe the perpetuation of salvific provision and practice, not biological transmission. Char-5's Lev 16:34 (annual perpetual statute) actively contradicts a deposit model — the requirement for recurring atonement implies that no cumulative constitutional change results from its performance. Char-1's household salvation language (Act 16:31) describes communal reception through shared faith, not genetic inheritance. The absence of contradiction beyond Char-5's implicit counter-evidence means the silence across the cluster is neutral rather than suppressive: M38's authors are not arguing against bodily deposit but are simply not operating within a framework where somatic-constitutional inheritance of salvific states is a live category. This cluster-wide finding consolidates for T5.7: M38 salvation characteristics produce inner-being and relational transformation, not physiological transmission.
- **[21949]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The comparative analysis reveals a consistent cluster-level pattern: divine initiative is the singular ultimate origin across all seven characteristics, while the mode of transmission and the structure of human reception create apparent multiplicity within that singularity. Four characteristics (Char-1, -5, -7, and Char-4's dominant strand) present origin as unambiguously singular and divine — no human contribution generates the characteristic, only receives it. Two characteristics (Char-3 and Char-2) show structural complexity: Char-3 presents a genuine dual-origin structure where human interior faith forms prior to external prompting, yet the saving act is attributed entirely to Christ's power; Char-2 shows creaturely-reactive triggering alongside divinely-ordained contexts. Char-6 presents a three-strand origin (divine bestowal, mediated revelation, and the constitutive human faculty of hope), but all three strands depend on divine provision for their content and sustaining power. The cluster-level finding is that M38 does not present a competitive-origin model (divine versus human) but a nested-origin model: the singular divine origin encompasses and enables the multiple human modes of reception. Where human interior activity appears to precede the divine act (Char-3's interior monologue), it is itself operating within a divinely-structured encounter, not generating salvation independently. Psa 49:7 (Char-7) provides the cluster's most explicit statement of this logic: human capacity cannot originate ransom.
- **[21950]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Cross-characteristic comparison reveals that constitutional movement in M38 follows a dominant three-phase pattern with characteristic-specific variations at each phase. The dominant pattern is: (1) external divine initiative → (2) inner-being reception and transformation (soul, conscience, heart, spirit) → (3) embodied or ethical expression. This pattern is evidenced in Char-1 (divine word proclaimed → soul receives through faith → body confesses and wears salvation), Char-4 (sacrifice enacted externally → divine acceptance → conscience cleared), Char-5 (divine command → priestly ritual → community's inner-being standing restored), Char-6 (divine promise → hope sustained in soul → expressed through body and behavior), and Char-7 (divine ransoming act → soul receives as identity-forming event → expressed through lips and ethical action). Char-2 and Char-3 show inversions within this dominant pattern: Char-2 begins at the body (physical threat) before moving to soul and then toward God; Char-3 begins at the interior (heart-conviction) before moving outward through body to produce healing and then inward again as peace. The cluster-level finding is that constitutional movement in M38 is directionally centripetal toward the inner-being as its telos (every sequence reaches inward to soul or conscience for its transformative core) but culminates centrifugally in expression, action, or embodied form. The two-directional polarity — depth-seeking and expression-generating — is the defining movement signature of the cluster.
- **[21951]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The three characteristics with explicit spirit-level instances (Char-1, -6, -7) collectively reveal a shared depth-logic: spirit-level engagement marks the point where the self is no longer partially engaged but wholly implicated. In Char-1 (1 Cor 5:5), spirit-level reveals that salvation's reach is indestructible even when all surface expressions are stripped away — the spirit is the irreducible remainder of salvific reality. In Char-6 (Luk 1:47), spirit-level reveals that when anticipated salvation arrives, the response penetrates to the animating center of personhood — exultation at spirit-level is not one affect among others but the whole self overflowing. In Char-7 (Psa 31:5), spirit-level reveals that ransomed identity enables total self-surrender — the spirit's commitment to God is made possible precisely by the prior fact of being ransomed. Across these three instances, spirit-level location consistently marks a transition from partial to total engagement: partial soul-engagement (cognitive, volitional, affective) can occur without reaching the spirit, but spirit-level engagement signals a qualitative shift to whole-self participation. The four characteristics silent at spirit-level (Char-2, -3, -4, -5) are not shallower in other respects, but they operate through mechanisms (crisis-reaction, healing-faith, ritual-atonement, priestly-mediation) whose constitutional home is soul and conscience rather than the spirit's totalizing depth. The spirit marks ultimacy, not ordinary operation.
- **[21952]** **[CLUSTER]** E — No silence note is required for any characteristic across the cluster. All seven characteristics evidence soul-level location, making this the one constitutional register where the silence-check yields a universally positive result. The comparative observation from this prompt is itself significant: the universal positivity across all seven characteristics — including Char-5 (priestly mediation, where explicit nephesh vocabulary is absent but inner-being outcomes are inferentially attested) — confirms that soul-level location is the non-negotiable constitutional ground of M38 salvation. Where explicit vocabulary is lacking (Char-5), the functional evidence (accumulated guilt-weight, conscience-unburdening, restored standing) is sufficient to establish soul-level engagement inferentially. This uniformity across characteristics as diverse as physical rescue (Char-2), ritual atonement mechanism (Char-5), and eschatological hope (Char-6) reveals that the soul is not one location among several for M38 salvation but the constitutive medium through which all salvific characteristics operate. No characteristic in the cluster can be instantiated without soul-level engagement, whether that engagement is explicit or inferred.
- **[21953]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Two characteristics are explicitly silent on heart-location: Char-2 (physical rescue from mortal danger) and Char-5 (priestly mediation machinery of atonement). The silence in both cases is structurally coherent rather than incidental. Char-2's fear-faith dynamic in mortal danger operates at the level of the whole soul (nepeš/psychē in distress) and the volitional cry — the heart's integrating function is not named because the characteristic bypasses deliberate heart-integration under conditions of acute threat; the response is too immediate and total for faculty-specific location. Char-5's silence reflects the characteristic's institutional-external structure: the priestly atonement machinery is administered through prescribed ritual procedure rather than personal interior engagement, making heart-location both functionally absent and structurally inappropriate. The three characteristics with indirect or inferential heart-location (Char-4, -6, -7) versus two with explicit heart-location (Char-1, -3) reveal that heart-engagement in M38 salvation correlates with the degree of required personal interior agency — believing, trusting, willing — and is absent or peripheral where the characteristic operates through external mechanism or crisis-bypassing of deliberate inner function.
- **[21954]** **[CLUSTER]** S — Five of seven characteristics are explicitly silent on mind-location: Char-2, -3, -4, -5, and -7. The breadth of this silence is the cluster's most distinctive finding at the mind level. The silences are not random: they follow a pattern. Characteristics operating through immediate crisis-response (Char-2), pre-verbal conviction (Char-3), ritual-enacted conscience-clearing (Char-4), procedural priestly mechanism (Char-5), and formative-memory identity (Char-7, which does surface mind-function in other prompts but not here) all operate below the level of discursive mental engagement. The two characteristics that do engage the mind (Char-1, -6) do so in a secondary and stabilizing capacity — the mind receives and holds salvific truth (Char-1), maintains sobriety and vigilance under hope (Char-6) — rather than generating the characteristic. The cluster-level implication is that M38 salvation is constitutively sub-rational in its operative core: it takes root, transfers guilt, heals, ransoms, and sustains hope through soul, conscience, will, and body without requiring the mind's prior or primary engagement. This does not devalue intellect but repositions it — the mind is the faculty that articulates and holds what the soul has already received.
- **[21955]** **[CLUSTER]** E — One characteristic is explicitly silent on additional soul-subset location beyond the general inner-being register: Char-5 (priestly mediation machinery of atonement). The silence in Char-5 is structurally consistent with its character as an institutionally-administered, externally-enacted mechanism: the characteristic's constitutional footprint is defined by the outcome it produces in others (the atoned-for community's guilt-clearing, standing-restoration) rather than by specific sub-faculty engagement within a single person's inner life. Six of seven characteristics surface at least one additional soul-subset beyond heart and mind, with conscience appearing as the most cross-characteristic sub-location (Char-1, -3, -4), the will appearing in Char-1, -3, and -6, and the holistic nepeš in Char-2 and -7. The cluster-level observation is that Char-5's silence at this level is not a deficiency but a structural feature — it is the only characteristic whose constitutive action takes place primarily in the priest and the ritual apparatus rather than in the inner life of a believing individual. The other six characteristics are constitutively inner-being phenomena distributing across identifiable soul subsets; Char-5 is constitutively an externally-mediated phenomenon whose inner-being effects are received rather than exercised.
- **[21956]** **[CLUSTER]** E — No silence note is required across the cluster: all seven characteristics evidence some form of body-part or body-level linkage. The cluster is entirely positive at this level, making body-engagement another near-universal (alongside soul-level location) among M38 salvation characteristics. The comparison across the silence-check confirms what was established in T2.6.1–2: the degree of anatomical specificity varies from highly precise (Char-3: hand, eyes, skin; Char-4: lips, hand; Char-5: head, full body) to diffuse and metaphorical (Char-6: helmet, groaning; Char-7: lips, crowning joy; Char-2: tears, voice, imperilled body), but no characteristic is wholly disembodied. The theological implication of this universal positivity is that M38 salvation in all its modes — rescue, healing, atonement, priestly mediation, hope, ransom, eschatological faith — is constitutively incarnational: it engages the human being as a bodily creature, not merely as an inner spiritual entity. The body's universal presence across the cluster resists any dualistic reading of M38 salvation as purely inward or spiritual.
- **[21957]** **[CLUSTER]** E — No silence note is required: all seven characteristics evidence body-characteristic links with identifiable directionality. The positive result across the entire cluster confirms that the body's participatory role in M38 salvation is not optional or supplementary but constitutive. The cross-cluster comparison from this silence-check reinforces the directional typology established in T2.7.1–2: soul-to-body expression (Char-1, -3 primary, -6, -7), body-to-soul feedback (Char-2, Char-3 secondary), and body-mediating-to-soul (Char-4, -5) are all present across the cluster with no characteristic entirely absent from body-involvement. The structural implication is that M38 salvation cannot be reduced to a purely inward phenomenon in any of its seven characteristic forms — each requires the body's participation either as the medium of expression, the precipitant of engagement, or the enacted surface of transformation. This directional universality means that embodiment is not a secondary concern added to an already-complete inner-being account of salvation but is part of the constitutive architecture of every characteristic.
- **[21958]** **[CLUSTER]** S — All seven characteristics are explicitly silent on constitutional bodily deposit, and this finding is recorded for T5.7. The cluster-wide silence is complete and structurally coherent. No verse across any characteristic in M38 describes salvation's operation producing a lasting change in bodily constitution, a heritable alteration in physical design, or a DNA-level consequence. The cluster distinguishes clearly between two things that might be confused with somatic deposit: (1) generational perpetuation through covenant, ritual, and commanded remembrance (Char-7: Exo 13:15; Char-4: Deu 21:8; Char-6: Isa 51:8) — this is relational and institutional transmission, not somatic; and (2) the body's real-time participatory role in salvation's operation (established across T2.6–7) — this is functional engagement, not constitutional accumulation. Char-5's annual-reset structure is the cluster's one implicit counter-evidence point: the recurring requirement for atonement structurally excludes a deposit model. For T5.7: M38 presents a salvation that is constitutively inner-being (soul, conscience, spirit) and relationally covenantal in its lasting effects, with the body participating functionally but not accumulating constitutionally. This is a cluster-level finding of absence, noted explicitly and consistently across all seven characteristics.
- **[21959]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The comparative analysis reveals a two-tier finding: the ontological source (God as exclusive originating agent of salvation) does not change across any context in any characteristic, but the mode of that divine origination varies significantly and consistently across contexts. Char-1 shows variation across covenant-fulfillment, proclamation, pure gift, and perseverance contexts — all with God as source, the mode adapting to the relational and historical frame. Char-4 shows variation across Levitical priestly, royal intercessory, purely divine initiative, and NT once-for-all sacrifice contexts — the human mediating element changes while the divine source remains. Char-5 shows stability of divine origin with variation only in the human priestly actor (Levitical high priest, Moses, Phinehas, temple priests). Char-7 shows the widest contextual range — historical-corporate, individual-experiential, eschatological-ultimate, substitutionary-ritual — with God as ransoming agent in every context without exception. Char-3 shows the most internally complex variation: communally-mediated, crisis-commanded, and individually-initiated faith contexts all produce healing through Christ's power. Char-2 and Char-6 show contextual variation in the precipitating situation (crisis, suffering, eschatological anticipation) while maintaining divine rescue or divine provision as the constant. The cluster-level synthesis is that contextual variation in M38 is always modal (how God acts, through what human instrument, in what relational frame) rather than ontological (whether God is the source). The singular divine origin is the one invariant across the entire cluster.
- **[21960]** **[CLUSTER]** E — No silence note is required across the cluster: all seven characteristics evidence constitutional movement across at least two levels. The silence-check here yields a universally positive result, confirming that constitutional movement is a defining structural feature of M38 salvation as a whole rather than a property of individual characteristics. The cross-characteristic comparison from this final silence-check crystallizes the cluster's movement architecture. Every characteristic in M38 involves traversal: nothing stays at one constitutional level. Divine initiative crosses the boundary into inner-being reception (universal). Inner-being transformation crosses outward into body or action (universal across at least some instances). And in the most penetrating instances (Char-1, -6, -7), the movement descends all the way to spirit-level before returning outward as expression or surrender. The cluster as a whole presents salvation not as a static possession located at one constitutional level but as a dynamic movement — initiated externally, penetrating inwardly, and issuing outwardly — that by design engages the full constitutional range of the human person. The absence of any static, single-level characteristic across all seven is itself a cluster-level theological statement: salvation in M38 is constitutively a whole-person movement, not a partial-person event.
- **[21961]** **[CLUSTER]** S — Across the seven characteristics comprising Cluster M38, spirit-level (pneuma/rûaḥ) location is the exception rather than the rule, and the aggregate pattern is closer to silence than presence. Four of seven characteristics return explicit silence verdicts (CHAR-2, CHAR-3, CHAR-4, CHAR-5), meaning no verse in those sub-corpora names the human spirit as the site, locus, or register of the salvific dynamic under examination. This is not a minor gap — those four characteristics collectively span well over 100 verses across Leviticus, Psalms, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Daniel, Luke, Romans, Hebrews, Galatians, and 1 John, and the silence is consistent and non-incidental.

Three characteristics (CHAR-1, CHAR-6, CHAR-7) return partial findings, not strong patterns. CHAR-1 yields a single datum (1 Cor 5:5) in which the spirit is named as the eschatological object of salvation — one verse in 120. CHAR-6 yields one datum (Luk 1:47) in which Mary's spirit exults in God her Savior — one verse in 37. CHAR-7 is the most constitutionally distributed finding, with Psa 31:5 providing spirit-level location alongside soul, body, and action evidence — yet even here, spirit is one strand in a multi-level weave, not a dominant register.

The honest cluster-scope verdict is this: spirit-level location is not a constitutional home for Cluster M38's salvation dynamics. Where it appears, it appears at the edges — in extreme ecclesial discipline (CHAR-1), in a singular Magnificat moment (CHAR-6), and in a Davidic entrusting prayer (CHAR-7). The four silent characteristics, which include atonement reception, priestly mediation, healing wholeness, and physical rescue, locate their dynamics at the soul, conscience, and body levels without recourse to the spirit stratum at all.

No integration bias should be applied here: the cluster does not constitute evidence that salvation operates primarily at the spirit level. The weight of evidence across M38 places spirit-level location as sparse, peripheral, and unrepeated — present enough to note, absent enough that primary constitutional weight must be assigned elsewhere.
- **[21962]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Across the seven characteristics, inner hearing and inner sight are the dominant perceptive modes, but their relationship to the characteristic differs structurally. Hearing is the entry channel for characteristics whose saving content arrives as proclaimed word (CHAR-1: "word of salvation" received through hearing; CHAR-7: the herald's announcement). Inner sight operates differently depending on whether the characteristic is anticipatory or retrospective: CHAR-6 projects sight forward toward an invisible eschatological horizon; CHAR-7 recovers sight as the experiential marker of rescue from the pit. Spiritual discernment — the capacity to perceive what physical senses cannot access — appears across CHAR-1, CHAR-2 (Noah's reception of unreceived warning), CHAR-3 (faith-seekers perceiving Jesus's healing identity despite physical impairment), and CHAR-6. The cluster's most revealing contrast is between CHAR-2 and CHAR-3: in CHAR-2, physical perception of danger hyper-activates and narrows the inner being to terror; in CHAR-3, physical perception is precisely what is bypassed or irrelevant, with spiritual apprehension operative in its place. CHAR-4 and CHAR-5 show perception functioning preparatorily — as the trigger for atonement-seeking (perceiving one's guilt, perceiving the holy presence) — rather than as the constitutive mode of the characteristic itself. The cluster pattern thus distinguishes characteristics where perception is the entry-point of salvific content (CHAR-1, CHAR-7) from those where perception is the precipitating input preceding the characteristic's core operation (CHAR-2, CHAR-4, CHAR-5), and from one characteristic where the entire posture is a training of perception toward the unseen (CHAR-6).
- **[21963]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster reveals a consistent pattern: cognition is engaged across all seven characteristics, but never as the originating or causally sufficient faculty. In every case something else — hearing, conscience, affect, crisis, divine warning — activates the characteristic, and cognition participates as a secondary, integrating function. The most significant differentiator is the type of cognitive engagement. CHAR-5 and CHAR-4 engage procedural and diagnostic cognition respectively — knowing the prescribed sequence, knowing that one has sinned — without requiring speculative or exploratory understanding. CHAR-6 and CHAR-1 engage the most demanding cognitive modes: investigative searching (1Pe 1:10), temporal discernment (the appointed day), and the formation of wisdom (2Ti 3:15). CHAR-2 produces the starkest spectrum within a single characteristic: acute mortal crisis bypasses deliberative cognition entirely in panic mode, while the Gethsemane deliberation represents cognition held steady and deepened under maximum anguish. CHAR-3 operates at the minimal threshold — enough cognitive content to generate a conditional proposition ("if I touch…"), but not extended theological reasoning. Across the cluster, the finding that emerges is that salvation-cluster characteristics do not require cognitive sophistication as a precondition for operation: CHAR-4 and CHAR-5 function for the economically humble, the accidentally sinning, and those whose only available cognitive act is recognizing guilt. Cognition serves the characteristic; the characteristic does not arise from cognition.
- **[21964]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster divides sharply on memory: CHAR-7 is constitutively memory-dependent (the characteristic cannot exist without active retrieval of the past ransom event), while CHAR-2 is constitutively memory-independent (the entire sub-group is present-tense and situationally immediate, with memory absent across every VCG). This contrast is the cluster's most revealing polarity. Between these poles, a gradient appears: CHAR-1 uses remembered historical rescue as a warning resource and temporal anchor but does not depend on memory as its generative centre; CHAR-6 draws on generational covenantal memory as the infrastructure that makes hope possible, supplying the content that anticipation projects forward. CHAR-4 introduces the theologically distinctive bidirectional memory pattern: divine memory cleared (God remembers sins no more), divine memory redirected to positive remembrance, while human memory of past sin persists after covering, producing humility and silence. CHAR-5 institutionalises memory through annual-cycle structure — the Day of Atonement is the community's regularised confrontation with accumulated sin-memory. CHAR-3's memory engagement is inferential: prior testimony about Jesus functions as substrate enabling present conviction, but memory is not the operative faculty in the healing moment itself. The cluster pattern reveals that memory is most central to characteristics with a retrospective identity-structure (CHAR-7) and most absent from characteristics defined by acute present-moment crisis (CHAR-2).
- **[21965]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Every characteristic in the cluster engages affect, but the affective register, intensity, and phase differ markedly. CHAR-2 is the most affect-saturated: terror, anguish, weeping, despair, and reverent awe are not accompaniments to the characteristic but constitutive of it — affect is the phenomenological register within which the entire dynamic occurs. CHAR-6 spans the widest affective range (anguish, exultation, eager longing, confident rest, communal distress) and is notable for being affectively permissive — neither suppressing negative affect nor resolving it prematurely. CHAR-1 shows a three-phase affective arc: pre-salvific grief and urgency, reception peace and awe, ongoing gratitude and eschatological joy. CHAR-4 produces a specifically chastened register: not euphoria but relief-under-shame — the burden lifted while contrition remains. CHAR-5 concentrates affective engagement in the mediator (zeal, solidarity-burden, urgency) while the prescribed procedural structure itself is largely affect-neutral. CHAR-3's primary affective contribution is transformative: fear displaced by peace, urgency channelled into healing. CHAR-7's affective culmination is the most comprehensive: sorrow and sighing flee entirely, replaced by everlasting joy (Isa 35:10). The cross-cluster pattern reveals that affect is most fully engaged not in cognitive or procedural characteristics (CHAR-4, CHAR-5 in their institutional form) but in those where crisis, anticipation, or identity-transformation press upon the whole inner person.
- **[21966]** **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster is predominantly silent on creativity, with one significant exception and one partial engagement. CHAR-6 is the sole characteristic with substantial creativity engagement: the soul that must hold an invisible salvation as real generates imaginative figuring — city walls, divine warrior's helmet, blazing torch, cosmic chariot — because the eschatological object exceeds any single image and requires imaginative construction to be held present. CHAR-2 provides the only other evidenced engagement: Noah's ark-building in VCG-04, where faith in a warning about unseen catastrophe generates sustained creative-productive action. Critically, this is the exception within CHAR-2; its crisis modes (panic-cry, despair) completely bypass creativity. CHAR-1, CHAR-3, CHAR-4, CHAR-5, and CHAR-7 are all silent. The cluster pattern is itself a structural finding: six of seven characteristics do not engage the creative faculty because their operative posture is receptive, responsive, or prescribed rather than originating. The one characteristic that generates creative imagination (CHAR-6) does so precisely because it must make present to the inner being a reality not yet visible — imagination is recruited where perception and memory cannot reach. The systematic silence elsewhere reveals that the salvation-cluster characteristics are constitutively receptive in mode: salvation is received, atonement is performed by another, healing is received through faith, ransom is remembered — none of these are creative acts.
- **[21967]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Volition is engaged across all seven characteristics, making it one of the cluster's most universally operative faculties. However, the cluster reveals three distinct volitional modes. The first is volitional surrender: CHAR-1 requires the will's surrender of self-preservation (Mat 16:25 cluster), and CHAR-2 in VCG-03 presents the most complete volitional surrender in the entire cluster — Jesus's deliberate choice not to exercise the rescue-cry, accepting the appointed hour. The second is volitional activation: CHAR-3 is constitutively volitional — the woman presses through the crowd, Bartimaeus persists against opposition, the father pleads with determination. The third is volitional constraint within divine prescription: CHAR-5 is most clearly this mode — the priest must comply with the ordained sequence, and volitional freedom is exercised within those boundaries. CHAR-6 adds the dimension of sustained volitional discipline — choosing to wait, to align conduct morally, to set hope — across time. CHAR-4 reveals the asymmetry most clearly: volition is engaged in the approach to atonement (bringing sacrifice, praying) but is not volitionally productive of the atonement itself, which is received. CHAR-7 shows volition both freed and directed by the characteristic: ransom removes oppressive constraint and reorients the will toward obedience and ethical action. The cluster finding is that salvation-related characteristics consistently engage volition but never as the causally sufficient faculty — the will participates without being the origin of salvation.
- **[21968]** **[CLUSTER]** E — Agency is engaged across the entire cluster, but its distribution and direction reveal the cluster's defining theological structure: divine agency is primary and causally originating in every characteristic; human agency is derivative, responsive, and enabled. This asymmetry is most explicit in CHAR-1 (God saves; humans call, confess, endure), CHAR-4 (God atones causally; humans activate the reception channel), and CHAR-6 (human effort at self-rescue produces nothing; divine saving agency acts with certainty). CHAR-5 presents the most structurally concentrated priestly agency — the priest acts, initiates, and mediates while the community receives — making the agency asymmetry between mediator and recipient a constitutive feature of the characteristic. CHAR-3 is the most distinctive agency finding in the cluster: Jesus's attribution formula ("your faith has made you well") elevates human agency to named secondary causation within a divinely-effected event, an elevation unique in the cluster.
